子不语 - What the Master Would Not Discuss

Translation created with model: together-qwen3-14b-3step-fewshot / step-by-step-3step-fewshot

    Home
    1

    子不语 清 袁枚

    　　●自序“怪、力、乱、神”，子所不语也。然“龙血”、“鬼车”，《系词》语之；“玄鸟”生商，牛羊饲稷，《雅》、《颂》语之。左丘明亲受业于圣人，而内外传语此四者尤详，厥何故欤？盖圣人教人“文、行、忠、信”而已，此外则“未知生，焉知死”，“敬鬼神而远之”，所以立人道之极也。《周易》取象幽渺，诗人自记祥瑞，左氏恢奇多闻，垂为文章，所以穷天地之变也，其理皆并行而不悖。

    Explanation

    1

    Translation:

    Subtitled: "What Confucius Does Not Speak Of" by Yuan Mei, Qing Dynasty

    ● Preface: Confucius does not speak of strange, violent, disorderly, or superstitious matters. Yet, "dragon blood" and "ghost carriage" are discussed in the Xici (Book of Changes); the legend of the "black bird" giving birth to the Shang dynasty and cows and sheep guiding the founder to a field of millet are recorded in the Shijing (Book of Poetry). Zuo Qiuming, who personally studied under a sage, provides particularly detailed accounts of these four topics in his Zuo Zhuan (Commentary of Zuo). Why is this so? Confucius taught only "literature, conduct, loyalty, and trustworthiness." Beyond these, he said, "If one does not know life, how can one know death?" and "Respect the spirits and gods but keep a distance from them," thereby establishing the highest principles of human conduct. The Yijing (Book of Changes) explores the subtle and profound, while poets record auspicious signs. Zuo Qiuming, with his vast knowledge and imaginative storytelling, writes articles that explore the changes of heaven and earth. These different approaches coexist without contradiction.

    2

    余生平寡嗜好，凡饮酒度曲ヅυ可以接群居之欢者，一无能焉，文史外无以自娱，乃广采游心骇耳之事，妄言妄听，记而存之，非有所惑也，譬如嗜味者餍八珍矣，而不广尝夫氐醢葵菹则脾困；嗜音者备《咸》、《韶》矣，而不旁及于侏亻离亻禁亻末则耳狭。以妄驱庸，以骇起惰，不有博弈者乎？为之犹贤，是亦裨谌适野之一乐也。昔颜鲁公、李邺侯功在社稷，而好谈神怪；韩昌黎以道自任，而喜驳杂无稽之谈；徐骑省排斥佛、老，而好采异闻，门下士竟有伪造以取媚者。四贤之长，吾无能为役也；四贤之短，则吾窃取之矣。

    Explanation

    2

    I have a few questions to ensure the translation is accurate and faithful to the original text:

    1. "凡饮酒度曲ヅυ可以接群居之欢者，一无能焉" - The character "ヅυ" seems to be a placeholder or a typo. Could you clarify what this character is supposed to represent? It might be important for understanding the context of the sentence.

    2. "乃广采游心骇耳之事，妄言妄听，记而存之" - The phrase "游心骇耳" is a bit ambiguous. Does it mean "things that wander the mind and startle the ears" or "things that captivate the mind and startle the ears"? The interpretation could affect the translation.

    3. "以妄驱庸，以骇起惰" - The phrase "以妄驱庸" is a bit unclear. Does it mean "to drive away the mediocre with the extraordinary" or "to drive away the mediocre with the fantastical"? The choice of words could influence the translation.

    4. "昔颜鲁公、李邺侯功在社稷，而好谈神怪" - The phrase "功在社稷" is clear, but "好谈神怪" could be interpreted as "enjoyed talking about gods and ghosts" or "enjoyed discussing supernatural phenomena." Which interpretation is more appropriate?

    5. "四贤之长，吾无能为役也；四贤之短，则吾窃取之矣" - The phrase "四贤之长" is clear, but "四贤之短" could be interpreted as "the weaknesses of the four sages" or "the shortcomings of the four sages." Which interpretation is more appropriate?

    Please clarify these points so I can provide the most accurate translation.

    3

    书成，初名《子不语》，后见元人说部有雷同者，乃改为《新齐谐》云。

    　　●卷一○李通判广西李通判者，巨富也。家蓄七姬，珍宝山积。通判年二十七疾卒。有老仆者，素忠谨，伤其主早亡，与七姬共设斋醮。忽一道人持簿化缘，老仆呵之曰：“吾家主早亡，无暇施汝。”道士笑曰：“尔亦思家主复生乎？吾能作法，令其返魂。”老仆惊，奔语诸姬，群讶然。出拜，则道士去矣。老仆与群妾悔轻慢神仙，致令化去，各相归咎。

    　　未几，老仆过市，遇道士于途。老仆惊且喜，强持之请罪乞哀。道士曰：“我非靳尔主之复生也，阴司例：死人还阳，须得替代。恐尔家无人代死，吾是以去。”老仆曰：“请归商之。”

    Explanation

    3

    The book was completed and initially titled The Master Didn't Speak. Later, the author discovered that Yuan dynasty works had similar titles, so he changed the title to New Qi Xie.

    In the tenth volume of the Guangxi section, there was a magistrate named Li, who was extremely wealthy. He kept seven concubines and amassed a mountain of treasures. At the age of twenty-seven, the magistrate died suddenly from illness. There was an old servant who was always loyal and careful. Grieving the early death of his master, he set up a ritual with the seven concubines. Suddenly, a Taoist priest came with a register to beg for alms. The old servant scolded him, saying, "Our master died young, and we have no time to give you anything." The Taoist priest laughed and said, "Do you also want your master to come back to life? I can perform a spell to make him return." The old servant was startled and rushed to tell the concubines, who were all surprised. When they went out to pay respects, the Taoist priest had already left. The old servant and the concubines regretted their disrespect to the Taoist priest, who had disappeared, and blamed each other.

    After a short while, the old servant passed through the market and met the Taoist priest on the road. The old servant was both surprised and delighted, and he insisted on holding the Taoist priest to ask for forgiveness and mercy. The Taoist priest said, "I did not refuse to bring your master back to life. According to the rules of the underworld, when a dead person returns to life, someone must die in their place. I was afraid that there was no one in your household to die instead, so I left." The old servant said, "Please let me go back and discuss this with them."

    4

    拉道士至家，以道士语告群妾。群妾初闻道士之来也，甚喜；继闻将代死也，皆恚，各相视噤不发声。老仆毅然曰：“诸娘子青年可惜，老奴残年何足惜？”

    　　出见道士曰：“老奴者代，可乎？”道士曰：“尔能无悔无怖则可。”曰：“能。”

    　　道士曰：“念汝诚心，可出外与亲友作别。待我作法，三日法成，七日法验矣。”

    　　老仆奉道士于家，旦夕敬礼。身至某某家，告以故，泣而诀别。其亲友有笑者，有敬者，有怜者，有揶揄不信者。老仆过圣帝庙——素所奉也，入而拜且祷曰：“奴代家主死，求圣帝助道士放回家主魂魄。”语未竟，有赤脚僧立案前叱曰：“汝满面妖气，大祸至矣！吾救汝，慎弗泄。”赠一纸包曰：“临时取看。”

    　　言毕不见。老仆归，偷开之：手抓五具，绳索一根。遂置怀中。

    Explanation

    4

    The Taoist priest was brought to the house, and the group of concubines were informed of what the priest had said. Initially, upon hearing of the priest's arrival, they were very happy; but upon learning that he would die in their place, they all became angry, each looking at the others in silence. The old servant spoke resolutely, "The young ladies are young and precious, but my old age is not worth regretting."

    He then went out to meet the Taoist priest and said, "I will take your place, is that acceptable?" The priest replied, "If you can do it without regret or fear, then it is acceptable." The servant said, "I can."

    The priest said, "Considering your sincerity, you may go out to bid farewell to your friends and relatives. Wait for me to perform the ritual, which will be completed in three days, and its effectiveness will be verified in seven days."

    The old servant treated the Taoist priest with respect at home, paying him honor day and night. He went to the home of a certain person, informed them of the reason, and bid farewell while crying. Among his friends and relatives, some laughed, some were respectful, some were sympathetic, and some mocked and did not believe. The old servant passed by the Temple of the Holy Emperor, which he usually worshipped, and entered to worship and pray, saying, "I will die in place of my master. I ask the Holy Emperor to help the Taoist priest return my master's soul." Before he finished speaking, a barefoot monk stood in front of the table and shouted, "You are covered with demonic energy, great disaster is coming! I will save you, but be careful not to reveal this." He gave a paper package and said, "Take it out when the time comes."

    After he spoke, he disappeared. The old servant returned home and secretly opened the package: it contained five hand-cuffs and a rope. He then put them in his bosom.

    5

    俄而三日之期已届，道士命移老仆床与家主灵柩相对，铁锁扃门，凿穴以通饮食。道士与群姬相近处筑坛诵咒。居亡何，了无他异。老仆疑之。心甫动，闻床下飒然有声，两黑人自地跃出：绿睛深目，通体短毛，长二尺许，头大如车轮。

    　　两夹々视老仆，且视且走，绕棺而行，以齿啮棺缝。缝开，闻咳嗽声，宛然家主也。二鬼启棺之前和，扶家主出。状奄然若不胜病者。二鬼手摩其腹，口渐有声。老仆目之，形是家主，音则道士。愀然曰：“圣帝之言，得无验乎！”急揣怀中纸。五爪飞出，变为金龙，长数丈，攫老仆于室中，以绳缚梁上。老仆昏然，注目下视：二鬼扶家主自棺中出，至老仆卧床，无人焉者。家主大呼曰：“法败矣！”二鬼狰狞，绕屋寻觅，卒不得。家主怒甚，取老仆床帐被褥，碎裂之。一鬼仰头，见老仆在梁，大喜，与家主腾身取之。未及屋梁，震雷一声，仆坠于地，棺合如故，二鬼亦不复见矣。

    Explanation

    5

    Soon after the three-day period had expired, the Taoist ordered the old servant's bed to be moved to face the family head's coffin. He locked the door with an iron lock and dug a hole to pass food and drink. The Taoist and the group of maidens built an altar nearby and chanted spells. After a while, there was no other unusual occurrence. The old servant began to doubt. As his thoughts stirred, he heard a rustling sound from under the bed, and two black people jumped out from the ground: they had green eyes and deep-set eyes, were covered with short fur all over their bodies, were about two feet long, and had heads as big as carriage wheels.

    The two stared at the old servant, looking and walking at the same time, walking around the coffin, and biting the seams of the coffin. The seams opened, and a coughing sound was heard, which was clearly the family head. The two demons opened the coffin lid together and helped the family head out. The family head looked weak and sickly. The two demons rubbed their stomachs, and their mouths gradually made sounds. The old servant looked at them, seeing the appearance of the family head but hearing the voice of the Taoist. He said with a worried expression, "Could the words of the Holy Emperor not be true?" He quickly took out the paper from his pocket. Five claws flew out and transformed into a golden dragon, several zhang long, which seized the old servant in the room and tied him to the beam with a rope. The old servant became dazed and looked down with his eyes: the two demons helped the family head out of the coffin and reached the old servant's bed, but there was no one there. The family head shouted loudly, "The method has failed!" The two demons looked ferocious, searched around the house but finally could not find. The family head was very angry and tore up the old servant's bed curtains and quilts. One demon looked up, saw the old servant on the beam, and was very happy, then jumped up with the family head to catch him. Before reaching the roof beam, a thunderclap sounded, and the old servant fell to the ground, the coffin closed as before, and the two demons disappeared again.

    6

    群妾闻雷，往启户视之。老仆具道所见。相与急视道士。道士已为雷震死坛所，其尸上有硫磺大书“妖道炼法易形，图财贪色，天条决斩如律令”十七字。

    　　○蔡书生杭州北关门外有一屋，鬼屡见，人不敢居，扃锁甚固。书生蔡姓者将买其宅。

    　　人危之，蔡不听。券成，家人不肯入。蔡亲自启屋，秉烛坐。至夜半，有女子冉冉来，颈拖红帛，向蔡侠拜，结绳于梁，伸颈就之。蔡无怖色。女子再挂一绳，招蔡。蔡曳一足就之。女子曰：“君误矣。”蔡笑曰：“汝误才有今日，我勿误也。”鬼大笑，伏地再拜去。自此，怪遂绝，蔡亦登第。或云即蔡炳侯方伯也。

    Explanation

    6

    The concubines heard thunder and went to open the door to look. The old servant detailed what he had seen. They hurriedly looked at the Taoist. The Taoist had been struck dead by thunder at the altar, and there was sulfur written in large characters on his body: "The evil Taoist who transformed his form to seek wealth and lust, was condemned by heavenly law to be decapitated according to the law." Seventeen characters were written in this manner.

    ○ A scholar named Piao lived outside the northern gate of Hangzhou. There was a house there where ghosts were often seen, and no one dared to live there; it was locked very securely. A scholar named Piao wanted to buy the house.

    People warned him, but Piao did not listen. After the contract was signed, his family members refused to enter. Piao personally opened the house and sat with a candle. At midnight, a woman came slowly, her neck dragging a red silk, and she bowed gracefully to Piao. She tied a rope to the beam and extended her neck to it. Piao showed no fear. The woman hung another rope and beckoned to Piao. Piao pulled one foot towards her. The woman said, "You were mistaken to have this day." Piao laughed and said, "You were mistaken to have this day, but I am not mistaken." The ghost laughed loudly, prostrated herself, and left. From then on, the strange occurrences ceased, and Piao also passed the imperial examination. Some say it was Piao Binghou, the Governor.

    7

    ○南昌士人江南南昌县有士人某，读书北兰寺，一长一少，甚相友善。长者归家暴卒，少者不知也，在寺读书如故。天晚睡矣，见长者披闼入，登床抚其背曰：“吾别兄不十日，竟以暴疾亡。今我鬼也，朋友之情不能自割，特来诀别。”少者阴喝，不能言。死者慰之曰：“吾欲害兄，岂肯直告？兄慎弗怖。吾之所以来此者，欲以身后相托也。”少者心稍定，问：“托何事？”曰：“吾有老母，年七十馀，妻年未三十，得数斛米，足以养生，愿兄周恤之，此其一也。吾有文稿未梓，愿兄为镌刻，俾微名不泯，此其二也。吾欠卖笔者钱数千，未经偿还，愿兄偿之，此其三也。”少者唯唯。死者起立曰：“既承兄担承，吾亦去矣。”言毕欲走。

    　　少者见其言近人情，貌如平昔，渐无怖意，乃泣留之，曰：“与君长诀，何不稍缓须叟去耶？”死者亦泣，回坐其床，更叙平生。数语复起曰：“吾去矣。”

    Explanation

    7

    In the county of Nanchang in Jiangnan, there was a scholar who studied at the North Blue Temple. He had two friends, one older and one younger, who were very close to each other. The older friend returned home and suddenly died of an illness, but the younger one did not know this and continued to study at the temple as usual. One evening, as he was about to sleep, he saw the older friend push open the door and climb onto the bed, patting his back and saying, "I have been away from my brother for less than ten days, and I have died suddenly. Now I am a ghost, and I cannot sever the bond of friendship, so I have come to say goodbye." The younger one tried to shout but could not speak. The deceased comforted him, saying, "If I wanted to harm you, would I tell you directly? Please do not be afraid. The reason I have come is to entrust you with matters after my death." The younger one's heart gradually calmed, and he asked, "What matters do you entrust me with?" The deceased replied, "I have an elderly mother, over seventy years old, and a wife who is not yet thirty. With a few measures of rice, they can live comfortably. I wish for my brother to look after them, that is the first matter. I have some writings that have not been printed, and I wish for my brother to have them engraved, so that my faint name will not fade away, that is the second matter. I owe the scribe several thousand coins, which I have not yet repaid, and I wish for my brother to repay them, that is the third matter." The younger one nodded in agreement. The deceased stood up and said, "Since my brother has taken on these matters, I will also leave now." After finishing his words, he wanted to go.

    The younger one saw that his words were close to human nature, and he looked just as he did before, so he gradually lost his fear. He then cried and tried to keep him, saying, "With such a long farewell, why can't you delay a little while before leaving?" The deceased also cried, sat back on the bed, and recounted their life events. After a few words, he stood up again and said, "I must leave now."

    8

    立而不行，两眼瞠视，貌渐丑败。少者惧，促之曰：“君言既毕，可去矣。”尸竟不去。少者拍床大呼，亦不去，屹立如故。少者愈骇，起而奔，尸随之奔。少者奔愈急，尸奔亦急。追逐数里，少者逾墙仆地，尸不能逾墙，而垂首墙外，口中涎沫与少者之面相滴涔涔也。

    　　天明，路人过之，饮以姜汁，少者苏。尸主家方觅见不得，闻信，舁归成殡。

    　　识者曰：“人之魂善而魄恶，人之魂灵而魄愚。其始来也，一灵不泯，魄附魂以行；其既去也，心事既毕，魂一散而魄滞。魂在，则其人也；魂去，则非其人也。世之移尸走影，皆魄为之，惟有道之人为能制魄。

    　　○曾虚舟康熙年间，有曾虚舟者，自言四川荣昌县人，佯狂吴、楚间，言多奇中。所到处，老幼男妇环之而行。虚舟嬉笑骂，所言辄中人隐。或与人好言，其人大哭去；或笞骂人，人大喜过望。在问者自知之，旁人不知。

    Explanation

    8

    The Corpse That Wouldn't Leave

    The corpse stood motionless, its eyes wide open and staring, its appearance gradually becoming ugly and decaying. The young person, frightened, urged it to say, "Your words have been spoken; you may leave now." But the corpse still did not leave. The young person slapped the bed and shouted loudly, but the corpse remained unmoved, standing firmly as before. The young person became even more frightened, got up and ran, with the corpse following in pursuit. The faster the young person ran, the faster the corpse ran. They chased each other for several miles until the young person jumped over a wall and fell to the ground. The corpse could not jump over the wall, hanging its head over the edge, with saliva dripping from its mouth onto the young person's face.

    At dawn, passersby came by and gave the young person ginger juice to drink, and the young person regained consciousness. The family of the corpse was searching for it but had not found it. Upon hearing the news, they carried the corpse back to be buried.

    Those who understand say: "The soul is good while the ghostly essence is evil; the soul is intelligent while the ghostly essence is foolish. When it first arrives, the soul remains intact, and the ghostly essence follows the soul. When it departs, the soul has completed its task and disperses, leaving the ghostly essence to linger. When the soul is present, it is a person; when the soul leaves, it is no longer a person. The movement of corpses and shadows in the world are all caused by the ghostly essence, and only those with virtue can control the ghostly essence."

    ○ During the reign of Emperor Kangxi, there was a man named Zeng Xuzhou, who claimed to be from Rongchang County in Sichuan. He pretended to be mad in Wu and Chu, and his words often hit the mark in strange ways. Wherever he went, elders, young, men, and women surrounded him as he walked. Zeng Xuzhou laughed, joked, and cursed, and what he said always hit the mark in people's hidden thoughts. Sometimes he spoke kindly to someone, and the person cried and left; sometimes he scolded or cursed someone, and the person was overjoyed. Those who asked knew it themselves, while others did not know.

    9

    杭州王子坚先生知泸溪县事，罢官后，或议其祖坟风水不利。子坚意欲迁葬而未果，闻虚舟来，走问之。适虚舟持棒登高阜，众人环挤，子坚不得前。虚舟望见子坚，遥击以棒，骂曰：“你莫来！你莫来！你来便想抠尸盗骨了！行不得！

    　　行不得！“子坚悚然而归。后子坚子文璇官至御史。

    　　○钟孝廉余同年邵又房，幼从钟孝廉某，常熟人也，先生性方正，不苟言笑，与又房同卧起。忽夜半醒，哭曰：“吾死矣。”又房问故，曰：“吾梦见二隶人从地下耸身起，至榻前拉吾同行。路泱泱然，黄沙白草，了不见人。行数里，引入一官衙，有神乌纱冠，南向坐。隶掖我跪堂下，神曰：”汝知罪乎？‘曰：“不知。’神曰：”试思之。‘我思良久，曰：“某知矣。某不孝，某父母死，停棺二十年，无力卜葬，罪当万死。’神曰：”罪小。‘曰：“某少时曾淫一婢，又狎二妓。’神曰：”罪小。‘曰：“某有口过，好讥弹人文章。

    Explanation

    9

    A Man from Hangzhou and His Son, and a Dream of Divine Judgment

    Mr. Wang Zijian of Hangzhou served as the magistrate of Luxi County. After being dismissed from office, some people speculated that the feng shui of his ancestral tomb was unfavorable. Wang Zijian intended to relocate the tomb but did not succeed. He heard that Xu Zhou was coming and hurried to ask him. Just as Xu Zhou was holding a stick and climbing a high mound, the crowd was surrounding and pushing him, and Wang Zijian could not get through. Xu Zhou saw Wang Zijian, struck him with the stick from afar, and shouted, "You must not come! You must not come! If you come, you will want to steal bones! You cannot! You cannot!" Wang Zijian was startled and returned home. Later, Wang Zijian's son Wenxuan became a censor.

    ○Zhong Shoulian, my fellow examinee from the same year, Shao Youfang, was young and followed Zhong Shoulian, who was from Changshu. Mr. Zhong was upright and did not speak or laugh frivolously. He slept and rose with Shao Youfang. Suddenly at midnight, he woke up and cried, saying, "I am going to die." Shao Youfang asked him why, and he said, "I dreamed that two servants emerged from the ground, came to my bed, and pulled me along. The road was vast, with yellow sand and white grass, and no one was seen. After walking several miles, they led me to a government office, where a god wearing a black cap sat facing south. The servants pushed me to kneel in front of the hall. The god said, 'Do you know your sins?' I said, 'I don't know.' The god said, 'Think about it.' I thought for a long time and said, 'I know now. I am unfilial. My parents died, and the coffin has been lying for twenty years. I have no means to bury them. I deserve to die a thousand deaths.' The god said, 'The sin is small.' I said, 'When I was young, I had sexual relations with a maid and was intimate with two courtesans.' The god said, 'The sin is small.' I said, 'I have spoken ill of others' writings.'

    10

    ’神曰：”此更小矣。‘曰：’然则某无他罪。‘神顾左右曰：“令渠照来。’左右取水一盘，沃其面，恍惚悟前生姓杨，名敝，曾偕友贸易湖南，利其财物，推入水中死。不觉战栗，匐伏神前曰：”知罪。‘神厉声曰：“还不变么！’举手拍案，霹雳一声，天崩地坼，城郭、衙署、神鬼、器械之类，了无所睹；但见汪洋大水，无边无岸，一身渺然，飘浮于菜叶之上。自念叶轻身重，何得不坠？回视己身，已化蛆虫，耳目口鼻，悉如芥子，不觉大哭而醒。吾梦若是，其能久乎？”又房为宽解曰：“先生毋苦，梦不足凭也。”先生命速具棺殓之物。越三日，呕血暴亡。

    Explanation

    10

    The deity said, "This is even worse." He replied, "Then I have no other sins." The deity looked around and said, "Let him show himself." The attendants took a basin of water and sprinkled it on his face. He vaguely remembered his past life, his name was Yang Bi. He had once traveled with friends to Hunan, profited from their goods, and pushed them into the water to die. He couldn't help but tremble and prostrated himself before the deity, saying, "I know my guilt." The deity spoke sternly, "Won't you change?" He raised his hand and struck the table, there was a thunderclap. The sky and earth split apart. The city walls, government offices, gods, ghosts, and weapons, all vanished. All he saw was vast, boundless water. He felt small and insignificant, floating on a piece of lettuce. He thought the leaf was light and his body heavy, how could he not sink? He looked at himself and saw he had turned into a maggot. His eyes, ears, nose, and mouth were all as small as mustard seeds. He couldn't help but cry and woke up. If my dream is like this, how long can it last? The friend tried to comfort him, saying, "Mr., don't be sad, dreams are not to be trusted." The man ordered the preparation of a coffin and burial items. Three days later, he vomited blood and died suddenly.

    11

    ○南山顽石海昌陈秀才某，祷梦于肃愍庙。梦肃愍开正门延之，秀才逡巡。肃愍曰：“汝异日我门生也，礼应正门入。”坐未定，侍者启：“汤溪县城隍禀见。”随见一神峨冠来。肃愍命陈与抗礼，曰：“渠属吏，汝门生，汝宜上坐。”秀才惶恐而坐。闻城隍神与肃愍语甚细，不可辨，但闻“死在广西，中在汤溪，南山顽石，一活万年”十六字。城隍告退，肃愍命陈送之。至门，城隍曰：“向与于公之言，君颇闻乎？”曰：“但闻十六字。”神曰：“志之，异日当有验也。”入见肃愍，言亦如之。惊而醒，以梦语人，莫解其故。

    Explanation

    11

    A Scholar from Haiyang Named Chen, Dreaming at the Temple of the Revered and Compassionate

    A scholar from Haiyang, whose name was Chen, sought a dream at the temple of the Revered and Compassionate. In his dream, the revered figure opened the main gate to welcome him. The scholar hesitated, and the revered figure said, "You will be my disciple in the future, and according to propriety, you should enter through the main gate." Before they could settle, a servant announced, "The city god of Tangxi County requests an audience." Soon, a god with a majestic headdress appeared. The revered figure commanded Chen to treat the city god as an equal, saying, "He is a subordinate official, and you are my disciple; you should take the upper seat." The scholar, greatly alarmed, sat down. He overheard the city god and the revered figure speaking in a low voice, which he could not understand, but he heard sixteen words: "Death in Guangxi, success in Tangxi, the stubborn stone of the south, and one life lasting ten thousand years." After the conversation, the city god withdrew, and the revered figure sent the scholar to see him off. At the gate, the city god asked, "Did you hear what I said to Yu Gong?" The scholar replied, "I only heard the sixteen words." The god said, "Remember them, for they will be proven true in the future." Upon returning to the temple, the scholar reported the same events to the revered figure. He woke up in great surprise and told others about his dream, but no one could understand its meaning.

    12

    陈家贫，有表弟李姓者，选广西某府通判，欲与同行。陈不可，曰：“梦中神言‘死在广西’，若同行，恐不祥。”通判解之曰：“神言‘始在广西’，乃始终之‘始’，非死生之‘死’也。若既死在广西矣，又安得‘中在汤溪’乎？”

    　　陈以为然，偕至广西。

    　　通判署中西厢房，封锁甚秘，人莫敢开。陈开之，中有园亭花石，遂移榻焉。

    　　月馀无恙。八月中秋，在园醉歌曰：“月明如水照楼台。”闻空中有人拊掌笑曰：“‘月明如水浸楼台’，易‘照’字便不佳。”陈大骇，仰视之，有一老翁，白藤帽，葛衣，坐梧桐枝上。陈悸，急趋卧内。老翁落地，以手持之曰：“无怖。

    Explanation

    12

    A Poor Man's Tale

    Chen, coming from a poor family, had a cousin named Li who was appointed as the magistrate of a certain district in Guangxi. Li wished to travel together with Chen. Chen refused, saying, "In a dream, a divine message said 'I will die in Guangxi.' If we travel together, it might be inauspicious." The magistrate explained, "The divine message says 'I will start in Guangxi,' meaning the beginning of my journey, not death. If I were to die in Guangxi, how could I have 'continued in Táng Xī'?"

    Chen agreed and went to Guangxi with the magistrate.

    The magistrate's office had a western wing that was sealed and no one dared to open. Chen opened it and found a garden with pavilions and stones, so he moved his bed there.

    After a month, nothing went wrong. On the Mid-Autumn Festival, Chen sang while drunk in the garden, saying, "The bright moonlight like water illuminates the towers and pavilions." He heard someone clapping and laughing in the air, saying, "‘The bright moonlight like water seeps into the towers and pavilions,’ changing 'illuminates' to 'seeps' makes the line better." Chen was terrified, looked up, and saw an old man wearing a white straw hat and a light, coarse cotton or linen garment, sitting on a Chinese parasol tree branch. Chen was afraid and rushed to his room. The old man landed and held him, saying, "Do not be afraid."

    13

    世有风雅之鬼如我者乎？“问：”翁何神？“曰：”勿言。吾且与汝论诗。“陈见其须眉古朴，不异常人，意渐解。入室内，互相唱和。老翁所作字，皆蝌蚪形，不能尽识。问之，曰：”吾少年时，俗尚此种笔画，今颇欲以楷法易之，缘手熟，一时未能骤改。“所云少年时，乃娲皇前也。自此每夜辄来，情甚狎。

    　　通判家僮常见陈持杯向空处对饮，急白通判。通判亦觉陈神气恍惚，责曰：“汝染邪气，恐‘死在广西’之言验矣。”陈大悟，与通判谋归家避之。甫登舟，老翁先在，旁人俱莫见也。路过江西，老翁谓曰：“明日将入浙境，吾与汝缘尽矣，不得不倾吐一言：吾修道一万年，未成正果，为少檀香三千斤，刻一玄女像耳。今向汝乞之，否则将借汝之心肺。”陈大惊，问：“翁修问道？曰：”斤车大道。“陈悟”斤“、”车“二字，合成一”斩“字，愈骇，曰：”俟归家商之。“

    Explanation

    13

    Is there a refined ghost like me in the world? "Ask: 'What kind of spirit are you, sir?' He replied: 'Do not speak of it. I will discuss poetry with you.' Chen saw his eyebrows and beard were ancient and simple, not unlike ordinary people, and gradually understood. They entered the room, and they reciprocally recited poetry. The old man's writing was all in ancient script, which Chen could not fully read. He asked, 'When I was young, the fashion was for this kind of stroke. Now I wish to change it to regular script, but because my hands are accustomed, I cannot change it suddenly.' The old man's youth refers to before the time of Nüwa. From then on, he came every night, and the relationship became very intimate.

    The magistrate's servant often saw Chen holding a cup and drinking in the air, and immediately reported to the magistrate. The magistrate also noticed that Chen seemed distracted, and scolded him: 'You have been influenced by evil spirits, and the prophecy of "dying in Guangxi" might come true.' Chen realized the gravity of the situation and decided to return home to avoid the ghost. As soon as he boarded the boat, the old man was already there, though no one else could see him. They passed through Jiangxi, and the old man said, 'Tomorrow we will enter Zhejiang, and our缘分 is coming to an end. I must tell you one thing: I have been cultivating my path for ten thousand years, but I have not yet achieved enlightenment. I need three thousand catties of sandalwood to carve an image of the Queen Mother of the West. I am asking you for it. Otherwise, I will take your heart and lungs.' Chen was greatly shocked and asked, 'You cultivate the path? He replied: 'The path of the axe and chariot.' Chen realized that the characters '斤' and '车' combined to form '斩', and was even more terrified, saying, 'Wait until I return home to discuss it.'

    14

    同至海昌，告其亲友，皆曰：“肃慰所谓‘南山顽石’者，得毋此怪耶？”

    　　次日，老翁至。陈曰：“翁家可住南山乎？”翁变色，骂曰：“此非汝所能言，必有恶人教汝。”陈以其语语友。友曰：“然则拉此怪入肃愍庙可也。”如其言，将至庙，老翁失色反走。陈两手挟持之，强掖以入。老翁长啸一声，冲天去。自此，怪遂绝。

    　　后陈生冒籍汤溪，竟成进士。会试房师，乃状元于振也。

    　　○酆都知县四川酆都县，俗传人鬼交界处。县中有井，每岁焚纸钱帛镪投之，约费三千金，名“纳阴司钱粮”。人或吝惜，必生瘟疫。国初，知县刘纲到任，闻而禁之，众论哗然。令持之颇坚。众曰：“公能与鬼神言明乃可。”令曰：“鬼神何在？”

    Explanation

    14

    Together they went to Haichang and informed his relatives and friends, who all said, "Isn't this the strange thing that Su Wei referred to as 'the stubborn stone of the South Mountain'?"

    The next day, the old man arrived. Chen asked, "Can your family live in the South Mountain?" The old man changed color and shouted, "This is not something you can say; there must be some evil person teaching you." Chen repeated these words to his friend. The friend said, "Then we should drag this strange thing into the temple of Su Min." Following this advice, they were about to reach the temple when the old man turned pale and ran away. Chen held him with both hands and forcibly led him inside. The old man let out a long howl and flew into the sky. From then on, the strange thing ceased.

    Later, Chen pretended to be from Tangxi and eventually became a Jinshi (imperial scholar). His examiner during the imperial examinations was the Zhuangyuan (top scorer) Yu Zhen.

    ○ The County Magistrate of Fengdu, Sichuan Province, Fengdu County, is said to be the place where the human and ghost worlds meet. There is a well in the county, and every year, people burn paper money, silk, and other offerings into it, costing about 3,000 gold pieces, known as "paying taxes to the underworld." If people are stingy, they will surely suffer from epidemics. At the beginning of the Ming Dynasty, the county magistrate Liu Gang took office and heard about this practice. He banned it, causing public outrage. However, the magistrate was very firm in his decision. The people said, "You must be able to communicate with the ghosts and spirits to do this." The magistrate replied, "Where are the ghosts and spirits?"

    15

    曰：“井底即鬼神所居，无人敢往。”令毅然曰：“为民请命，死何惜？吾当自行。”命左右取长绳，缚而坠焉。众持留之，令不可。其幕客李诜，豪士也，请令曰：“吾欲知鬼神之情状，请与子俱。”令沮之，客不可，亦缚而坠焉。入井五丈许，地黑复明，灿然有天光。所见城郭宫室，悉如阳世。其人民藐小，映日无影，蹈空而行，自言“在此者不知有地也”。见县令，皆罗拜曰：“公阳官，来何为？”今曰：“吾为阳间百姓请免阴司钱粮。”众鬼啧啧称贤，手加额曰：“此事须与包阎罗商之。”令曰：“包公何在？”曰：“在殿上。”引至一处，宫室巍峨，上有冕旒而坐者，年七十馀，容貌方严。群鬼传呼曰：“某县令至。”

    Explanation

    15

    The magistrate said, "The bottom of the well is where ghosts and spirits reside, and no one dares to go there." The magistrate resolutely said, "To plead for the people, what is there to fear about death? I shall go myself." He ordered his attendants to fetch a long rope, tied himself up, and descended. The crowd tried to hold him back, but the magistrate could not be stopped. His secretary, Li Sheng, a bold man, asked the magistrate, "I want to know the state of the ghosts and spirits. Please let me accompany you." The magistrate tried to dissuade him, but Li Sheng could not be stopped, and he also tied himself up and descended. They descended about five zhang, and the ground was dark but then became bright again, brightly lit with heavenly light. What they saw were cities, palaces, and buildings, all like the world above. The people there were tiny, casting no shadows by the sun, walking through the air, and said, "Those who are here do not know there is earth." When they saw the county magistrate, they all bowed in a circle and said, "You are an official of the sun, why have you come here?" The magistrate said, "I have come to plead for the people of the world above to be exempt from the taxes of the underworld." The ghosts praised him repeatedly as wise, put their hands to their foreheads, and said, "This matter must be discussed with Bao Yanluo." The magistrate said, "Where is Bao Gong?" They said, "He is on the throne." They led him to a place where the palaces were grand, and a man with a crown and streamers sat above, about seventy years old, with a stern appearance. The ghosts announced, "The county magistrate has arrived."

    16

    公下阶迎，揖以上坐，曰：“阴阳道隔，公来何为？”令起立拱手曰：“酆都水旱频年，民力竭矣。朝廷国课，尚苦不输，岂能为阴司纳帛镪，再作租户哉？知县冒死而来，为民请命。”包公笑曰：“世有妖憎恶道，借鬼神为口实，诱人修斋打醮，倾家者不下千万。鬼神幽明道隔，不能家喻户晓，破其诬罔。明公为民除弊，虽不来此，谁敢相违？今更宠临，具征仁勇。”语未竟，红光自天而下。

    　　包公起曰：“伏魔大帝至矣，公少避。”刘退至后堂。少顷，关神绿袍长髯，冉冉而下，与包公行宾主礼，语多不可辨。关神曰：“公处有生人气，何也？”包公具道所以。关曰：“若然，则贤令也，我愿见之。”令与幕客李，惶恐出拜。

    Explanation

    16

    The magistrate descended the steps to greet, bowed, and invited the official to sit above, saying, "The realms of the living and the dead are separated by a path. Why did you come?" The magistrate stood up, bowed his hands, and said, "Fengdu has suffered frequent floods and droughts over the years, and the people's strength is exhausted. We still struggle to pay the imperial tax; how can we pay silk and money to the underworld and become tenants again? I risked my life to come here, pleading for the people." Bao Zheng smiled and said, "There are evil people who hate the right path and use ghosts and spirits as an excuse to lure people into fasting and rituals, ruining not less than ten thousand families. Ghosts and spirits are separated by a path, and their distinction cannot be known to every household, breaking their deception. You have removed corruption for the people; even if you did not come here, who would dare to disobey? Your visit here fully demonstrates your benevolence and courage." Before he finished speaking, a red light descended from the sky.

    Bao Zheng stood up and said, "The Emperor of Subduing Demons has arrived. Please step aside." Liu retreated to the back hall. After a short while, Guan Yu, with a green robe and long beard, slowly descended, and he and Bao Zheng observed the formal guest-host etiquette. Much of their conversation was indistinguishable. Guan Yu said, "There is the energy of a living person here. Why is that?" Bao Zheng explained the reason. Guan Yu said, "If that is the case, then you are an excellent magistrate. I wish to meet him." The magistrate and his attendant Li, fearful and respectful, came out to bow.

    17

    关赐坐，颜色甚温，问世事甚悉，惟不及幽冥之事。

    　　李素赣，遽问曰：“玄德公何在？”关不答，色不怿，帽发尽指，即辞去。

    　　包公大惊，谓李曰：“汝必为雷击死，吾不能救汝矣。此事何可问也！况于臣子之前呼其君之字乎！”令代为乞哀。包公曰：“但令速死，免致焚尸。”取匣中玉印方尺许，解李袍背印之。令与李拜谢毕，仍缒而出。甫到酆都南门，李竟中风而亡。未几，暴雷震电，绕其棺椁，衣服焚烧殆尽，惟背间有印处不坏。

    　　○骷髅报仇常熟孙君寿，性狞恶，好慢神虐鬼。与人游山，胀如厕，戏取荒冢骷髅，蹲踞之，令吞其粪，曰：“汝食佳乎？”骷髅张口曰：“佳。”君寿大骇，急走。

    Explanation

    17

    Guan Yu invited him to sit, his demeanor was very warm, and he asked about worldly affairs in great detail, but only avoided questions about the underworld.

    Li, who had always been respectful, suddenly asked, "Where is Lord Xuan德?" Guan Yu did not answer, his expression was not pleased, his hat and hair were all pointing, and he immediately took his leave.

    Judge Bao was greatly alarmed and told Li, "You will surely be struck by lightning, I cannot save you. How can this be asked! How much more so to call one's lord by his personal name in front of his subjects!" He ordered someone to beg for mercy on Li's behalf. Judge Bao said, "Just let you die quickly, to avoid burning the body." He took a jade seal from a box, about a foot square, unfastened Li's robe and placed the seal on his back. He ordered him to bow and thank Li, and still descended by rope. Just as he arrived at the south gate of Fengdu, Li died of a stroke. Not long after, a violent thunderstorm surrounded his coffin, his clothes were almost completely burned, only the area where the seal was placed remained unharmed.

    ○ Skull Seeking Revenge

    In Changshu, there was a man named Sun Junshou, who was cruel and evil, and enjoyed mocking gods and tormenting ghosts. While traveling with someone in the mountains, he became angry and went to the toilet. He playfully took a skull from a desolate grave, sat on it, and made it swallow his excrement, saying, "Do you like it?" The skull opened its mouth and said, "Yes." Sun Junshou was greatly frightened and hurried away.

    18

    骷髅随之滚地，如车轮然。君寿至桥，骷髅不得上。君寿登高望之，骷髅仍滚归原处。君寿至家，面如死灰，遂病。日遗矢，辄手取吞之，自呼曰：“汝食佳乎？”

    　　食毕更遗，遗毕更食，三日而死。

    　　○骷髅吹气杭州闵茂嘉，好弈，其师孙姓者，常与之弈。雍正五年六月，暑甚，闵招友五人，循环而弈。孙弈毕，曰：“我倦，去东厢少睡，再来决胜。”少顷，闻东厢有叫号声。闵与四人趋视之，见孙伏地。涎沫满颐。饮以姜汁，苏，问之。曰：“吾床上睡未熟，觉背间有一点冷，如胡桃大，渐至盘大，未几而半席皆冷，直透心骨，未得其故。闻床下弗弗然有声，俯视之，一骷髅张口隔席吹我，不觉骇绝，遂仆于地。骷髅竟以头击我。闻人来，始去。”四人咸请掘之。闵家子惧有祸，不敢掘，遂扃东厢。

    Explanation

    18

    A Skull Rolling Like a Wheel

    As Mr. Junshou walked, the skull followed and rolled on the ground, like a wheel. When he reached the bridge, the skull could not go up. When he climbed high to look at it, the skull still rolled back to its original place. When he returned home, his face was ashen, and he fell ill. Every day, he excreted, and he took it in his hand and swallowed it, calling out to himself, "Is this good to eat?"

    After eating, he excreted again; after excreting, he ate again. He died after three days.

    ○ Skull Blowing Air

    Min Maoguang of Hangzhou loved chess, and his teacher, Mr. Sun, often played with him. In June of the fifth year of Yongzheng, it was very hot, so Min invited five friends to play chess in a loop. After Mr. Sun finished playing, he said, "I am tired; I will go to the eastern chamber to take a short nap and then return to decide the winner." Shortly after, they heard a calling sound from the eastern chamber. Min and the four others hurried to look, and they saw Mr. Sun lying on the ground. His mouth was full of saliva. They gave him ginger juice, and he revived, asking what had happened. He said, "I was sleeping on my bed and had not yet fallen asleep when I felt a cold sensation on my back, like a walnut in size. It gradually grew to the size of a tray, and soon the entire half of the mat was cold, penetrating to my bones. I did not understand the cause. I heard a rustling sound from under the bed. Looking down, I saw a skull opening its mouth and blowing across the mat at me. I was so terrified that I fell to the ground. The skull even hit me with its head. When I heard people coming, it finally left." The four others all asked to dig up the area. Min's son was afraid of disaster and dared not dig, so they locked the eastern chamber.

    19

    ○赵大将军刺皮脸怪赵大将军良栋，平三藩后，路过四川成都，川抚迎之，授馆于民家。将军嫌其隘，意欲宿城西察院衙门。抚军曰：“闻此中关锁百馀年，颇有怪，不敢为公备。”将军笑曰：“吾荡平寇贼，杀人无算，妖鬼有灵，亦当畏我。”即遣丁役扫除。置眷属于内室，而己独占正房，枕军中所用长戟而寝。

    　　至二鼓，帐钩声铿然，有长身而白衣者垂大腹障床面，烛光青冷。将军起，厉声喝之。怪退行三步，烛光为之一明，照见头面，俨然俗所画方相神也。将军拔戟刺之，怪闪身于梁；再刺，再走，逐入一夹道中，隐不复见。将军还房，觉有尾之者，回目之，此怪微笑蹑其后。将军大怒，骂曰：“世哪得有此皮脸怪耶！”

    Explanation

    19

    General Zhao Dalong, the great general, was passing through Chengdu in Sichuan after suppressing the Three Feudatories. The governor of Sichuan welcomed him and hosted him in a civilian's house. The general found the place too narrow and wished to stay in the western city's censorate office. The governor said, "I have heard that this place has been sealed for over a hundred years and is haunted. I dare not prepare it for you." The general laughed and said, "I have pacified the rebels and killed countless people. Even if the ghosts and spirits have power, they should also fear me." He immediately sent laborers to clean the place. He placed his family in the inner room and occupied the main house alone, using a long spear from the army as a pillow to sleep.

    At the second watch, there was a clear metallic sound from the tent hook. A tall, white-clad figure with a large belly blocked the view of the bed, and the candlelight was cold and blue. The general woke up and shouted at it loudly. The ghost retreated three steps, and the candlelight became brighter, revealing its face, which looked like the fangxiang god commonly depicted in folk art. The general pulled out his spear and stabbed at it, and the ghost dodged to the beam; he stabbed again, and it ran away, chased into a narrow alley, and disappeared. The general returned to his room and felt someone following him. He looked back and saw this ghost smiling and following him. The general became very angry and shouted, "How can there be such a skin-faced monster in the world!"

    20

    众家丁起，各持兵仗来，怪复退走。过夹道，入一空房，见沙飞尘起，簇簇有声，似其丑类共来格斗者。怪至中堂，挺然立，作负状。家丁相视无敢前。将军愈怒，手刺以戟，正中其腹，膨亨有声，其身面不复见矣，但有两金眼在壁上，大如铜盘，光夹夹射人。众家丁各以刀击之，化为满房火星，初大后小，以至于灭，东方已明。

    　　将军次日上马行，以所见语阖城文武，咸为咋舌，终不知何怪。

    　　○狐生员劝人修仙赵大将军之子襄敏公总督保定，夜读书西楼，门户已闭，有自窗缝中侧身入者，形甚扁；至楼中，以手搓头及手足，渐次而圆，方巾朱履，向上长揖拱手曰：“生员狐仙也，居此百年，蒙诸大人俱许在此。公忽来读书，生员不敢抗天子之大臣，故来请示。公必欲在此读书，某宜迁让，须宽宽三日。如公见怜，容其卵息于此，则请扃锁如平时。

    Explanation

    20

    The guards rose, each carrying weapons, and the monster retreated again. It passed through a narrow passage and entered an empty room, where it saw sand flying and dust rising, with a clattering sound, as if its fellow creatures were coming to fight. The monster stood upright in the central hall, as if carrying something heavy, and the guards looked at each other and none dared to advance. The general became even angrier, and with his spear, he struck it right in the abdomen. It made a loud noise, and its body and face were no longer visible. But there were two golden eyes on the wall, as large as copper plates, shining brightly and glaring at people. The guards struck it with their swords, and it turned into sparks filling the room, starting large and then small, until they disappeared. The east was already bright.

    The next day, the general rode out and told all the officials and soldiers in the city about what he had seen. Everyone was shocked and speechless, but ultimately did not know what kind of monster it was.

    The Fox Scholar Advises on Cultivating the Dao

    The son of General Zhao, who was known as the Xiangmin Gong, was the governor of Baoding. One night, while he was reading in the west tower, the doors and windows were already closed. Suddenly, a figure entered through the crack in the window, its shape extremely flat. Upon reaching the tower, it used its hands to rub its head and limbs, gradually becoming round. Wearing a square cap and red shoes, it bowed and clasped its hands respectfully, saying, "I am a fox spirit, residing here for a hundred years. I have been permitted to stay here by all the great men. Now that you have suddenly come to read, I dare not defy the high minister of the Son of Heaven, so I come to seek your permission. If you insist on reading here, I should yield, but please give me three days' notice. If you show mercy and allow my offspring to remain here, I will lock up as usual."

    21

    ”赵公大骇，笑曰：“尔狐矣，安得有生员？”曰：“群狐蒙太山娘娘考试，每岁一次。取其文理精通者为生员，劣者为野狐。生员可以修仙，野狐不许修仙。”因劝赵公曰：“公等贵人，可惜不学仙耳。如某等，学仙最难。先学人形，再学人语。学人语者，先学鸟语；学鸟语者，又必须尽学四海九州之鸟语；无所不能，然后能为人声，以成人形，其功已五百年矣。人学仙，较异类学仙少五百年功苦。若贵人、文人学仙，较凡人又省三百年功劳。大率学仙者，千年而成，此定理也。”公喜其言，即于次日扃西楼让之。

    　　此二事得于镇远太守讳之坛者，即将军之孙，且曰：“吾父后悔未问太山娘娘出何题目考狐也。”

    Explanation

    21

    Zhaogong was greatly startled and smiled, saying, "You are a fox, how could there be a scholar?" The fox replied, "The foxes are subjected to an examination by the goddess of Mount Tai, once a year. Those proficient in literature and reasoning are selected as scholars, while the inferior ones become wild foxes. Scholars can practice Daoism, but wild foxes are not allowed to do so." The fox then advised Zhaogong, "You gentlemen are nobles, a pity that you do not learn Daoism. For us foxes, learning Daoism is the most difficult. We first learn to take on a human form, then human speech. Those who learn human speech first learn bird language; those who learn bird language must also learn all bird languages from all over the world. Only after being able to do everything can they speak human language and take on a human form, which has taken them five hundred years. Humans learning Daoism require five hundred years less effort and hardship compared to other beings. If nobles and literati learn Daoism, they require three hundred years less effort compared to ordinary people. Generally, it takes a thousand years to learn Daoism, and this is a fixed rule." Zhaogong was pleased with his words and immediately locked the west tower the next day to allow him to stay.

    These two stories were obtained from the governor of Zhenyuan, whose name is withheld, who is the grandson of a general, and he said, "My father regrets not asking the goddess of Mount Tai what questions were used to examine the foxes."

    22

    ○煞神受枷淮安李姓者与妻某氏琴瑟调甚。李三十余病亡，已殓矣。妻不忍钉棺，朝夕哭，启而视之。故事：民间人死七日，则有迎煞之举，虽至戚，皆回避。妻独不肯，置子女于别室，己坐亡者帐中待之。

    　　至二鼓，阴风飒然，灯火尽绿。见一鬼红发圆眼，长丈余，手持铁叉，以绳牵其夫从窗外入。见棺前设酒馔，便放叉解绳，坐而大啖。每咽物，腹中啧啧有声。其夫摩抚旧时几案，怆然长叹，走至床前揭帐。妻哭抱之，泠然如一团冷云，遂裹以被。红发神竞前牵夺。妻大呼，子女尽至，红发神踉跄走。妻与子女以所裹魂放置棺中，尸渐奄然有气，遂抱至卧床上，灌以米汁，天明而苏。其所遗铁叉，俗所焚纸叉也。复为夫妇二十余年。

    　　妻六旬矣，偶祷于城隍庙，恍惚中见二弓丁舁一枷犯至。之所枷者，即红发神也。骂妇曰：“吾以贪馋故，为尔所弄，枷二十年矣！今乃相遇，肯放汝耶！”妇至家而卒。

    Explanation

    22

    A Woman and Her Husband's Return from the Dead in Huai'an

    A man named Li from Huai'an and his wife, whose name is not mentioned, had a very harmonious relationship. When Li, in his thirties, fell ill and passed away, he was already buried. However, his wife could not bear to nail the coffin shut. She wept day and night, and one day, she opened the coffin to look at him.

    According to tradition, when a person dies, there is a ritual called "welcoming the malevolent spirit" performed on the seventh day after death. Even close relatives avoid this ritual. But the wife refused to participate. She placed her children in a separate room and sat in the tent of the deceased, waiting for the spirit.

    At the second drumbeat of the night, a chilling wind blew, and the lights turned green. She saw a ghost with red hair and large eyes, about ten feet tall, holding an iron fork and dragging her husband into the room from the window. The ghost saw the food and drink set up in front of the coffin, and it dropped the fork and untied the rope, sitting down to eat voraciously. Each time it swallowed something, there was a loud noise in its stomach. Her husband, recognizing the old table, sighed deeply and walked to the bed, pulling back the curtain. The wife cried and embraced him, feeling him as cold as a cloud. She wrapped him in a blanket. The red-haired spirit tried to take him away. The wife screamed, and her children came running. The red-haired spirit stumbled and fled. The wife and her children then placed the wrapped spirit back in the coffin. The body gradually regained a faint breath and was carried to the bed, where they gave him a drink of rice water. He woke up the next morning. The iron fork left behind was a paper fork burned as part of a ritual.

    The couple lived together for another twenty years. When the wife was sixty years old, she prayed at the city god temple. In a daze, she saw two guards carrying a prisoner in shackles. The prisoner was the red-haired spirit. It scolded the wife, saying, "I was greedy and gluttonous, and you manipulated me, shackling me for twenty years! Now that we meet again, will you let me go?" The wife returned home and soon passed away.

    23

    ○张士贵直隶安州参将张士贵，以公廨太仄，买屋于城东。俗传其屋有怪。张素倔强，必欲居之。既移家矣，其中堂每夜闻击鼓声，家人惶恐。张乃挟弓矢，秉烛坐。

    　　至夜静时，梁上忽伸一头，睨而相笑。张射之，全身坠地，短黑而肥，腹大如五石匏；矢中其脐，入一尺许。鬼以手摩腹，笑曰：“好箭！”复射之，摩笑如前。

    　　张大呼，家人齐进，鬼升梁而走，詈曰：“必灭汝家！”次日天明，参将之妻暴卒；天暮，参将之子又卒。张棺殓毕，悲悔不已。

    　　居月馀，闻复壁中有呻吟声，往视，即其所殡之妻、子也。饮以姜汁，扬扬如平生。问之，皆曰：“吾未尝死，但昏昏如梦，见两大黑手，掷我于此。”开棺视之，荡然无有。方知人死有命，虽恶鬼相怨，亦仅能以幻术揶揄之，不能杀也。

    Explanation

    23

    Zhang Shigui, the Chief Military Officer of Anzhou in Zhili, bought a house in the eastern part of the city because his official residence was too cramped. It is said that the house was haunted. Zhang, who was known for his stubbornness, insisted on moving in. After he moved his family in, the main hall of the house would often hear drumming sounds at night, causing the family to be terrified. Zhang then took a bow and arrow and sat up all night with a candle.

    When it was quiet at night, a head suddenly extended from the beam and glanced at Zhang, laughing at him. Zhang shot an arrow at it, and the entire body fell to the ground. It was short, black, and fat, with an abdomen as large as a five-stone gourd. The arrow had hit its navel, penetrating about a foot deep. The ghost rubbed its abdomen and said, "Good shot!" Zhang shot it again, and the ghost rubbed its abdomen and laughed as before.

    Zhang shouted, and the family rushed in. The ghost climbed back onto the beam and ran away, cursing, "I will destroy your family!" The next day, Zhang's wife suddenly died. At dusk, his son also died. After Zhang had the bodies prepared for burial, he was filled with grief and regret.

    After a month, they heard moaning sounds coming from a secret wall. They went to investigate and found that it was the wife and son who had been buried. They gave them some ginger juice, and they acted as if they were alive. When asked, they both said, "We never died; we just felt like we were in a dream, and saw two large black hands throw us into this place." When they opened the coffin, it was completely empty. This made them realize that death is predetermined by fate, and even if evil spirits hold a grudge, they can only use magic to mock the living, not to kill them.

    24

    ○杜工部四川杜某，乾隆丁巳进士，为工部郎，年五十馀，续取襄阳某氏。婚夕，同年毕集。工部行礼毕，将入房，见花烛上有童子，长三四寸，踞烛盘，以口吹气，欲灭其火。工部喝之，应声走，两烛齐灭。宾客惊视，工部变色，汗如雨下。侍妾扶之登床，工部以手指屋之上下左右，云：“悉有人头。”汗愈甚，口渐不能言，是夕卒。襄阳夫人出轿时，见有蓬发女子迎问曰：“欲镌图章否？”夫人怪其语不伦，不之应。及工部死，始知揶揄夫人者即此怪也。

    　　工部卒后，附魂于夫人之体，每食，必扼其喉，悲啼曰：“舍不得。”同年周翰林煌正色责之曰：“杜君何愤愤！尔死与夫人何干？而反索其命乎？”鬼大哭绝声，夫人病随愈。

    Explanation

    24

    Du Gongbu, a man from Sichuan, was a successful candidate in the imperial examination of the year Ding Si during the reign of Emperor Qianlong. He served as a Lang in the Ministry of Works and, at the age of over fifty, remarried a woman from Xiangyang. On the night of their wedding, his contemporaries gathered. After completing the wedding rituals, Du Gongbu was about to enter the bridal chamber when he saw a small child, about three to four inches tall, sitting on the candle holder, blowing on the flames to try to extinguish them. Du Gongbu shouted at the child, who immediately ran away, causing both candles to go out. The guests were startled, and Du Gongbu's face turned pale; he was drenched in sweat. His servant helped him onto the bed, where he pointed to all sides of the room and said, "There are heads everywhere." His sweat increased, and he gradually lost the ability to speak. He died that night.

    When the lady from Xiangyang stepped out of her sedan chair, a woman with disheveled hair approached and asked, "Do you want to have a seal engraved?" The lady found the question strange and did not respond. It was only after Du Gongbu's death that she realized the woman who had mocked her was the same ghost.

    After Du Gongbu's death, his spirit attached itself to his wife's body. Every time she ate, he would strangle her throat and cry bitterly, saying, "I can't bear to leave." His contemporary, Zhou Huanlin, a scholar at the Imperial Academy, sternly rebuked him, saying, "Du Gongbu, why are you so angry? Your death has nothing to do with your wife; why are you seeking her life instead?" The ghost cried out in a loud, continuous manner, and the lady's illness soon subsided.

    25

    ○胡求为鬼球方阁学苞有仆胡求，年三十馀，随阁学入直。阁学修书武英殿，胡仆宿浴德堂中。夜三鼓，见二人舁之阶下，时月明如昼，照见二人皆青黑色，短袖仄襟，胡恐，急走。随见东首一神，红袍乌纱，长丈馀，以靴脚踢之，滚至西首。复有一神，如东首状貌衣裳，亦以靴脚踢之，滚至东首，将胡当作抛球者然。胡痛不可忍。五更鸡鸣，二神始去。胡委顿于地。明旦视之，遍身青肿，几无完肤。病数月始愈。

    　　○江中三太子苏州进士顾三典好食鼋，渔者知之，每得鼋，必售顾家。顾之岳母李氏夜梦金甲人哀求曰：“吾江中三太子也，为尔婿某所获，幸免我，心不忘报。”次早，遣家人驰救，则厨人已解之矣。是年进士家无故火自焚，图史散尽。未焚之夕，家畜一犬忽人立，以前两足擎双盂水献主人。又见屋壁上有历代祖宗，状貌如绘。

    Explanation

    25

    ○ A servant named Hu Qiu, who served the high-ranking scholar Fang Ge, was in his thirties and accompanied him on duty. The scholar was compiling books in the Wuying Hall, and Hu Qiu stayed at the Yude Hall. At the third watch of the night, he saw two people carrying him down the steps. The moon was bright as day, and he could see that the two people were dark blue, wearing short sleeves and tight-fitting robes. Frightened, Hu Qiu ran away. He then saw a deity on the eastern side, dressed in a red robe and black hat, about ten feet tall, who kicked him with his boot, causing him to roll to the western side. Another deity, similar in appearance and attire, also kicked him with his boot, rolling him back to the eastern side, treating him as if he were a ball. The pain was unbearable. At the fifth watch, when the rooster crowed, the two deities finally left. Hu Qiu collapsed on the ground. The next morning, he was found to be covered in bruises and swelling, almost without any intact skin. He suffered from illness for several months before recovering.

    ○ In Suzhou, a scholar named Gu Sandian, who had passed the imperial examinations, enjoyed eating turtles. The fishermen knew this and always sold their turtles to his family. One night, Gu's mother-in-law, Lady Li, dreamed of a golden-armored figure pleading, "I am the Third Prince of the River. I was captured by your son-in-law, and I beg you to save me; I will never forget your kindness." The next morning, she sent her servants to rescue him, but the cook had already prepared the turtle. That year, the scholar's house was mysteriously set on fire, and all his books and historical records were destroyed. On the night before the fire, a dog in the house suddenly stood upright on its hind legs, holding two bowls of water in its paws and offering them to the master. The family also saw ancestral portraits on the walls, painted as if they were alive.

    26

    识者曰：“此阳不藏阴之像也，其将火乎？”已而果然。

    　　○田烈妻江苏巡抚徐公士林，素正直。为安庆太守时，日暮升堂，月色皎然，见一女子以黑帕蒙首，肩以上眉目不可辨，跪仪门外，若诉冤者。徐公知为鬼，令吏卒持牌喝曰：“有冤者魂许进！”女子冉冉入，跪阶下，声嘶如小儿，吏卒不见，但闻其声。自言姓田，寡居守节，为其夫兄方德逼嫁谋产，致令缢死。徐公为拘夫兄，与鬼对质。初讯时，殊不服；回首见女子，大骇，遂吐情实。乃置之法，一郡哗以为神。公作《田烈妇碑记》以旌之。时泰安赵相国国麟为巡抚，责徐公，为此事作访闻足矣，何必托鬼神以自奇。徐公深以为愧。然其事颇实，不能秘也。

    Explanation

    26

    Those who know said, "This is a sign that the yang is not containing the yin, will there be fire?" Later, it turned out to be true.

    ○ The loyal wife of Tian, Governor Xu Shilin of Jiangsu, was always upright and honest. When he served as the governor of Anqing, at dusk, he ascended the hall, and the moonlight was bright and clear. He saw a woman covering her head with a black scarf, her face and eyes above the shoulders indistinguishable, kneeling outside the ceremonial gate as if to complain of injustice. Governor Xu knew she was a ghost and ordered the officials to hold a tablet and shout, "Spirits of those with grievances, you may enter!" The woman gradually entered, knelt at the bottom of the steps, her voice hoarse like a child's, the officials could not see her, but only hear her voice. She said her surname was Tian, she was a widow who remained loyal to her husband, and her husband's brother, Fang De, forced her into a marriage to gain her property, leading to her suicide. Governor Xu arrested her husband's brother and had the ghost testify against him. Initially, the brother did not admit to the charges; when he turned his head and saw the woman, he was greatly frightened and then confessed the truth. The brother was punished according to the law, and the entire county was amazed and thought it was miraculous. Governor Xu wrote a stele inscription to honor her. At that time, Governor Zhao Guolin of Sichuan criticized Governor Xu, saying that writing an inquiry about this matter was sufficient, why rely on ghosts and spirits to make oneself seem extraordinary? Governor Xu deeply felt ashamed. However, the story was quite true and could not be kept secret.

    27

    徐公未遇时，往京师，路上有同行客忽称背痛，跪地叩首曰：“我响马贼也，利公之财，将手剑公。忽有金甲神以捶击我，遂仆于地。公日后非凡人也。”言毕死。

    　　○鬼着衣受网庐州府舒城县乡民陈姓者妻，忽为一女鬼所凭，或扼其喉，或缚其颈，旁人不能见，妇甚苦之。时将手抓领内，多出麻草绳索。夫授以桃枝一束，曰：“来即击之。”鬼怒，闹更甚。夫无可奈何，乃入城求叶道士，赠以二十金，延之家中，设坛作法。布八卦阵于四方，中置小瓶；以五色纸剪成女衣十数件，置瓶侧。

    　　道士披发持咒。漏三下，妇人曰：“鬼来矣，手持猪肉。”夫以桃枝迎击之，果空中坠肉数块。道士告妇人曰：“如彼肯穿我纸衣，便好拿矣。”少顷，鬼果取衣。妇故意喝曰：“不许窃衣。”鬼笑曰：“这样华服，理该我着。”乃尽服之。

    Explanation

    27

    Xu Gong, before achieving success, traveled to the capital city. On the road, a fellow traveler suddenly claimed to have back pain, kneeling and bowing his head to say, "I am a highway robber, seeking your wealth, intending to stab you with a sword. Suddenly, a golden-armored god struck me with a mace, and I fell to the ground. You will not be an ordinary person in the future." After saying this, he died.

    ○ A Woman Possessed by a Ghost and Captured by Paper Clothes

    A woman from a village in Shucheng County, Luzhou Prefecture, was suddenly possessed by a female ghost. The ghost sometimes choked her throat and sometimes bound her neck, but others could not see it. The woman suffered greatly from it. At times, she would grab her collar, and often produced hemp rope. Her husband gave her a bundle of peach branches and said, "If it comes, strike it." The ghost became angry, and the disturbance grew worse. The husband could do nothing, so he went into the city to seek out Master Ye, a Taoist priest. He gave him twenty gold coins and invited him to their home, where he set up an altar and performed rituals. He arranged an Eight Trigrams formation in all directions, placed a small bottle in the center, and cut ten or more pieces of women's clothing from colored paper, placing them beside the bottle.

    The Taoist priest untied his hair and chanted incantations. At the third stroke of the night bell, the woman said, "The ghost has arrived, holding a piece of pork." Her husband struck it with the peach branches, and indeed, several pieces of meat fell from the air. The Taoist priest told the woman, "If it agrees to wear my paper clothes, it will be easy to catch it." A short while later, the ghost indeed took the clothes. The woman deliberately shouted, "Do not steal my clothes." The ghost laughed and said, "Such fine clothing should be worn by me." And she put them all on.

    28

    衣化为网，重重包裹，始宽后紧，遂不能出其阵中。道士书符作咒，以法水一杯当头打去，水泼而杯不破。鬼在东，杯击之于东；鬼在西，杯击之于西。杯碎，而鬼头亦裂矣。随即擒纳瓶内，封以法印五色纸，埋桃树下。复以二符入绛香末，搓为二团，付妇人曰：“此鬼亦有丈夫，半月内必来复仇，以此击之，可无患矣。”

    　　越数日，果有男鬼狰狞而来。妇如其法，鬼乃逃去。

    　　○阿龙苏州徐世球，居木渎，幼入城中，读书于韩其武家。朝有仆曰阿龙，年二十侍书室颇勤。一夕，徐读书楼上，命阿龙下取茶。少顷，阿龙失色而至，曰：“某见一白衣人在楼下狂走，呼之不应，殆鬼耶？”徐笑而不信。次夕，阿龙不敢上楼，徐命柳姓者代其职。至二更，柳下取茶，足有所触，遂仆地，视之，阿龙死于阶下。柳大呼，徐与韩氏诸宾客共来审视，见阿龙颈下有手搦痕，青黑如柳叶大，耳目口鼻尽塞黄泥，尸横而气未绝。

    Explanation

    28

    A Man Transformed into a Net

    The clothing transformed into a net, wrapping him tightly, first loosely and then tightly, so that he could not escape from its array. The Taoist priest wrote a talisman, chanted a spell, and threw a cup of magical water at the ghost's head. The water splashed, but the cup did not break. When the ghost was in the east, the cup struck it in the east; when the ghost was in the west, the cup struck it in the west. The cup shattered, and the ghost's head was also torn apart. Immediately, the ghost was captured and sealed in a bottle with a talisman and five-colored paper, then buried under a peach tree. The Taoist priest then mixed two talismans into red incense powder, formed them into two balls, and gave them to the woman, saying, "This ghost also has a husband. Within a month, he will surely come to seek revenge. Use these to strike him, and you will be free from harm."

    A few days later, a male ghost indeed appeared, looking fierce and menacing. The woman followed the instructions, and the ghost fled.

    ○ Arong

    In Suzhou, there was a man named Xu Shiqiu who lived in Mudu. When he was young, he entered the city to study at the home of Han Qiwu. In the morning, there was a servant named Arong, who was twenty years old and served in the study diligently. One evening, while Xu was reading on the upper floor, he ordered Arong to go downstairs to fetch tea. Shortly after, Arong came back with a pale face, saying, "I saw a white-clad figure running wildly downstairs. I called out to it, but it did not respond. It must be a ghost!" Xu laughed and did not believe him. The next evening, Arong was too frightened to go upstairs, so Xu ordered someone surnamed Liu to take his place. At the second watch of the night, Liu went downstairs to fetch tea but stepped on something and fell to the ground. When he looked up, Arong was dead at the foot of the stairs. Liu shouted loudly, and Xu, along with Han Qiwu and his guests, came to examine the scene. They saw that Arong had hand marks on his neck, which were dark blue and as large as willow leaves. His ears, eyes, mouth, and nose were all clogged with yellow mud. His body lay motionless, but he was not yet dead.

    29

    饮以姜汁，乃苏，曰：“吾下阶时，昨白衣者当头立，年可四十馀，短髯黑面，向我张嘴，伸其舌，长尺许。吾欲叫喊，遂为所击，以手夹我喉。旁有一老者，白须高冠，劝曰：”渠年少，未可欺侮。‘我尔时几欲气绝，适柳某撞我脚上，白衣者冲屋去矣。“徐命众人扶之登床，床上鬼灯数十，如极大萤火，彻夜不绝。次日，阿龙痴迷不食，韩氏召女巫胗之。巫曰：”取县官堂上朱笔，在病者心上书一’正‘字，颈上书一’刀‘字，两手书两’火‘字，便可救也。“韩氏如其言。书至左手”火“字，阿龙张目大叫曰：”勿烧我！我即去可也。“自此怪遂绝。阿龙至今犹存。

    Explanation

    29

    He was fed ginger juice, and then he revived, saying, "When I was descending the steps, the white-clad person from yesterday stood in front of me. He was about forty years old, with a short beard and a dark face, opened his mouth towards me, extended his tongue, which was about a foot long. I wanted to shout, but was then struck, and he grabbed my throat with his hand. Beside me, there was an elderly man with a white beard and a tall hat, who advised, 'He is young, and should not be bullied.' At that time, I almost suffocated, just as Mr. Liu accidentally bumped into my foot, and the white-clad person rushed out of the room. Gently, Mrs. Han ordered everyone to help him onto the bed, where dozens of ghost lamps, like large fireflies, appeared all night without stopping. The next day, Arong was dazed and refused to eat. Mrs. Han summoned a female shaman to diagnose him. The shaman said, 'Take the red pen from the county official's hall, and write a '正' character on the patient's heart, a '刀' character on his neck, and two '火' characters on both hands, and he will be saved.' Mrs. Han followed her instructions. When she wrote the '火' character on his left hand, Arong opened his eyes and shouted, 'Do not burn me! I will leave immediately.' From then on, the strange occurrences ceased, and Arong is still alive today."

    30

    ○大乐上人洛阳水陆庵僧，号大乐上人，饶于财。其邻人周其充县役，家贫，承催税租，皆侵蚀之。每逢比期，辄向上人借贷，数年间，积至七两。上人知其无力偿还，不复取索。役颇感恩，相见必曰：“吾不能报上人恩，死当为驴马以报。”居无何，晚，有人叩门，甚急。问为谁，应声曰：“周某也，来报恩耳。”上人启户，了不见人，以为有相戏者。是夜，所畜驴产一驹。明旦访役，果死。上人至驴旁，产驹奋首翘足，若相识者。

    Explanation

    30

    A Monk Named Dàlè Shàngrén

    Dàlè Shàngrén was a monk residing at Shuǐlù Àn in Luoyang, who was wealthy. His neighbor, Zhou Qichong, was a county official but was poor. He received and collected taxes but embezzled them all. Whenever the deadline for payment arrived, he would always borrow money from the monk, and over several years, the debt accumulated to seven taels. Knowing that Zhou was unable to repay, the monk did not pursue repayment. The official was very grateful and would always say when they met, "I cannot repay the monk's kindness, but I will repay as a donkey or horse after death." Soon after, one evening, someone knocked on the door urgently. When asked who it was, the person responded, "It's Zhou, here to repay a debt of gratitude." The monk opened the door but saw no one, thinking someone was playing a trick. That night, the donkey he kept gave birth to a foal. The next morning, when he visited the official, he indeed found him dead. The monk went to the side of the donkey, and the foal raised its head and lifted its hooves, as if recognizing him.

    31

    上人乘之一年。有山西客来宿，爱其驹，求买之。上人弗许，不忍明言其故。

    　　客曰：“然则借我骑往某县一宿，可乎？”上人许之。客上鞍揽辔，笑曰：“吾诈和尚耳。我爱此驴，骑之未必即返。我已措价置汝几上，可归取之。”不顾而驰。上人无可奈何，入房视之，几上白金七两，如其所负之数。

    　　○山西王二熊翰林涤斋先生为余言：康熙年间游京师，与陈参政议、计副宪某饮报国寺。

    　　三人俱早贵，喜繁华，以席间不得声妓为怅，遣人召女巫某唱秧歌劝酒。女巫唱终，半席腹胀，将溲焉，出至墙下。少顷返，则两目瞪视，跪三人前呼曰：“我山西王二也，某年月日为店王赵三谋财杀死，埋骨于此寺之墙下。求三长官代为伸冤。”三人相顾大骇，莫敢发声。熊晓之曰：“此司坊官事，非我辈所能主张。”

    Explanation

    31

    The monk had been using the donkey for a year. One day, a guest from Shanxi came to stay and admired the donkey, asking to buy it. The monk refused, unable to openly state the reason.

    The guest then said, "Then may I borrow it to ride to a certain county for a night, is that possible?" The monk agreed. The guest put on the saddle and took the reins, smiling and saying, "I was just joking with you, monk. I love this donkey, and if I ride it, I might not return. I have already set the price and placed it on your table; you can take it back." Without looking back, he rode away. The monk was helpless and went into the room to see, finding seven taels of white gold (silver) on the table, exactly the amount he owed.

    ○Wang Er Xiong, a scholar-official from Shanxi known as Xiong Dizhai, told me: During the Kangxi era, he traveled to the capital and dined at Baoguo Temple with Chen, a provincial governor, and Ji, a deputy governor.

    All three had risen to high positions early in life and enjoyed luxury. They were displeased that there were no singing girls at the banquet and sent someone to summon a female shaman to sing and dance to encourage the drinking. After the shaman finished singing, one of the guests felt bloated and went to the wall to relieve himself. Shortly after returning, he had wide-eyed and knelt before the three, shouting, "I am Wang Er from Shanxi. On a certain day, I was killed by storekeeper Zhao San for money and buried beneath the wall of this temple. I ask the three officials to seek justice for me." The three looked at each other in shock and dared not speak. Xiong explained to them, "This is a matter for the local magistrate; it is not something we can decide."

    32

    女巫曰：“现任司坊官俞公与熊爷有交，但求熊爷转请俞公到此掘验足也。”熊曰：“此事重大，空言无信，如何可行？”巫曰：“论理某当自陈，但某形质朽烂，须附生人而言，诸位老爷替我筹之。”言毕，女巫仆地。良久醒，。问之，茫然无知。三公谋曰：“我辈何能替鬼诉冤？诉亦不信。明日盍请俞司坊官共饮此处，召女巫质之，则冤白矣。”

    　　次日，招俞司坊至寺饮，告之故。召女巫，巫大惧，不肯复来。司坊官遣役拘之，巫始至。既入寺门，言状悉如昨日。司坊官启巡城御史，发掘墙下，得白骨一具，颈下有伤。询之土人，云：“从前此墙系山东济南府赵三安歇客寓之所，某年卷店逃归山东。”乃移文专差关提至济南，果有其人。文到之日，赵三一叫而绝。

    Explanation

    32

    The female witch said, "The current prison official, Mr. Yu, has a connection with Mr. Xiong. I merely ask Mr. Xiong to ask Mr. Yu to come here for an excavation and inspection." Mr. Xiong replied, "This matter is serious; mere words are not reliable. How can it be done?" The witch said, "Logically, I should speak for myself, but due to my deteriorated state, I must rely on a living person to convey my message. Please, the honorable gentlemen, help me with this." After speaking, the female witch collapsed to the ground. After a long time, she woke up. When asked, she was茫然无知 (máng rán wú zhī) (bewildered and ignorant). The three officials deliberated and said, "How can we, mere mortals, plead the case of a ghost? Even if we did, it would not be believed. Tomorrow, why don't we invite Mr. Yu, the prison official, to drink together here and summon the female witch to verify the complaint? Then the冤 (yuan) (injustice) will be cleared."

    The next day, Mr. Yu, the prison official, was invited to the temple to drink, and the reason was explained to him. The female witch was summoned, but she was greatly frightened and refused to come again. The prison official sent someone to detain her, and the witch finally arrived. Upon entering the temple gate, she recounted the events exactly as she had the day before. The prison official then reported to the city patrol magistrate, who ordered the excavation of the wall. A white bone was found, with an injury on the neck. When asked the locals, they said, "Previously, this wall was the lodging place of Zhao San, an innkeeper from Jinan, Shandong. In a certain year, he fled back to Shandong after closing his shop." A special envoy was then sent to Jinan to bring Zhao San to court. When the document arrived, Zhao San called out and died.

    33

    ○大福未享苏州罗姓者，年二十馀，元旦梦其亡祖谓曰：“汝于十月某日将死，万不能免，可速理后事。”醒后语其家人，群惊怖焉。至期，众家人环而视之，罗无他恙，至暮如故。家人以为梦不足信。二更后，罗溲于墙，久而不返。家人急往视，衣离其身矣。取灯照之，裸死于墙东，去衣服十馀步；心口尚温，不敢遽殓。

    　　次夜苏，告家人曰：“冤业耳。我奸妻婢小春，有胎不认，致妻拷掠而亡。

    　　渠诉冥司，亲来拘我。适我至墙，渠以手剥我衣，如我曩时淫彼之状。我昏迷不省，遂同至阴司城隍衙门。正欲讯鞫，适渠亦以前生别事发觉，为山西城隍所拘。

    　　阴官不肯久系狱囚，故仍令还阳。恐终不免也。“罗父问曰：”尔亦问阳间事乎？“

    Explanation

    33

    A Man from Suzhou, Unfulfilled in His Fortune

    A man from Suzhou, whose surname was Luo, was in his twenties. On the first day of the lunar new year, he dreamed that his deceased ancestor told him, "You will die on a certain day in October, and there is no way to escape it. You must quickly prepare the after-death affairs." Upon waking, he informed his family, who were all terrified. When the day arrived, the family gathered around him, but Luo seemed fine, and by evening, he was as usual. The family thought the dream was not to be trusted. After the second watch of the night, Luo urinated on the wall and did not return for a long time. The family rushed to check on him and found that his clothes had fallen off his body. They lit a lamp to see, and they discovered him naked and dead, lying about ten steps east of the wall; his chest was still warm, so they dared not hastily bury him.

    The next night, Luo woke up and told his family, "It is a matter of karmic retribution. I committed adultery with my wife's maid, Xiaochun, and when she became pregnant, I did not acknowledge it, which led to my wife being beaten to death. She appealed to the underworld, and they personally came to arrest me. When I reached the wall, they used their hands to strip me, just as I had done to her in the past. I was in a state of confusion and did not understand what was happening, so they took me to the office of the city god in the underworld. They were about to interrogate me when it happened that she too had been discovered for a different past life transgression and was being held by the city god of Shanxi. The officials in the underworld did not want to keep prisoners for too long, so they allowed me to return to the living. But I fear I cannot escape this fate in the end." Luo's father asked, "Did you also inquire about matters in the mortal world?"

    34

    曰：“我自知死不可逭，恐老父无养，故问管我之隶：”吾父异日何如？‘隶笑曰：“念汝孝心，尔父大福未享。’”家人闻之，皆为老翁喜，翁亦窃自负。

    　　未逾月，罗父竟以臌胀亡，腹大如匏，始知“大福”者，大腹之应。其子又隔三年乃死。

    　　○观音堂余同官赵公讳天爵者，自言为句容令时，下乡验尸。薄暮，宿古庙。梦老妪，面有积尘，发脱左鬓，立而请曰：“万蓝扼我咽喉，公为有司，须速救我。”赵惊醒张目，灯前隐隐犹有所见。急起逐之，了无所得。

    　　次早闲步，见庙侧有观音堂，旁塑一老妇，宛如梦中人。堂前沟巷狭甚，为民房出入之所。呼庙僧问曰：“汝里中得毋有万蓝乎？”僧曰：“在观音堂前出入者，即万蓝家也。”唤蓝至，问：“尔屋祖遗乎？”曰：“非也。此屋本从前观音堂大门出入之地，今年正月，寺僧盗售于我，价二十金。”赵亦不告以梦，即二十金为赎还基址，加修葺焉。

    Explanation

    34

    I said:
    曰：“我自知死不可逭，恐老父无养，故问管我之隶：”吾父异日何如？‘隶笑曰：“念汝孝心，尔父大福未享。’”家人闻之，皆为老翁喜，翁亦窃自负。

    　　未逾月，罗父竟以臌胀亡，腹大如匏，始知“大福”者，大腹之应。其子又隔三年乃死。

    　　○观音堂余同官赵公讳天爵者，自言为句容令时，下乡验尸。薄暮，宿古庙。梦老妪，面有积尘，发脱左鬓，立而请曰：“万蓝扼我咽喉，公为有司，须速救我。”赵惊醒张目，灯前隐隐犹有所见。急起逐之，了无所得。

    　　次早闲步，见庙侧有观音堂，旁塑一老妇，宛如梦中人。堂前沟巷狭甚，为民房出入之所。呼庙僧问曰：“汝里中得毋有万蓝乎？”僧曰：“在观音堂前出入者，即万蓝家也。”唤蓝至，问：“尔屋祖遗乎？”曰：“非也。此屋本从前观音堂大门出入之地，今年正月，寺僧盗售于我，价二十金。”赵亦不告以梦，即二十金为赎还基址，加修葺焉。

    Translation:
    He said, "I know that death is inevitable for me, but I fear that my old father will not be cared for, so I asked the servant in charge of me, 'How will my father be in the future?' The servant laughed and said, 'Consider your filial piety; your father has not yet enjoyed great fortune.'" The family heard this and all rejoiced for the old man, who also secretly felt proud.

    Not even a month later, Luo's father died of ascites, his belly as big as a gourd. It was then that they realized "great fortune" was the response to a big belly. His son died three years later.

    I, the same official as Zhao Tianjue, who was known as Zhao Gong, in the Guanyin Hall, said that when he was the magistrate of Jurong, he went to the countryside to examine a body. At dusk, he stayed in an ancient temple. He dreamed of an old woman with dust on her face and hair falling from her left temple, who stood and asked, "Wan Lan is choking my throat. You, as a government official, must save me quickly." Zhao woke up with wide eyes, and in the dim light, he still seemed to see something. He hurriedly got up to chase it, but found nothing.

    The next morning, he took a leisurely walk and saw a Guanyin Hall beside the temple. There was a statue of an old woman beside it, who looked just like the one in his dream. The alley in front of the hall was very narrow, serving as an entrance and exit for the houses. He called the temple monk and asked, "Is there no Wan Lan in your village?" The monk replied, "Those who come and go in front of the Guanyin Hall are the Wan Lan family." He called Wan Lan and asked, "Is your house inherited from your ancestors?" Wan Lan replied, "No. This house was originally the entrance and exit of the Guanyin Hall's main gate. Last January, the temple monk sold it to me for twenty gold pieces." Zhao did not tell him about the dream, but paid twenty gold pieces to redeem the land and repair it.

    35

    是时，赵年四十馀，尚无嗣。数月后，夫人有身。将产之夕，梦老妪复来，抱一儿与之。夫人觉，梦亦如公，遂产一儿。

    　　○常格诉冤乾隆十六年八月初三日，阅邸抄。见景山遗失陈设古玩数件，内务府官疑挑土工人所窃，召执役者数十人，立而讯之。一人忽跪诉曰：“我常格也，系正黄旗人，年十二岁。赴市买物，为工人赵二图奸不遂，将刀杀死，埋我于厚载门外堆炭地方。我家父母某，尚未知也。求大人掘验伸冤。”言毕仆地。少顷，复跃而起曰：“我即赵二，杀常格者我也。”内务府大人见其状，知有冤，移交刑部掘验，尸伤宛然。访其父母，曰：“我家儿遗失已一月，尚未知其死也。”随拘询赵二，尽吐情实。刑部奏：“赵二自吐凶情，迹似自首，例宜减等；但为冤鬼所凭，不便援引此例，拟斩立决。”奉旨依议。

    Explanation

    35

    At that time, Zhao was over forty years old and still had no heir. Several months later, his wife became pregnant. On the night before she was to give birth, she dreamed of an old woman coming again, holding a child and giving it to her. When the wife woke up, she had the same dream as her husband, and she then gave birth to a son.

    ○ Changge's Appeal for Justice

    On the third day of the eighth month in the sixteenth year of the Qianlong era, reading the official gazette, it was seen that several antique decorations had been lost at Jing Mountain. The officials of the Imperial Household Department suspected that the workers digging soil had stolen them, so they summoned dozens of laborers and interrogated them while they were standing. One person suddenly knelt and said, "I am Changge, a member of the Plain Yellow Banner, aged twelve. I went to the market to buy things, and was about to be raped by worker Zhao Er but failed, so he killed me with a knife and buried me outside the Thick Bearing Gate in a pile of coal. My parents, whose names I will not mention, do not yet know. I beg you to dig and verify my innocence." After speaking, he fell to the ground. A short while later, he jumped up and said, "I am Zhao Er, the one who killed Changge." The official of the Imperial Household Department saw his state and knew there was injustice, so he transferred the case to the Ministry of Punishment for digging and verification. The injuries on the body were clearly visible. Visiting his parents, they said, "Our son has been missing for a month, and we do not yet know he is dead." They then summoned Zhao Er and questioned him, and he fully confessed the truth. The Ministry of Punishment reported, "Zhao Er confessed the crime himself, which seems like self-reporting, and according to the law, he should receive a reduced sentence; but because he was possessed by a vengeful spirit, it is inconvenient to apply this rule, so we propose immediate execution." The imperial decree followed the advice.

    36

    ○蒲州盐枭岳水轩过山西蒲州盐池，见关神祠内塑张桓侯像，与关面南坐。旁有周将军像，怒目狰狞，手拖铁练，锁朽木一枝，不解何故。土人指而言曰：“此盐枭也。”

    　　问其故，曰：“宋元间，取盐池之水，熬煎数日，而盐不成。商民惶惑，祷于庙。梦关神召众人谓曰：”汝盐池为蚩尤所据，故烧不成盐。我享血食，自宜料理。但蚩尤之魄，吾能制之；其妻名枭者，悍恶尤甚，我不能制，须吾弟张翼德来，始能擒服。吾已遣人自益州召之矣。‘众人惊寤。旦，即在庙中添塑桓侯像。

    　　其夕风雷大作，朽木一根，已在铁练之上。次日，取水煮盐，成者十倍。“始悟今所称”盐枭“，实始于此。

    　　○灵壁女借尸还魂王砚庭知灵璧县事。村中有农妇李氏，年三十许，貌丑而瞽，病臌胀十馀年，腹大如豕。一夕卒，夫入城买棺。棺到，将殓，妇已生矣，双目尽明，腹亦平复。

    Explanation

    36

    A Salt Smuggler in Puzhou

    Yue Shuixuan, a salt smuggler from Puzhou, passed by the salt pools of Puzhou in Shanxi. He saw a statue of Zhang Fei, the Marquis of Huan, inside the temple dedicated to Guan Yu, seated facing south. Beside it was a statue of Zhou Cang, the General Zhou, with a fierce and terrifying expression, holding a chain in hand and locking a piece of rotten wood. He could not understand why this was so. A local person pointed and said, "This is the salt smuggler."

    When asked about the reason, he replied, "During the Yuan祐 period, people took water from the salt pools and boiled it for several days, but the salt did not form. Merchants and civilians were confused and anxious, and they prayed at the temple. In a dream, Guan Yu appeared and called out to the crowd, saying, 'Your salt pool is occupied by Chi You, that is why you cannot make salt. I enjoy blood sacrifices, so I should handle it myself. But Chi You's spirit, I can control; his wife, named Xiāo, is especially fierce and evil, and I cannot control her. I need my brother Zhang Yide to come, to finally capture and subdue her. I have already sent someone to call him from Yizhou.' The crowd woke up in shock. In the morning, they immediately added a statue of Zhang Fei to the temple. That night, there was a great storm with thunder, and a piece of rotten wood was already on the chain. The next day, when they took water to boil salt, the yield was ten times more." They finally realized that the term 'salt smuggler' originates from this, and it really started from this.

    A Woman Who Borrowed a Corpse to Return to Life

    Wang Yanting, the magistrate of Lingbi County, heard of a story in a village. There was a farmer's wife named Li, about thirty years old, who was ugly and blind, suffering from severe ascites for over ten years, with a belly as large as a pig. One night, she died. Her husband went into town to buy a coffin. When the coffin arrived and they were about to bury her, the woman had already given birth. Her eyes were completely clear, and her belly had also returned to normal.

    37

    夫喜，近之。妻坚拒，泣曰：“吾某村中王姑娘也，尚未婚嫁，何为至此？吾之父母姊妹，俱在何处？”其夫大骇，急告某村，则举家哭其幼女，尸已埋矣。其父母狂奔而至。妇一见泣抱，历叙生平，事皆符合。其未婚之家亦来视，妇犹羞涩，赤见于面。遂两家争此妇，鸣于官。砚庭为之作合，断归村农。乾隆二十一年事。

    　　○汉高祖弑义帝山东驿盐道卢宪观暴卒，已而复苏，云前身本九江王英布也。弑义帝，乃高祖使之，非项羽所使也。高祖阴弑义帝，嫁名项羽，而伪与诸侯讨弑义帝者。羽讼于上帝，须布为质。质明，果系高祖所弑。陈平六出奇计，此其一也。故卢死而复苏。问：“何以迟二千年而谳始定？”曰：“羽以坑咸阳卒二十万，上帝震怒，戮于阴山受无量罪。今始满贯，方得诉冤。”

    Explanation

    37

    A man was overjoyed, and the event occurred nearby. His wife firmly refused and cried, saying, "I am Miss Wang from a certain village, not yet married, why am I here? Where are my parents and sisters all?" Her husband was greatly frightened and hurriedly informed the village. Then the whole family cried for their young daughter, and the body had already been buried. Her parents rushed wildly and arrived. The woman embraced and cried upon seeing her, and recounted her life in detail. All the facts matched. Her family who had not yet married her also came to look at her. The woman was still shy, and her face turned red. Then the two families disputed over this woman and appealed to the authorities. Judge Yan Ting arranged the marriage and ruled that she should go to the village farmer. The matter occurred in the 21st year of the Qianlong era.

    ○ During the reign of Emperor Gaozu, the Salt Commissioner of Shandong, Lu Xianguan, suddenly died. He then revived and claimed that his former life was that of Ying Bu, the King of Jiangzhou. The assassination of Emperor Yi was ordered by Emperor Gaozu, not by Xiang Yu. Emperor Gaozu secretly had Emperor Yi assassinated, and blamed it on Xiang Yu, pretending to join the feudal lords in punishing those who assassinated Emperor Yi. Xiang Yu appealed to the Heavenly Emperor, demanding Ying Bu as a hostage. At dawn, it was confirmed that Emperor Yi was indeed assassinated by Emperor Gaozu. This was one of the six ingenious strategies devised by Chen Ping. Thus, Lu Xianguan died and then revived. Asked, "Why did it take two thousand years for the verdict to be settled?" He replied, "Xiang Yu buried 200,000 soldiers from Xianyang, and the Heavenly Emperor was furious, punishing him in the Yin Mountains with countless crimes. Now his term has ended, and he can finally appeal his innocence."

    38

    按王阮亭《池北偶谈》载张巡妾报冤事，亦迟至千年。盖张以忠节故，而报复难；项以惨戮故，而申诉亦难也。

    　　○地穷宫保定督标守备李昌明暴卒，三日，尸不寒，家人未敢棺殓。忽尸腹胀大如鼓，一溺而苏，握送殓者手曰：“我将死时，苦楚异甚，自脚趾至于肩领，气散出，不可收。既死，觉身体轻倩，颇佳于生时。所到处，天色深黄，无日色，飞沙茫茫。足不履地，一切屋舍人物，都无所见。我神魂飘忽，随风东南行。许久，天色渐明，沙少止。俯视东北角，有长河一条，河内牧羊者三人；羊白色，肥大如马。我问：”家安在？‘牧羊人不答。又走约数十里，见远处隐隐宫殿，瓦皆黄琉璃，如帝王居。近前，有二人靴帽袍带立殿下，如世上所演高力士、童贯形状。

    Explanation

    38

    According to Wang Ruanting's Pool North Oddities, there is a story about Zhang Xun's concubine seeking revenge, which was delayed until a thousand years later. Zhang was loyal and righteous, but it was difficult to avenge him; Xiang Yu was cruelly killed, and it was also difficult to appeal for justice.

    ○ Li Changming, the military supervisor of the Baoding garrison, died suddenly. Three days later, his body did not cool, and the family dared not coffin and wrap it. Suddenly, the corpse's abdomen swelled like a drum, and after urinating once, he revived. He grasped the hands of those preparing the coffin and said, "When I was about to die, I suffered greatly, from my toes to my collar, the breath scattered and could not be contained. After death, I felt my body was light and pleasant, better than in life. Wherever I went, the sky was deep yellow, without sunlight, and the flying sand was vast. My feet did not touch the ground, and I could not see any houses, people, or things. My spirit and soul drifted, following the wind southeast. After a long time, the sky gradually brightened, and the flying sand decreased.
