He saw four people descend from the stairs and stand before the lamp: one wearing a black hat and a red robe, another wearing a square headband and a Taoist robe, and the other two wearing warm hats and fur coats. They sighed together. Shortly after, five women dressed in traditional attire came and stood before the lamp, weeping. An elderly woman pointed to the groom in the bed and asked, "Can you help us?" The man in the black hat and red robe shook his head and said, "It is no use." He also wept and said, "I will seek help from Mr. Zhang to save our family's last hope." They comforted each other, sitting or walking around. The groom was terrified and could not make a sound. By the fifth watch, they helped each other up the stairs. Suddenly, a man with a dark face emerged from under the table and hurriedly climbed the stairs, pulling the man in the red robe and saying, "Can't you save me too?" The man in the red robe nodded. By then, the rooster had already crowed, and the man with the dark face rushed back under the table. The groom waited until the window began to lighten, put on his clothes, and entered the room, asking who lived upstairs. They replied, "The statues of our ancestors are being set up for worship, and no one lives there." The groom went upstairs to see the statues, but their clothing and appearance were different from what he had seen. He did not understand why and kept it a secret.

    159

    先是，徐家三子皆受业于张有虔先生，是年，张馆松江。五月中，以母病归，乞其弟子往权馆。徐故富家，皆不欲出。张强之，主人命第三子往。有阿寿者，奴产子也，向事张谨，因命同往。主仆出门，未二十日，杭州虾蟆瘟大作。徐一家上下十二口，死者十人，惟第三子与阿寿以外出故免。闻丧，归。婿以所见语之，徐愕然曰：“阿寿之父名阿黑，以面黑故也，君所见从桌下出者是矣。”

    　　○蒋文恪公说二事余座主蒋文恪公，居李广桥赐第。自言：少时读书平台，其地与他屋隔远，每夜坐呼人，辄有应声而无人至。一夜欲溲，窗外月不甚明，又无相伴者，乃呼其所随僮名，应声答。令之入，卒不入。启户出，见一人方枕外墙门阈，以头向内而应。公初疑为某僮醉，骂之，其卧如故。公怒，行至阈连，思扑之，见所卧人长三尺，方巾皂衣，白须，如世所塑土地样。公喝之，其人冉冉没矣。

    Explanation

    159

    Previously, the three sons of the Xu family had all received instruction from Mr. Zhang Youqian. That year, Zhang took up a teaching post in Songjiang. In mid-May, due to his mother's illness, he returned home and asked his disciples to temporarily take over his teaching post. The Xu family, being wealthy, were all reluctant to go. Zhang insisted, and the owner ordered the third son to go. There was Aishou, a son born to a slave, who had always been diligent in serving Zhang, and so was also sent along. The master and servant left, and within twenty days, a plague known as the "Frog Plague" broke out in Hangzhou. Twelve members of the Xu family died, leaving only the third son and Aishou, who had escaped death by being away from home. Upon hearing of the death, they returned. The husband recounted what he had seen, and Xu was startled, saying, "Aishou's father was named Aihēi, because his face was dark. The person you saw emerging from under the table is him."

    ○ Two Stories Told by Mr. Jiang Wenke My patron, Mr. Jiang Wenke, lived in the imperial residence granted by the emperor at Li Guang Bridge. He said: When young, he studied at a high platform, a place far from other houses. Every night, when he called out to someone, there was always a response, but no one appeared. One night, when he needed to urinate, the moon outside was not very bright, and there was no one to accompany him. He called out the name of his attendant, and there was a voice that answered. He ordered the attendant to come, but the attendant did not come. He opened the door and stepped out, and saw a person lying on the threshold of the outer wall, with his head facing inward and responding. At first, Jiang Wenke suspected that it was one of his attendants who was drunk and scolded him, but the person continued to lie there as before. Jiang Wenke became angry and walked to the threshold, intending to strike him. He saw that the person was only three feet tall, wearing a square cap and black robe, with white hair, resembling the image of a land deity. Jiang Wenke shouted at him, and the person slowly disappeared.

    160

    公父文肃公戒子孙不得近优人，故终文肃之世，从无演戏觞客之事。文肃殁后十年，文恪稍稍演戏，而不敢蓄养令人。老奴顾升乘文恪燕坐，谈及梨园，怂恿曰：“外间优人总不若家伶为佳，且便于传唤。家中奴产子甚众，何不延教师择数奴演之？”文恪心动，未答。忽见顾升惊怖，面色顿异，两手如受桎梏，身倒于地，以头钻入椅脚中，由一椅脚穿至第二椅脚，由第二椅脚穿至第三椅脚。

    　　自首至足，若纳于匣。呼之不应。公急召巫医，百计解救。夜半始苏，曰：“怕杀！怕杀！方前言毕时，见一长人ㄏ奴出，先老主人坐堂上，声色俱厉，曰：‘尔为吾家世仆，吾之遗训，尔岂不知！何得导五郎蓄戏子？着捆打四十，活掩棺中！’奴闷绝，不知所为。最后闻远远有呼唤声，奴在棺中，欲应不能。后稍觉清快，亦不知何以得出。”验其臀，果有青黑痕。

    Explanation

    160

    The Wenzu Gong, a respected ancestor, warned his descendants not to associate with entertainers, so throughout his lifetime, there was never any entertainment with guests through performance. Ten years after the death of the Wenzu Gong, the Wenke Gong began to entertain guests with performances but did not dare to keep performers. An old servant named Gu Sheng took advantage of Wenke Gong's leisure time and mentioned the entertainment world, urging him to say, "Performers outside are not as good as our own performers, and it is convenient to summon them. There are many children born to the household slaves; why not hire a teacher to train a few of them to perform?" Wenke Gong was moved by this suggestion but did not answer. Suddenly, he saw Gu Sheng terrified, his face suddenly changed, his hands as if bound, and he fell to the ground, trying to put his head into the chair leg, from one chair leg through to the second chair leg, and then to the third chair leg.

    From head to foot, it was as if he was being put into a box. They could not call him back. The master urgently summoned a witch and a doctor, who tried every means to save him. He woke up at midnight and said, "So scared! So scared! Just as I finished speaking, I saw a tall person pulling out a slave. The old master was sitting on the main seat, with a stern voice and expression, saying, 'You are a servant of our family for generations. My legacy and instructions, do you not know? How can you lead Wenke Gong to keep performers? You should be tied up and beaten forty times, and buried alive in a coffin!' The slave fainted and did not know what happened. Finally, he heard a distant calling sound. The slave was in the coffin, wanting to respond but could not. Later, he felt slightly better but did not know how he came out." They examined his buttocks and found indeed bruises in blue and black.

    161

    ○猎户除狐海昌元化镇，有富家，卧房三间在楼上。日间，人俱下楼理家务。一日其妇上楼取衣，楼门内闭，加橛焉。因思：家中人皆在下，谁为此者？板隙窥之，见男子坐于床，疑为偷儿，呼家人齐上。其人大声曰：“我当移家此楼。我先来，家眷行且至矣。假尔床桌一用，余物还汝。”自窗间掷其箧箱零星之物于地。少顷，闻楼上聚语声，三间房内，老幼杂沓，敲盘而唱曰：“主人翁！主人翁！千里客来，酒无一钟？”其家畏之，具酒四桌置庭中，其桌即凭空取上。食毕，复从空掷下。此后，亦不甚作恶。

    Explanation

    161

    In the town of Huayua, Haiyang, there was a wealthy household with three bedrooms on the upper floor. During the day, everyone would go downstairs to manage household affairs. One day, the wife went upstairs to fetch clothes, but found the door locked from the inside with a peg. She wondered who could have done this, as everyone was downstairs. Peering through a crack in the board, she saw a man sitting on the bed. Suspecting him to be a thief, she called her family to come upstairs. The man shouted, "I intend to move my family into this house. I arrived first, and my family will soon be here. I will borrow your bed and table for a while, and return the rest of the items to you." He threw his boxes and miscellaneous items out of the window onto the ground. Soon after, they heard voices gathering on the upper floor, with people of all ages talking in the three rooms. They began to knock on plates and sing, "Master! Master! A guest from afar has come, but there is no cup of wine?" The family was afraid and prepared four tables of wine, placing them in the courtyard. The tables were then taken up from nothingness. After eating, the tables were thrown back down from the air. After this, the man did not cause much trouble.

    162

    富家延道士为驱除，方在外定议归，楼上人又唱曰：“狗道，狗道，何人敢到！”明日，道士至，方布坛，若有物捶之；踉跄奔出，一切神像法器，皆撒门外。自此，日夜不宁。乃至江西求张天师，天师命法官某来。其怪又唱曰：“天师，天师，无法可施。法官，法官，来亦枉然。”俄而，法官至，若有人ㄏ其首而掷之，面破衣裂，法官大惭，曰：“此怪力量大，须请谢法官来才可。谢住长安，镇某观中。”主人迎谢来，立坛施法，怪竟不唱。富家喜甚。忽红光一道，有白须者从空中至楼，呼曰：“毋畏谢道士。谢所行法，我能破之！”谢坐厅前诵咒，掷钵于地，走如飞，周厅盘旋，欲飞上楼者屡矣，而终不得上。须臾，楼上摇铜铃，琅琅声响，钵遂委地，不复转动。谢惊曰：“吾力竭，不能除此怪。”

    Explanation

    162

    A wealthy family invited a Taoist priest to perform rituals to drive away evil spirits. Just as he was about to return after making arrangements outside, someone on the upper floor chanted, "Taoist priest, Taoist priest, who dares to come!" The next day, when the Taoist priest arrived and set up the altar, he was suddenly struck by something and stumbled out, leaving all the statues of deities and ritual objects scattered outside. From then on, the family experienced continuous unrest. They even went to Jiangxi to seek help from Master Zhang, who ordered a certain judge to come. The spirit again chanted, "Master Zhang, Master Zhang, there is no way to deal with it. Judge, Judge, your coming is also in vain." Soon after, the judge arrived, and as if someone hit him on the head and threw him, he was left with a broken face and torn clothes, greatly ashamed. He said, "This spirit has great power, and only Judge Xie can handle it. Judge Xie lives in Chang'an and resides in a certain temple." The host welcomed Judge Xie, set up the altar, and performed rituals, but the spirit finally did not chant. The wealthy family was very happy. Suddenly, a streak of red light appeared, and an old man with a white beard came from the sky to the upper floor, calling out, "Do not fear Judge Xie. Judge Xie's rituals, I can break them!" Judge Xie sat in front of the hall, recited incantations, and threw the bowl to the ground. He ran like flying, circling the hall, and tried to fly up to the upper floor many times but could not finally get up. In a moment, the copper bell on the upper floor was shaken, producing a clear ringing sound, and the bowl fell to the ground, no longer moving. Judge Xie was surprised and said, "My strength is exhausted, and I cannot eliminate this spirit."

    163

    即取钵走，而楼上欢呼之声彻墙外。自是，作祟无所不至。如是者又半年。

    　　冬暮大雪，有猎户十余人来借宿，其家告以“借宿不难，恐有扰累。”猎户曰：“此狐也，我辈猎狐者也，但求烧酒饮醉，当有以报君。”其家即沽酒具肴馔，彻内外燃巨烛。猎户轰饮，大醉，各出鸟枪，装火药，向空点放。烟尘障天，竟夕震动，迨天明雪止始去。其家方虑惊骇之当更作祟，乃竟夕悄然。又数日，了无所闻。上楼察之：则群毛委地，窗尽开，而其怪迁矣。

    Explanation

    163

    The bowl was immediately taken and run away with, while cheers from upstairs could be heard outside the wall. From then on, the mischievous spirit caused trouble without limit. This continued for another half a year.

    One late winter day, heavy snow fell, and more than ten hunters came to borrow a place to stay. The host said, "It's not difficult to provide lodging, but I fear it might cause disturbance or inconvenience." The hunters replied, "This is a fox, and we are fox hunters. We only ask for some strong liquor to get drunk, and we will have something to repay you." The host immediately bought wine and prepared food, and lit large candles throughout the inside and outside. The hunters drank heartily, getting very drunk, and each took out their bird guns, loaded them with gunpowder, and fired into the air. Smoke and dust obscured the sky, and the whole night was shaken. They left only after the next morning when the snow stopped.

    The host was initially concerned that the commotion might lead to even more mischief, but the night passed quietly. After several days, there was no further news. Upon checking upstairs, feathers and fur were found lying on the ground, and the windows were all opened. It seemed that the strange phenomenon had moved on.

    164

    ●卷五○城隍替人训妻杭州望仙桥周生，业儒，妇凶悍，数忤其姑。每岁逢佳节，着麻农拜姑于堂，诅其死也。周孝而懦，不能制妻，惟日具疏祷城隍神，愿殛妇以安母。章凡九焚，不应；乃更为忿语，责神无灵。

    　　是夕，梦一卒来，曰：“城隍召汝。”周随往，入跪庙中。城隍曰：“尔妇忤逆状吾岂不知，但查汝命，只一妻，无继妻，恰有子二人。尔孝子，胡可无后，故暂宽汝妇。汝何哓哓！”周曰：“妇恶如是，奈堂上何！且某与妇恩义既绝，又安得有嗣？”城隍曰：“尔昔何媒？”曰：“范、陈二姓。”乃命拘二人至，责曰：“某女不良，而汝为媒，嫁于孝子，害皆由汝。”呼杖之。二人不服，曰：“某无罪。女处闺中，其贤否某等无由知。

    Explanation

    164

    Volume 50: The City God's Intervention in a Wife's Discipline

    In Hangzhou, near the Wàngxiān Bridge, there lived a scholar named Zhou Sheng. His wife was fierce and domineering, repeatedly defying her mother-in-law. Every year during festivals, she would wear a coarse garment and kneel before her mother-in-law in the hall, cursing her to death. Zhou Sheng, though filial, was weak and unable to control his wife. He wrote daily petitions to the City God, praying that his wife be punished so that his mother could be at peace. He burned nine such petitions, but there was no response. Frustrated, he spoke angrily, blaming the City God for being ineffective.

    That night, Zhou Sheng dreamed of a soldier who said, "The City God has summoned you." Zhou followed the soldier to the temple, where he knelt before the City God. The City God said, "I am aware of your wife's rebellious behavior, but upon checking your fate, you have only one wife, no second wife, and two sons. You are a filial son, how can you have no descendants? Therefore, I am temporarily lenient with your wife. Why do you complain so much?" Zhou replied, "My wife is so wicked, what can I do for my mother? Besides, my relationship with my wife has ended, and how can I have any descendants?" The City God said, "Who was your matchmaker?" Zhou answered, "Fan and Chen families." The City God then ordered the two families to be brought before him and scolded them, saying, "This woman is bad, and you acted as her matchmaker, marrying her to a filial son. All the harm is due to you." He called for them to be beaten. The two matchmakers protested, saying, "We are innocent. The woman was in her闺房, and we had no way of knowing her virtue or lack thereof."

    165

    ”周亦代为祈免，曰：“二人不过要好作媒，非赚媒钱作诳语者，与伊何罪？据某愚见，妇人虽悍，未有不畏鬼神念经拜佛者。但求城隍神呼妇至，示之惩警，或得改逆为孝，事未可定。”城隍曰：“甚是。但尔辈皆善类，故以好面目相向，妇凶悍，非吾变相，不足以威。尔辈无恐。”命蓝面鬼持大锁往擒其妻，而以袍袖拂面。顷刻，变成青靛色，朱发睁眼。召两旁兵卒执刀锯者，皆狰狞凶猛。油铛肉磨，置列庭下。须臾，鬼牵妇至，觳觫跪阶前。城隍厉声数其罪状，取登注册示之。命夜叉：“拉下剥皮，放油锅中。”妇哀号伏罪，请后不敢。周及两媒代为之请，城隍曰：“念汝夫孝，姑宥汝，再犯者有如此刑。”乃各放归。

    Explanation

    165

    Zhou also interceded on their behalf to seek exemption, saying, "The two were merely matchmaking for good intentions, not deceiving for matchmaking fees by telling lies. What crime do they have? According to my humble opinion, a woman may be fierce, but there is no one who does not fear ghosts and gods and recite scriptures and worship Buddha. But I only ask the City God to summon the woman, to show her punishment and warning, perhaps she can change from disobedience to filial piety, the matter is uncertain." The City God said, "Very right. But you all are good people, so I show you a good face. The woman is fierce and violent, not my transformed appearance, is not enough to intimidate. You have nothing to fear." He ordered the Blue-Faced Ghost to carry a large lock to capture his wife, and used his robe sleeve to wipe his face. In an instant, he turned a deep blue-green, with red hair and wide-open eyes. He summoned the soldiers on both sides holding knives and saws, all of whom were fierce and terrifying. Oil pots and meat grinders were placed in rows in the courtyard. Soon, the ghost led the woman to the spot, trembling and kneeling at the steps. The City God sternly listed her crimes, took out the registration book and showed it to her. He ordered the Yaksha, "Pull her down and skin her, throw her into the oil pot." The woman wailed and confessed her guilt, promising not to do it again. Zhou and the two matchmakers interceded on her behalf. The City God said, "Considering your husband's filial piety, I will forgive you for now, if you commit the same crime again, you will face this punishment." Then they were all let go.

    166

    次日，夫妇证此梦皆同。妇自此善视其姑，后果生二子。

    　　○文信王湖州同征友沈炳震，尝昼寝书堂，梦青衣者引至一院，深竹蒙密，中设木床素几，几上镜高丈许。青衣曰：“公照前生。”沈自照：方巾朱履，非本朝衣冠矣。方错愕间，青衣曰：“公照三生。”沈又自照：则乌纱红袍，玉带皂靴，非儒者衣冠矣。

    　　有苍头闯然入跪叩头曰：“公犹识老奴乎？奴曾从公赴大同兵备道任者也，今二百馀年矣。”言毕，泣，手文卷一册献沈。沈问故，苍头曰：“公前生在明嘉靖间，姓王名秀，为大公兵备道。今日青衣召公，为地府文信王处有五百鬼诉冤，请公质问。老奴记杀此五百人，非公本意。起意者乃总兵某也。五百人，本刘七案内败卒，降后又反，故总兵杀之，以杜后患。公曾有手劝阻，总兵不从。

    Explanation

    166

    The next day, both husband and wife confirmed that they had the same dream. From then on, the wife treated her mother-in-law well, and indeed she gave birth to two sons.

    Master Wenxin Wang of Huzhou, who once sought friends named Shen Bingzhen, once slept in his study during the day and dreamed that a servant in plain clothes led him to a courtyard surrounded by a dense bamboo grove, with a wooden bed and a plain table set in the middle. On the table was a mirror about a zhang (about 3 meters) high. The servant said, "You are looking at your previous life." Shen looked at himself: he was wearing a scholar's cap and red shoes, not the official attire of the current dynasty. Just as he was startled, the servant said, "You are looking at your three lives." Shen looked again: he was wearing a black-gauze cap, a red robe, a jade belt, and black boots, not the attire of a scholar.

    A servant rushed in, knelt down, and bowed, saying, "Do you still remember your old servant? I once accompanied you to take up the post of military commissioner in Datong, more than two hundred years ago." After speaking, he wept and handed a document to Shen. Shen asked why he was crying. The servant said, "Your previous life was during the Jiajing period of the Ming dynasty. Your name was Wang Xiu, and you served as a military commissioner. Today, the servant summoned you because in the underworld governed by Master Wenxin Wang, five hundred ghosts are accusing you. You are questioning them. I remember that these five hundred people were originally defeated soldiers from the Liu Qi case. They surrendered but then rebelled again, so the general killed them to prevent future problems. You once tried to stop them, but the general did not listen."

    167

    老奴恐公忘记此书，难以辨雪，故袖此稿奉公。“沈亦恍然记前世事，与慰劳者再。

    　　青衣请曰：“公步行乎？乘轿乎？”老仆呵曰：“安有监司大员而步行者！”

    　　呼一舆，二夫甚华，掖沈行数里许。前有宫阙巍峨，中坐王者，冕旒白须；旁吏绛衣乌纱，持文簿呼：“兵备道王某进。”王曰：“且止，此总兵事也，先唤总兵。”有戎装金甲者从东厢入，沈视之，果某总兵，旧同官也。王与问答良久，语不可辨。随唤沈，沈至，揖王而立。王曰：“杀刘七党五百人，总兵业已承认，公有书劝止之，与公无干。然明朝法，总兵亦受兵备道节制。公令之不从，平日儒恧可知。”沈唯唯谢过。

    　　总兵争曰：“此五百人，非杀不可者也。曾诈降复反，不杀，则又将反。总兵为国杀之，非为私杀也。”言未已，阶下黑气如墨，声啾啾远来，血臭不可耐。

    167

    The old servant feared that you might forget this book, making it difficult to clear up the matter, so he kept this draft in his sleeve to present to you. "Shen also suddenly remembered past-life events and was comforted twice.

    The blue-clad servant asked, "Will you walk or take a sedan chair?" The old servant retorted, "How could a high-ranking official like a governor walk?"

    He called for a sedan chair, and two attendants were very splendid. They supported Shen and walked several miles. In front, there was a magnificent palace, and a king sat in the middle, wearing a crown and having a white beard; beside him, officials in red robes and black hats held documents and called out, "The Military Governor, Wang, please come forward." The king said, "Hold on, this is a matter concerning the general; call the general first." A soldier in military attire and golden armor entered from the east wing. Shen looked and saw that it was indeed the general, an old colleague. The king conversed with him for a long time, but the conversation was hard to understand. Then he called Shen. Shen came, bowed to the king, and stood. The king said, "The general has already admitted to killing five hundred people from the Liu Seven faction. You wrote a letter to try to stop him, which has nothing to do with you. However, according to the laws of the Ming Dynasty, the general is also subject to the jurisdiction of the military governor. Your command was not followed, and your usual scholarly shame is evident." Shen repeatedly apologized.

    The general argued, "These five hundred people must be killed. They once pretended to surrender but then rebelled again. If they are not killed, they will rebel again. The general killed them for the country, not for personal reasons." Before he finished speaking, a dark cloud like ink appeared downstairs, accompanied by distant chirping sounds, and the smell of blood was unbearable.

    168

    五百头拉杂如滚球，齐张口露牙，来啮总兵，兼睨沈。沈大惧，向王拜不已，且以袖中文书呈上。王拍案厉声曰：“断头奴！诈降复反事有之乎？”群鬼曰：“有之。”王曰：“然则总兵杀汝诚当，尚何哓哓！”群鬼曰：“当时诈降者，渠魁数人；复反者，亦渠魁数人；馀皆胁从者也。何可尽杀？且总兵意欲迎合嘉靖皇帝严刻之心，非真为国为民也。”王笑曰：“说总兵不为民可也，说总兵不为国不可也。”因谕五百鬼曰：“此事沉搁二百馀年，总为事属因公，阴官不能断。今总兵心迹未明，不能成神去；汝等怨气未散，又不能托生为人。我将以此事状上奏玉皇，听候处置。惟兵备道某所犯甚小，且有劝阻手书为据，可放还阳，他生罚作富家女子，以惩其柔懦之过。”五百鬼皆手持头叩阶，哒哒有声，曰：“惟大王命。”王命青衣者引沈出。

    Explanation

    168

    A chaotic mass of 500 heads, rolling like balls, opened their mouths and showed their teeth, attacking the general while also glaring at Shen. Shen was greatly frightened, and he continued to bow to the king without ceasing, presenting a document from his sleeve. The king struck the table and spoke sternly: "Head-chopper! Has there ever been a case of someone pretending to surrender and then rebelling?" The spirits replied, "Yes, there has." The king said, "Then the general's killing of you was indeed justified. Why are you still complaining?" The spirits said, "At that time, those who pretended to surrender were a few leaders, and those who rebelled were also a few leaders. The rest were coerced followers. Why should all of them be killed? Moreover, the general's intention was to please the harsh heart of Emperor Jiajing, not truly for the country or the people." The king laughed and said, "It is acceptable to say that the general did not act for the people, but it is unacceptable to say that he did not act for the country." He then instructed the 500 spirits: "This matter has been unresolved for over 200 years because it involved official matters, and the underworld officials could not decide. Now that the general's intentions are unclear, he cannot become a god; and your grievances remain unresolved, so you cannot be reborn as humans. I will report this matter to the Jade Emperor for a decision. As for the official in charge of military preparedness, his offense was minor, and there is a written record of his attempt to dissuade the general. He may be released back to the world, and in his next life, he will be punished as a wealthy woman to correct his weakness and cowardice." The 500 spirits all held their heads and knocked on the steps, making a sound, and said, "Only as the Great King commands." The king ordered a servant in green robes to lead Shen out.

    169

    行数里，仍至竹密书斋。老仆迎出，惊喜曰：“主人案结矣。”跪送再拜。

    　　青衣人呼至镜所，曰：“公视前生。”果仍巾履一前朝老诸生也。青衣人又呼曰：“公视今生。”不觉惊醒，汗出如雨，仍在书堂。家人环哭道：“晕去一昼夜，惟胸间微温。”

    　　文信王宫阙扁对甚多，不能记忆，只记宫门外金镌一联云：“阴间律例全无，那有法重情轻之案件；天上算盘最大，只等水落石出的时辰。”

    Explanation

    169

    Walking several miles, he still arrived at the dense bamboo study room. An old servant came out to meet him, surprised and said, "The master's case is settled." He knelt to send and bowed twice.

    The person in green clothes called to the mirror place and said, "You look at your past life." Indeed, it was still the hat and shoes of an old scholar from a previous dynasty. The person in green clothes called again, "You look at your present life." Unconsciously startled awake, sweating profusely, he was still in the study hall. Family members surrounded and cried, saying, "He fainted for a day and night, only slightly warm in the chest."

    There were many inscriptions on the palace gates of King Wenxin, but they could not be remembered. Only one couplet engraved in gold on the palace gate could be recalled: "In the underworld, there are no laws or regulations, how can there be cases where the law is lenient and the sentiment is light; in heaven, the abacus is the largest, waiting only for the time when the water recedes and the stones are revealed."

    170

    ○吴三复苏州吴三复者，其父某，饶于财，晚年中落，所存只万金，而负人者众。一日，谓三复曰：“我死则人望绝，汝辈犹得以所遗资生。”遂缢死。三复实未防救。其友顾心怡者，探知其事，伪设乩仙位而召三复请仙。三复往，焚香叩头，乩盘大书曰：“余尔父也。尔明知父将缢死，而汝竟不防于事先，又不救于事后，汝罪重，不日伏冥诛矣。”三复大惧，跪泣求忏悔。乩盘又书曰：“余舐犊情深，为汝想无他法，惟捐三千金交顾心怡立斗姥阁，一以超度我之亡魂，一以忏汝之罪孽，方可免死。”三复深信之，即以三千金与顾，立收券为凭。顾伪辞让，若不得已而后受者。少顷，饮三复酒，乘其醉，遣奴窃其券焚之。三复归家，券已遗失，遣人促顾立阁，顾曰：“某未受金，何能立阁？”三复心悟其奸，然其时家尚有余，亦不与校。

    Explanation

    170

    Wu Sanfu of Suzhou, whose father was once rich but fell into decline in his old age, leaving only ten thousand taels of silver while owing many people. One day, he told Wu Sanfu, "If I die, people will lose all hope, but you can still live off the remaining wealth." He then hanged himself. Wu Sanfu did not try to prevent or rescue him. His friend, Con Xinyi, learned of the matter and pretended to set up a spirit medium's seat to summon Wu Sanfu to ask for a message from his father. Wu Sanfu went, burned incense, and bowed. The spirit medium's board wrote, "I am your father. You knew your father was going to hang himself, yet you did not prevent it beforehand or save him afterward. Your sin is grave, and you will soon be punished in the afterlife." Wu Sanfu was greatly frightened and knelt, weeping, asking for forgiveness. The board then wrote, "I have deep affection like a mother cow for her calf. For you, I have no other solution but to donate three thousand taels of silver to Con Xinyi to build the Doumu Pavilion. This will both liberate my soul from suffering and atone for your sins, thus sparing you from death." Wu Sanfu deeply believed this and immediately gave three thousand taels of silver to Con Xinyi, receiving a receipt as proof. Con Xinyi pretended to refuse, as if accepting only under duress. Shortly after, he drank Wu Sanfu's wine, took advantage of his drunkenness, and sent a servant to steal and burn the receipt. When Wu Sanfu returned home, he found the receipt lost. He sent someone to urge Con Xinyi to build the pavilion, but Con Xinyi said, "I did not receive the money, how could I build the pavilion?" Wu Sanfu realized the deceit, but at that time, his family still had some wealth, so he did not dispute the matter.

    171

    又数年，三复窘甚，求贷于顾。顾以三千金营运，颇有赢余，意欲以三百金周给之。其叔某止之曰：“若与三百，则三千之说遂真矣，是小不忍而乱大谋也。”

    Explanation

    171

    After several more years, San Fu found himself in dire straits and sought a loan from Gu. Gu used 3,000 gold to conduct business and had considerable surplus. He intended to give 300 gold to help San Fu. However, San Fu’s uncle intervened and said, “If you give him 300 gold, then the claim of 3,000 gold will become true, and this is a minor matter to be unable to endure, but it will disrupt the greater plan.”

    172

    心怡以为然，卒不与。三复控官，俱以无券不准。三复怨甚，作牒词诉于城隍。

    　　焚牒三日，卒。再三日，顾心怡及其叔某偕亡。其夜，顾之邻人见苏州城隍司灯笼满巷。时乾隆二十九年四月事。

    　　○影光书楼事苏州史家巷蒋申吉，余年家子也。有子娶徐氏，年十九，琴瑟颇调。生产弥月，忽置酒唤郎君共饮，曰：“此别酒也，予与君缘满将去，昨日宿冤已到，势难挽回。谚曰：”夫妻本是同林鸟，大难来时各自飞。‘我死后，君亦勿复相念。“

    　　言毕大恸。蒋愕然，犹慰以好语。氏忽掷杯起立，竖眉目，非复平日容颜，卧床上，向西大呼曰：“汝记万历十二年影光书楼上事乎！两人设计害我，我死何惨！”呼毕，以手批颊，血出。未几，又以剪刀自刺。察其音，山东人语也。蒋家人环跪哀求，卒不解。如是者三日。

    Explanation

    172

    Gu Xinyi thought it was right, but ultimately refused to give it. He appealed to the authorities three times, but all were denied because there was no written agreement. Gu Xinyi became greatly resentful and wrote a petition to the City God's Office.

    He burned the petition for three days, and then he died. Three more days later, Gu Xinyi and his uncle both died. That night, neighbors of Gu saw lanterns from the City God's Office filling the alley. This was in the fourth month of the 29th year of the Qianlong Emperor's reign.

    The matter of the Shadow Light Book Hall in Suzhou's Shijia Lane concerns Jiang Shengji, a relative of mine. He had a son who married Xu, and at the age of nineteen, they were harmonious in their marriage. After giving birth and the baby being one month old, she suddenly prepared a drink and called her husband to drink together. She said, "This is the parting drink. My fate with you is complete and I must leave. Yesterday's old grudge has arrived, and the situation is difficult to reverse. The proverb says, 'Husband and wife are like birds in the same forest, but when great disaster comes, everyone flies apart.' After I die, you should not remember me anymore."

    After saying this, she wept bitterly. Jiang was startled, but still tried to comfort her with kind words. Xu suddenly threw down the cup and stood up, raising her eyebrows and glaring, no longer looking like herself. She lay on the bed and shouted towards the west, "Do you remember the incident at the Shadow Light Book Hall in the 12th year of the Wanli Emperor's reign? Two people plotted to harm me, how cruel my death was!" After shouting, she slapped her own cheek, causing blood to flow. Not long after, she took a pair of scissors and stabbed herself. Observing her voice, it was the dialect of Shandong. Jiang's family members knelt around her, begging her to stop, but she did not relent. This continued for three days.

    173

    有某和尚者，素有道行，申吉将遣人召之。徐氏厉声曰：“余汝家祖宗也，汝敢召僧驱我乎！”即作蒋氏之祖父语，口吻宛然；呼奴婢名，一一无爽；责子孙不肖事某某，亦复似是而非，有中有不中。和尚至门，徐氏曰：“秃奴可怖，且去，且去。”和尚甫出，则又詈曰：“汝家媳妇房中，能朝夕使和尚居乎？”

    　　和尚谓申吉曰：“此前世冤业，已二百馀年，才得寻着。积愈久者报愈深。老僧无能为。”走出，不肯复来，徐氏遂死。死时，面如裂帛，竟不知是何冤。此乾隆二十九年二月事。

    　　○波儿象江苏布政司书吏王文宾，昼寝，闻书室有布衣纟卒纟察声，视之，一隶卒也，见便昏迷，身随之行。至一处，殿宇清严，中坐两官：一白须年老者上坐，一壮年面麻而黑须者旁坐。阶下以金丝熏笼罩一兽，壮如猪，尖嘴绿毛。见王来，张嘴奋跃，欲前相啮。王惧，跪身向左。左一人蓝缕枯瘠，状如乞丐，怒目睨王。

    Explanation

    173

    There was once a monk who was renowned for his spiritual attainments. Shen Ji was about to send someone to summon him. Xu, speaking sternly, said, "I am your family's ancestor. How dare you summon a monk to drive me away!" She then spoke as if she were Jiang's grandfather, her tone exactly like his; she called the names of servants and maids, none of which were wrong; she blamed the descendants for their unworthy actions towards someone, which was also somewhat right and somewhat wrong, some being right and some not. When the monk arrived at the door, Xu smiled and said, "The bald monk is terrifying, go away, go away." As soon as the monk left, she cursed again, "In your wife's room, can you make the monk stay all day and night?"

    The monk told Shen Ji, "This is a karmic debt from past lives, over two hundred years old, and only now has it been found. The longer it accumulates, the deeper the retribution. The old monk is powerless." He walked out and did not come back again, and Xu died. At the time of her death, her face was like torn silk, and it remained unknown what the grievance was. This happened in February of the 29th year of the Qianlong era.

    ○ A certain official scribe named Wang Wenbin in the Jiangsu Provincial Administration heard a rustling sound in his study while napping. He looked and saw a low-ranking soldier. Upon seeing him, he became dazed, and his body followed the soldier. They arrived at a place where the hall was clear and solemn, with two officials seated inside: an elderly man with white hair on the upper seat, and a young man with a pockmarked face and black beard sitting beside him. Below the steps, a beast was enclosed in a golden net, as big as a pig, with a pointed snout and green fur. Seeing Wang come, it opened its mouth and leaped forward, wanting to bite him. Wang was afraid and knelt to the left. To his left stood a person in tattered clothes, emaciated and looking like a beggar, glaring angrily at Wang.

    174

    白须官手招王跪近前，问曰：“五十三两之项，汝曾记得乎？”王愕然不解。壮年者笑曰：“长船变价案也，汝前生事耳。”王恍然悟前明海运一案。前明海运既停，海船数百只，追价充公。王前世亦为江苏书吏，专司此案。运丁追比无出，凑银贿王，图准充销，为居间者中饱，案仍不结。此蓝缕者，乃追比缢死之运丁也。王悟前世事由，即侃侃实对。两官点头曰：“冤既有主，当别拘中饱者治罪，汝可回阳。”命隶卒引出。黄埃蔽天，王知泉下，问狱卒曰：“彼乞丐睨我者，吾知为冤鬼矣。彼似猪非猪，欲啮我者，是何物耶？”隶卒曰：“此名‘波儿象’，非猪也。阴间畜养此兽，凡遇案件讯明，罪重之人，即付彼吞噬，如阳间‘投畀豺虎’故事。”王悚然。行至大河侧，被隶卒推入水，惊醒，妻子环榻而泣，昏沉者已三日矣。

    Explanation

    174

    The elderly official motioned for Wang to kneel before him and asked, "Do you remember the 53 taels?" Wang was startled and did not understand. The young man laughed and said, "It's the case of the long ship valuation, a matter from your past life." Wang suddenly recalled the maritime case from the Ming dynasty. When the maritime trade had ceased, hundreds of ships were valued and confiscated. In his past life, Wang had been a clerk in Jiangsu province, responsible for this case. The shipmen, pursued and pressured to pay, could not produce the money, so they gathered silver to bribe Wang, hoping to have their debts forgiven and the case settled, allowing the middlemen to profit excessively. The case remained unresolved. This ragged man was a shipman who had been pursued to suicide. Wang realized the events of his past life and spoke truthfully. The two officials nodded and said, "The wronged spirit has been identified. We will now arrest the middlemen who profited from the case and punish them. You may return to the living world." They ordered the low-ranking officials to guide him out. The murky atmosphere of the underworld enveloped him, and Wang knew he was in the realm of the dead. He asked the guard, "That beggar who glances at me with suspicion, I know he is a wronged spirit. That creature that looks like a pig but is not a pig, and wants to bite me, what is it?" The guard replied, "It is called a 'Boer Xiang,' not a pig. In the underworld, this creature is kept, and whenever a case is resolved, those who have committed serious crimes are given to it to devour, just as in the living world, the idiom 'cast into the jaws of tigers' is used." Wang felt a sense of dread. They walked to the side of a large river, where the low-ranking officials pushed him into the water. He awoke, and his wife and children were weeping around his bed, as he had been in a dazed state for three days.

    175

    ○斧断狐尾河间府丁姓者，不事生业，以狎邪为事。闻某处有狐仙迷人，丁独往，以名帖投之，愿为兄弟。是晚，狐果现形，自称愚兄吴清，年五十许。相得如平生欢。

    　　凡所求请，愚兄必为张罗。丁每夸于人，以为交人不如交狐。

    　　一日，丁谓吴曰：“我欲往扬州观灯，能否？”狐曰：“能。河间至扬，离二千里，弟衣我衣，闭目同行便至矣。”从之，凭空而起，两耳闻风声，顷刻至扬。有商家方演戏，丁与狐在空中观，忽闻场上锣鼓声喧，关圣单刀步出，狐大惊，舍丁而奔，丁不觉坠于席上。商人以为妖，械送江都县。鞫讯再三，解回原籍。

    　　见狐咎之。狐曰：“兄素胆小，闻关帝将出，故奔；且偶忆汝嫂，故急归。”

    Explanation

    175

    A Man Named Ding from Hejian Prefecture

    A man named Ding from Hejian Prefecture did not engage in livelihood and instead took debauchery as his occupation. He heard that there was a fox spirit in a certain place that bewitched people, so he went alone, presented a name card to it, and wished to be brothers. That evening, the fox spirit indeed appeared, introducing itself as his elder brother Wu Qing, who was around fifty years old. They got along as if they had known each other for a long time.

    For whatever he requested, his elder brother would certainly arrange for it. Ding often boasted to others that making friends with people was not as good as making friends with foxes.

    One day, Ding said to Wu, "I want to go to Yangzhou to watch the lanterns. Is that possible?" Wu replied, "Yes. From Hejian to Yangzhou, it is two thousand li away. You wear my clothes, close your eyes, and walk together, and you will arrive." Ding followed his advice, and they rose into the air. With both ears, he heard the sound of the wind, and they arrived in Yangzhou in a moment. A merchant was just performing a play, and Ding and the fox spirit watched it from the air. Suddenly, they heard the sound of drums and gongs on the stage, and Guan Di, holding a single sword, stepped out. The fox spirit was greatly startled, abandoned Ding, and rushed away. Ding did not realize he had fallen onto the stage. The merchant thought they were demons, arrested them, and sent them in chains to Jiangdu County. After being interrogated repeatedly, he was sent back to his hometown.

    Seeing the fox spirit, Ding blamed it. The fox spirit said, "You are usually timid. When you heard that Guan Di was about to appear, you ran away. And I incidentally remembered your sister-in-law, so I rushed back."

    176

    丁问：“嫂何在？”曰：“我狐也，焉能婚娶？不过魇迷良家妇耳。邻家李氏女，即汝嫂也。”丁心动，求见嫂。狐曰：“有何不可。但汝人，身无由入人密室。

    　　我有小袄，汝着之，便能出入窗户，如履无人之境。“丁如其言，竟入李家。李女久被狐蛊，状如白痴。丁登其床，女即与交。女为狐所染，气奄奄矣，忽近人身，酣畅异常，病亦渐愈。丁告以故，女秘之不言，而渐渐有乐丁厌狐之意。

    　　狐知之，召丁语曰：“开门揖盗，兄之罪也。近日嫂竟爱弟而憎我。弟固两世人身，女子爱之诚宜。然非兄之丑，亦无由显弟之美也。”丁问故，狐曰：“凡男子之阴，以头上肉肥重为贵。年十五六，即脱颖出，皮不裹棱，嗅之无秽气者，人类也。皮裹其头不净，棱下多腐渣而筋胜者，兽类也。弟不见羊马猪狗之阴，非皆皮裹头尖而以筋皮胜者乎！”出其阴示之，果细瘦而毛坚如锥。丁闻之，愈自得也。

    Explanation

    176

    Ding asked, "Where is my sister-in-law?" The fox replied, "I am a fox; how could I marry and have children? I merely bewitch respectable women. The neighbor's daughter from the Li family is your sister-in-law." Ding's heart stirred, and he requested to see his sister-in-law. The fox said, "What's wrong with that? But you are a human; you cannot enter a private room by yourself.

    I have a small robe; if you wear it, you will be able to enter and exit through the windows, as if walking in an empty place." Ding followed the fox's instructions and entered the Li household. The Li woman had been bewitched by the fox for a long time and acted like a fool. Ding climbed onto her bed, and the woman immediately engaged in sexual activity with him. The woman, infected by the fox, looked very weak, but suddenly she was close to his body, experiencing extreme pleasure, and her illness gradually improved. Ding told her the reason, and she kept it a secret, not speaking about it, but gradually developed a liking for Ding and a dislike for the fox.

    The fox knew about this and called Ding to speak to him, saying, "Inviting thieves in by opening the door, that is your fault, brother. Recently, your sister-in-law has come to love your younger brother and hates me. My younger brother indeed is a human being of two generations; it is indeed appropriate for women to love him. But not because of your ugliness, also cannot show my younger brother's beauty." Ding asked why, and the fox said, "The penis of a man, the fatter and heavier the head, the more valuable it is. At fifteen or sixteen years old, it will emerge, not covered by skin, and smells clean, is human. Covered by skin and not clean, with strong tendons and much rotten residue at the edges, is an animal. You haven't seen the penises of sheep, horses, pigs, and dogs; are not all covered by skin with sharp heads and strong tendons?" The fox showed his penis to Ding, and indeed it was thin and slender with stiff hair like a needle. Ding heard this and became even more satisfied with himself.

    177

    狐妒丁夺妇宠，阴就女子之床，取小袄归。丁傍晓钻窗，窗不开矣，块然坠地。女家父母大惊，以为获怪。先喷狗血，继沃屎溺，针炙倍至，受无量苦。丁以实情告，其家不信，幸女爱之，私为解脱，曰：“彼亦被狐惑耳，不如送之还家。”丁得脱归，将寻狐咎之，狐避不见。是晚，大书一纸贴丁门曰：“陈平盗嫂，宜有此报。从此拆开，弟兄分灶。”

    　　嗣后，丁与女断，狐仍往。其家设醮步罡，终不能禁。女一胎生四子，面状皆人类，而尻多一尾，落地能行，颇尽孝道，时随父出采蔬果奉母。一日，狐来向女泣曰：“我与卿缘尽矣。昨泰山娘娘知我蛊惑妇人，罚砌进香御路，永不许出境。吾次携四子同行。”袖中出一小斧交其女曰：“四儿子尾不断，终不得修到人身。卿人也，为我断之。”女如其言，各拜谢去。

    Explanation

    177

    The fox envied Ding's wife's favor and, in the dead of night, crept into the woman's bed and took a small garment. In the early morning, Ding tried to break into the window, but it did not open, and he fell to the ground. The woman's parents were greatly alarmed, thinking they had encountered a strange phenomenon. They first sprayed dog blood, then poured urine and feces, and subjected Ding to numerous acupuncture treatments, causing him immense suffering. Ding revealed the truth, but the family did not believe him. Fortunately, the woman loved him and privately helped him escape, saying, "He was also bewitched by the fox. It would be better to send him back home." Ding managed to return home and sought to find the fox to punish it, but the fox avoided him and did not appear. That night, the fox wrote a large scroll and posted it on Ding's door, which read: "Chen Ping stole his brother-in-law's wife, and thus deserves this retribution. From now on, we will be separated, and the brothers will have separate households."

    After this, Ding and the woman parted ways, but the fox continued to visit. The family held a ritual and walked the steps of the gang (a type of ritual dance), but could not stop the fox. The woman gave birth to four sons in one pregnancy, who all looked like humans but had an extra tail at the back. They could walk as soon as they were born and were very filial, often accompanying their father to gather vegetables and fruits to serve their mother. One day, the fox came to the woman and wept, saying, "My time with you is over. The Goddess of Mount Tai knows that I have bewitched women and has sentenced me to build a path for incense offerings, never allowing me to leave the area. I will take my four sons with me." The fox took out a small axe from his sleeve and handed it to the woman, saying, "If the tails of the four sons are not cut, they will never be able to become human. You are human, so please cut them for me." The woman followed the fox's instructions, and they each bowed and thanked her before leaving.

    178

    ○洗紫河车四川酆都县皂隶丁恺，持文书往夔州投递。过鬼门关，见前有石碑，上书“阴阳界”三字。丁走至碑下，摩观良久，不觉已出界外。欲返，迷路。不得已，任足而行。至一古庙，神像剥落，其旁牛头鬼蒙灰丝蛛网而立。丁怜庙中之无僧也，以袖拂去其尘网。

    　　又行二里许，闻水声潺潺，中隔长河，一妇人临水洗菜。菜色甚紫，枝叶环结如芙蓉。谛视渐近，乃其亡妻。妻见丁大惊曰：“君何至此？此非人间。”丁告之故，问妻：“所居何处？所洗何菜？”妻曰：“妾亡后为阎罗王隶卒牛头鬼所娶，家住河西槐树下。所洗者，即世上胞胎，俗名‘紫河车’是也。洗十次者，儿生清秀而贵；洗两三次者，中常之人；不洗者，昏愚秽浊之人。阎王以此事分派诸牛头管领，故我代夫洗之。”丁问妻：“可能使我还阳否？”妻曰：“待吾夫归商之。但妾既为君妇，又为鬼妻，新夫旧夫，殊觉启齿为羞。”语毕，邀至其家，谈家常，讯亲故近状。

    Explanation

    178

    A Man Who Washed the Purple River Vehicle

    In the Fengdu County of Sichuan, a servant named Ding Kai was carrying official documents to deliver them in Kuizhou. As he passed through the Ghost Gate, he saw a stone tablet ahead with the inscription "Yin Yang Boundary" written on it. Ding walked to the base of the tablet and examined it for a long time, not realizing that he had already crossed the boundary. He wanted to return but got lost. Unable to find his way back, he walked aimlessly. Eventually, he came to an ancient temple where the statues of the deities were peeling and flaking, with an ox-headed ghost standing nearby, covered in dust and spider webs. Ding felt sorry for the temple being without a monk and used his sleeve to brush away the dust and webs.

    After walking another two li, he heard the sound of water flowing gently. In the middle of the river, a woman was washing vegetables. The vegetables were very purple, with their branches and leaves tied together in a circular shape, resembling a lotus flower. As Ding observed her closely, he realized that she was his deceased wife. His wife was greatly surprised when she saw him and exclaimed, "Why are you here? This is not the human world." Ding told her the reason for his presence and asked her, "Where do you live? What vegetables are you washing?" His wife replied, "After I died, I was married to an ox-headed ghost who serves as a subordinate to King Yama. We live under a locust tree on the west side of the river. What I am washing are the placentas from the world, which are commonly known as 'Purple River Vehicles.' Those washed ten times will give birth to children who are clear, beautiful, and noble; those washed two or three times will give birth to average people; those who are not washed will give birth to dull, foolish, and filthy people. King Yama assigns this task to various ox-headed ghosts to manage, so I wash them on behalf of my husband." Ding asked his wife, "Can you make me return to the living?" His wife said, "Wait for my husband to return and we will discuss it. But I am already your wife and also the wife of a ghost. Having both a new and old husband makes me feel ashamed to speak about it." After her words, she invited him to her home, where they talked about family matters and asked about the recent news of relatives and friends.

    179

    少顷，外有敲门者，丁惧，伏床下。妻开门，牛头鬼入，取牛头掷于几上，一一假面具也。既去面具，眉目言笑，宛若平人，谓其妻曰：“惫甚！今日侍阎王审大案数十，脚跟立久酸痛，须斟酒饮我。”徐惊曰：“有生人气！”且嗅且寻。妻度不可隐，拉丁出，叩头告之故，代为哀求。牛头曰：“是人非独为妻故将救之，是实于我有德。我在庙中蒙灰满面，此人为我拭净，是一长者。但未知阳数何如，我明日往判官处偷查其簿，便当了然。”命丁坐，三人共饮。有肴馔至，丁将举箸，牛头与妻急夺之，曰：“鬼酒无妨，鬼肉不可食，食则常留此间矣。”

    Explanation

    179

    After a short while, someone knocked on the door outside, and Ding was afraid, so he crouched under the bed. His wife opened the door, and the bull-headed ghost entered, taking off its bull head and throwing it onto the table, revealing that it was a fake mask. After removing the mask, its facial expressions and speech were as if it were an ordinary person. It said to its wife, "I'm extremely tired! Today, I served Yama in reviewing dozens of major cases, and my heels ache from standing for so long. I need to pour me some wine." Gradually, it exclaimed, "There's a living person's smell!" and both smelled and searched. The wife realized that she could not hide the situation, pulled Ding out, knelt and bowed to tell the reason, and begged on his behalf. The ghost said, "This person will not only be saved for the sake of his wife, but he truly has virtue toward me. I was in the temple, covered in dust all over my face, and this person wiped it clean for me. He is a respectable person. But I do not know how his life span will be, so I will secretly check his record tomorrow at the judge's place, and then I will know for sure." It ordered Ding to sit down, and the three of them drank together. Food and drink arrived, and Ding was about to pick up his chopsticks, but the ghost and his wife quickly took them away, saying, "Ghost's wine is harmless, but ghost's meat cannot be eaten; if you eat it, you will remain here forever."

    180

    次日，牛头出，及暮，归，欣欣然贺曰：“昨查阴司簿册，汝阳数未终，且喜我有出关之差，正可送汝出界。”手持肉一块，红色臭腐，曰：“以赠汝，可发大财。”丁问故，曰：“此河南富人张某之背上肉也。张有恶行，阎王擒而钩其背于铁锥山。半夜肉溃，脱逃去。现在阳间患发背疮，千医不愈。汝往，以此肉研碎敷之即愈，彼此重酬汝。”丁拜谢，以纸裹而藏之，遂与同出关，牛头即不见。

    Explanation

    180

    The next day, a bull-headed figure appeared and, by evening, returned, joyfully greeting, "I checked the records of the underworld yesterday and found that your fate in the underworld has not yet come to an end. And I am glad that I have a mission to cross the boundary, which can escort you out of the boundary." He held a piece of red, rotten meat and said, "This is a gift for you, and it can make you wealthy." When asked why, he explained, "This is the meat from the back of a wealthy man named Zhang from Henan. Zhang had evil deeds, and the underworld ruler captured him and hooked his back on a mountain of iron spikes. In the middle of the night, the flesh rotted, and he escaped. Now he suffers from a boil on the back in the mortal world, which has not been cured by a thousand doctors. You should go and grind this meat into a paste and apply it to the wound, and it will heal. Both parties will reward you generously." The person bowed in gratitude, wrapped the meat in paper and hid it, then went out of the boundary together, and the bull-headed figure then disappeared.

    181

    丁至河南，果有张姓患背疮。医之痊，获五百金。

    　　○石门尸怪浙江石门县里书李念先，催租下乡，夜入荒村，无旅店。遥望远处茅舍有灯，向光而行。稍近，见破篱拦门，中有呻吟声。李大呼：“里书某催粮求宿，可速开门！”竟不应。李从篱外望，见遍地稻草，草中有人，枯瘠，如用灰纸糊其面者。面长五寸许，阔三寸许，奄奄然卧而宛转。李知为病重人，再三呼，始低声应曰：“客自推门。”李如其言入。病人告以“染疫垂危，举家死尽”，言甚惨。

    Explanation

    181

    Ding arrived in Henan and indeed met a man surnamed Zhang who was suffering from a back abscess. After treating him and curing him, Ding received five hundred gold coins.

    ○ The Ghost of Shimen, Zhejiang

    Li Nianxian, a village official from Shimen County, went to the countryside to collect taxes one night. He entered a deserted village and found no inn. From a distance, he saw a thatched hut with a light inside and followed the light. As he got closer, he saw a broken fence blocking the door, and inside, he heard groaning sounds. Li shouted loudly, "I am the village official Li, here to collect grain and ask for lodging. Please open the door quickly!" But there was no response. Li looked through the fence and saw rice straw covering the ground, with a person lying among the straw, emaciated and skeletal, as if his face was pasted with gray paper. His face was about five inches long and three inches wide, and he was lying weakly, twisting and turning. Li realized that the person was seriously ill. He called out repeatedly, and the person finally responded in a low voice, "The guest can push the door open himself." Li did as he was told and entered. The patient told him, "I am infected with a disease and on the verge of death; my entire family has died." His words were very sad.

    182

    强其外出买酒，辞不能。许谢钱二百，乃勉强爬起，持钱而行。

    　　壁间灯灭，李倦甚，倒卧草中，闻草中飒然有声，如人起立者。李疑之，取火石击火，照见一蓬发人，枯瘦更甚，面亦阔三寸许，眼闭血流，形同僵尸，倚草直立。问之，不应。李惊，乃益击火石。每火光一亮，则僵尸之面一现。李思遁出，坐而倒退。退一步，则僵尸进一步。李愈骇，抉篱而奔。尸追之，践草上，簌簌有声。狂奔里许，闯入酒店，大喊而仆。尸亦仆；酒家灌以姜汤，苏，具道其故。方知合村瘟疫，追人之尸，即病者之妻，死未棺殓，感阳气而走魄也。村人共往寻沽酒者，亦持钱倒于桥侧，离酒家尚五十余步。

    Explanation

    182

    Li was forced to go out and buy wine, but he declined. They agreed to pay him 200 coins, and he reluctantly struggled to get up, carrying the money with him.

    The lamp on the wall went out, and Li was very tired, so he collapsed onto the grass. He heard a rustling sound in the grass, like someone standing up. Li doubted it and took flint to strike fire, and saw a person with disheveled hair, even more emaciated, with a face about three inches wide, eyes closed, blood flowing, and the shape like a zombie, leaning against the grass and standing upright. Li asked him, but he did not respond. Li was startled, so he struck the flint even more. Every time the firelight flashed, the zombie's face appeared once. Li thought of fleeing out, sat down and backed up. Every step back, the zombie took one step forward. Li was even more terrified, pushed through the fence and ran. The corpse pursued him, stepping on the grass and making a rustling sound. He ran madly for about a li, rushed into the tavern, shouted and fell. The corpse also fell; the tavern owner poured ginger soup over him, and Li woke up, fully recounting the reason. Only then did he realize the whole village was suffering from an epidemic, the corpse pursuing people was actually the wife of the sick person, who had not been buried in a coffin, and was affected by the yang energy and had wandered spirit. The villagers all went together to find the person who had bought the wine, and he was also holding money and had fallen on the side of the bridge, still about fifty or sixty steps away from the tavern.

    183

    ○空心鬼杭州周豹先，家住东青巷。屋之大厅上，每夜立一人，红袍乌纱，长髯方面；旁侍二人，琐小猥鄙，衣青衣，听其使唤。其胸以下至肚腹，皆空透如水晶，人视之，虽隔肚腹，犹望见厅上所挂画也。

    　　周氏郎年十四，卧病，见乌纱者呼从者谋曰：“若何而害之？”从者曰：“明日渠将服卢浩亭之药，我二人变作药渣伏碗中，俾渠吞入，便可抽其肺肠。”

    　　次日，卢浩亭来诊脉，毕，周氏郎不肯服药，告家人以鬼语如此。家人买一钟馗忍挂堂上，鬼笑曰：“此近视眼钟先生，目昏昏然，人鬼不辨，何足惧哉！”盖画者戏为小鬼替钟馗取耳，钟馗忍痒，微合其目故也。

    　　居月馀，鬼又言曰：“是家气运未衰，闹之无益，不如他去。”乌纱者曰：“若如此，空过一家，将来成例，何以得血食乎？”抡其指曰：“今已周年，可索一属猪者去。”未几，果一奴属猪者死，而主人愈。周氏家人至今呼为“空心鬼”。

    Explanation

    183

    The Hollow-Bodied Ghost

    Zhou Baoshan of Hangzhou lived in Dongqing Lane. In his main hall, every night, a person would stand. He wore a red robe and a black hat, had a long beard, and a square face; two small, lowly, and contemptible servants stood beside him, dressed in blue robes, listening to his commands. From his chest down to his abdomen, he was all hollow and transparent like crystal. When people looked at him, they could see through his body and still see the paintings hanging on the walls of the hall.

    Zhou's young man, aged fourteen, was ill and saw the one in the black hat call his servants to plot. The one in the black hat asked, "How can we harm him?" One of the servants replied, "Tomorrow, he will take Lu Haoting's medicine. We can transform into drug residue and hide in the bowl, making him swallow us, and then we can extract his lungs and intestines."

    The next day, Lu Haoting came to diagnose his pulse. Afterward, the young man refused to take the medicine and told his family about the ghost's plan. The family bought a painting of Zhong馗 and hung it on the wall. The ghost laughed and said, "This nearsighted Mr. Zhong, with his dim eyes, cannot tell the difference between humans and ghosts, what is there to fear!" The painter made a joke by having a little ghost pull on Zhong馗's ear, causing him to squint slightly due to the itch.

    After more than a month, the ghost spoke again, "The family's fortune has not declined, and causing trouble will be of no benefit. We might as well go elsewhere." The one in the black hat said, "If we pass by a house without taking action, it will become a regular occurrence, and how will we get our blood meal?" He pointed out and said, "It has been a year, and we can take the life of someone born in the year of the pig." Not long after, indeed, a servant born in the year of the pig died, and the owner recovered. The Zhou family continues to call him the 'hollow-bodied ghost.'

    184

    ○画工画僵尸杭州刘以贤，善写照。邻人有一子一父而居室者。其父死，子孙外出买棺，嘱邻人代请以贤为其父传形。以贤往，入其室，虚无人焉。意死者必居楼上，乃蹑梯登楼，就死人之床，坐而抽笔。尸忽蹶然起，以贤知为走尸，坐而不动。尸亦不动，但闭目张口，翕翕然眉撑肉皱而已。以贤念身走则尸必追，不如竟画，乃取笔申纸，依尸样描摹。每臂动指运，尸亦如之。以贤大呼，无人答应。俄而其子上楼，见父尸起，惊而仆。又一邻上楼，见尸起，亦惊滚滚落楼下。以贤窘甚，强忍待之。俄而，抬棺者来。以贤徐记尸走畏苕帚，乃呼曰：“汝等持苕帚来！”抬棺者心知有走尸之事，持帚上楼，拂之，倒。乃取姜汤灌醒仆者，而纳尸入棺。

    Explanation

    184

    A Painter and the Zombie

    Liu Yixian of Hangzhou was skilled at painting portraits. A neighbor lived with his father and son. When the father died, the son and grandson went out to buy a coffin and asked the neighbor to invite Liu Yixian to paint a likeness of the deceased. Liu Yixian went to the house and entered the room, finding it empty. He assumed the dead man must be upstairs, so he climbed the ladder to the upper floor and sat on the bed where the deceased lay, taking out his brushes to begin painting. Suddenly, the corpse leapt up, and Liu Yixian recognized it as a zombie. He remained seated and still. The zombie also remained still, just closing its eyes and opening its mouth, with its eyebrows raised and its skin wrinkled. Liu Yixian thought that if he ran, the zombie would surely chase him, so he decided to finish the painting instead. He took out his brush and paper and began to sketch according to the shape of the zombie. As he moved his arms and fingers, the zombie mimicked his movements. Liu Yixian shouted for help, but no one answered. Soon, the son came upstairs and saw his father’s corpse move, and he fell down in shock. Another neighbor also came upstairs and saw the corpse move, and he too fell down in shock. Liu Yixian was in a very awkward situation, but he endured and waited. Soon, the coffin bearers arrived. Liu Yixian remembered that zombies feared brooms and called out, “Bring me a broom!” The bearers knew there was a zombie and brought a broom upstairs. They brushed the zombie, and it fell down. They then gave the son some ginger soup to revive him and placed the corpse in the coffin.

    185

    ○莺娇扬州妓莺娇，年二十四，矢志从良。有柴姓者娶为妾，婚期已定。太学生朱某慕之，以十金求欢。妓受其金，绐曰：“某夕来，当与郎同寝。”朱临期往，则花烛盈门，莺娇已登车矣。朱知为所诳，怅然反。逾年，莺娇病瘵卒。朱忽梦见莺娇披黑衫直入朱门，曰：“我来还债。”惊而醒。明日，家产一黑牛，向朱依依，若相识者。卖之，竟得十金。狎邪之费，尚且不可苟得也如此。

    Explanation

    185

    Eryang, a courtesan from Yangzhou, was twenty-four years old and had vowed to lead a virtuous life. A man surnamed Chi arranged to marry her as a concubine, and the wedding date was set. Zhu, a student at the Imperial College, admired her and offered ten gold coins to spend the night with her. The courtesan accepted the money and promised, "Come on a certain night, and I will sleep with you." When the night arrived, Zhu went to the courtesan's home, only to find that the house was filled with red candles and decorations, and the courtesan had already boarded the carriage to leave. Realizing he had been deceived, Zhu returned in a state of disappointment and sadness.

    A year later, the courtesan died from consumption. One night, Zhu suddenly dreamed that the courtesan appeared in a black robe and entered his home, saying, "I come to repay my debt." Startled awake, Zhu noticed that his family had a black cow that looked affectionately toward him. When the cow was sold, Zhu eventually obtained ten gold coins. Even the cost of courtesan services, it seems, cannot be obtained lightly.

    186

    ○旁观因果常州马秀才士麟，自言幼时从父读书北楼，窗开处，与卖菊叟王某露台相近。

    　　一日早起，倚窗望，天色微明，见王叟登台浇菊，毕，将下台。有担粪者荷二桶升台，意欲助浇。叟色不悦，拒之；而担粪者必欲上，遂相挤于台坡。天雨台滑，坡仄且高，叟以手推担粪者，上下势不敌，遂失足陨台下。叟急趋扶之，未起，而双桶压其胸，两足蹶然直矣。叟大骇，噤不发声，曳担粪者足，开后门，置之河干，复举其桶置尸傍，归闭门复卧。马时年幼，念此关人命事，不可妄谈，掩窗而已。日渐高，闻外轰传河干有死人里保报官。日午，武进知县鸣锣至。仵作跪启：“尸无伤，系失足跌死。”官询邻人，邻人齐称不知。乃命棺殓加封焉，出示招尸亲而去。

    Explanation

    186

    A Scholar's Tale of Causality in Changzhou

    Ma Shilin, a scholar from Changzhou, recounted that when he was young, he studied under his father in the north tower. The window opened to a place close to the raised platform of the elderly man Wang, who sold chrysanthemums.

    One day, early in the morning, leaning on the window and looking out, as the sky was slightly light, he saw the elderly man Wang climb the platform to water the chrysanthemums, finish, and was about to come down. There was a man carrying two buckets who climbed the platform, intending to help with watering. The elderly man looked unhappy and refused him; but the man carrying the manure insisted on going up, and they squeezed each other on the slope of the platform. It rained, making the platform slippery, and the slope was steep and high. The elderly man pushed the man carrying the manure with his hand, and the upper and lower positions were unequal in strength, so he lost his footing and fell off the platform. The elderly man hurried to help him up, but he did not get up, and the two buckets pressed against his chest, and his feet suddenly went straight. The elderly man was greatly frightened, stayed silent and did not speak, pulled the foot of the man carrying the manure, opened the back door, placed him on the riverbank, then lifted the buckets and placed them beside the body, returned home, closed the door, and lay down again. Ma was still young at the time and thought that this was a matter concerning human life, which could not be discussed lightly, so he simply closed the window. As the day progressed, he heard rumors spreading widely that there was a dead person on the riverbank, and the village head reported to the official. At noon, the magistrate of Wujin County arrived, beating a gong. The forensic expert knelt and reported: "The body has no injuries, it is a fall from a height." The official asked the neighbors, and they all claimed they did not know. He then ordered the coffin to be prepared and sealed, displayed a notice to find the family of the deceased, and left.

    187

    事隔九年，马年二十一，入学为生员。父亡，家贫，即于幼时读书所招徒授经。督学使者刘吴龙将临岁考，马早起温经，开窗，见远巷有人肩两桶冉冉来。

    　　谛视之，担粪者也。大骇，以为来报叟仇。俄而过叟门不入，别行数十步，入一李姓家。李颇富，亦近邻而居相望者也。马愈疑，起尾之，至李门。其家苍头踉跄出曰：“吾家娘子分娩甚急，将往招收生婆。”问：“有担桶者入乎？”曰：“无。”言未毕，门内又一婢出曰：“不必招收生婆，娘子已产一官人矣。”马方悟担粪者来托生，非报仇也。但窃怪李家颇富，担粪者何修得此？自此，留心访李家儿作何举止。

    Explanation

    187

    Nine years have passed, and Ma, now twenty-one, entered school to become a scholar. After his father's death and falling into poverty, he began to teach classics at the place where he had studied as a child. As the supervisor of education, Liu Wulong, was about to conduct the annual examination, Ma got up early to review the classics. He opened the window and saw someone carrying two buckets slowly coming from a distant alley.

    Upon closer inspection, he realized it was a man carrying manure. Greatly frightened, he thought the man had come to take revenge on the old man. Soon, the man passed by the old man's door without entering and went another several dozen steps before entering a house of the surname Li. The Li family was quite wealthy and also neighbors who lived opposite each other. Ma became more suspicious and got up to follow him, arriving at the Li family's door. A servant stumbled out and said, "Our lady is giving birth very urgently, and I will go to recruit a midwife." She asked, "Did anyone carrying buckets enter?" The servant replied, "No." Before she finished speaking, another maid came out and said, "There is no need to recruit a midwife; the lady has already given birth to a gentleman." Only then did Ma realize that the man carrying manure had come to reincarnate, not to take revenge. He secretly wondered how a man carrying manure could have such wealth. From that day on, he carefully investigated the behavior of the child in the Li family.

    188

    又七年，李氏儿渐长，不喜读书，好畜禽鸟；而王叟康健如故，年八十余，爱菊之性，老而弥笃。一日者，马又早起倚窗，叟上台灌菊，李氏儿亦登楼放鸽。

    　　忽十余鸽飞集叟花台栏杆上。儿惧飞去，再三呼鸽不动。儿不得已，寻取石子掷之，误中王叟。叟惊，失足陨于台下，良久不起，两足蹶然直矣。儿大骇，噤不发声，默默掩窗去。日渐高，叟之子孙咸来寻翁，知是失足跌死，哭殓而已。

    　　此事闻于刘绳庵相公。相公曰：“一担粪人，一叟，报复之巧如此，公平如此，而在局中者彼此不知，赖马姓人冷观历历。然则天下事吉凶祸福，各有来因，当无丝毫舛错，而惜乎从旁冷观者之无人也！”

    Explanation

    188

    Seven years later, the Li family's son had grown up, disliking books but enjoying keeping domestic animals and birds. Meanwhile, Old Man Wang remained as healthy as ever, over eighty years old, with his love for chrysanthemums growing stronger with age. One day, the horse got up early and leaned against the window. Old Man Wang went up to the terrace to water the chrysanthemums, and the Li family's son climbed the tower to release the pigeons.

    Suddenly, more than ten pigeons flew and gathered on the old man's flower terrace railing. The son was afraid they would fly away, called the pigeons repeatedly but they did not move. With no other choice, he found some pebbles and threw them, accidentally hitting Old Man Wang. The old man was startled, lost his footing, and fell from the terrace. He did not get up for a long time, and his legs were stiff. The son was greatly frightened, unable to speak, silently closed the window and left. As the day progressed, the old man's descendants all came to look for him. They knew he had died from falling and were only able to cry and prepare the body.

    This matter was heard by Mr. Liu Sheng'an, the Chancellor. He said, "A man who carries manure, an old man, their revenge was so clever and fair, yet those involved did not know about it. Thanks to the person surnamed Ma who observed coldly and clearly. Then, all things in the world have their own causes and effects, there should be no slightest error, but it is a pity that there are no observers from the side!"

    189

    ○徐四葬女子摆牙喇徐四，居京城金鱼胡同，家贫，屋内外五间，兄嫂二人同居。兄外出值宿。嫂素贤，谓徐四曰：“北风甚大，室惟一暖炕，吾与叔俱畏寒，而又不便同炕宿。我今夜归宿母家，以炕让叔。”叔唯唯，嫂遂归宁。

    　　夜二鼓，月色微明，有叩门者。走入，美少年，貂帽狐裘，手挈一囊，坐炕上泣曰：“君救我！我非男子，君亦不必问我所由来。但许我一宿，我以貂裘为赠。”解其囊示徐，金珠首饰，约值万金。徐年少，见其美貌怀宝，意不能无动。

    　　然终不知何家女，留之惧祸，拒之不忍，乃曰：“奶奶姑坐，我与邻人商量即归。”

    　　女曰：“诺。”徐自外掩门，奔往善觉寺，告方丈僧圆智。圆智者，高年有道，徐素所敬也。圆智闻之，亦大骇曰：“此必大家贵妾，有故奔出。留之有祸，拒之不忍，子不如在我庵中坐以待旦，俟天明归家未迟。”徐以为然。

    Explanation

    189

    Xu Si, a poor man living in Jin Yu Hutong in the capital, shared a five-room house with his elder brother and sister-in-law. His elder brother was on night duty, and his sister-in-law, who was always virtuous, told Xu Si, "The northern wind is very strong tonight. There is only one warm kang in the room, and both you and I are afraid of the cold, but it would be inconvenient for us to share the same kang. I will return to my mother's home tonight, so you can use the kang." Xu Si nodded in agreement, and his sister-in-law returned to her parents' home.

    At midnight, the moonlight was faintly bright, and someone knocked on the door. A beautiful young man walked in, wearing a sable hat and a fox fur coat, carrying a bag in his hand. He sat on the kang and cried, "Save me, sir! I am not a man. You don't need to ask where I come from. Just allow me to stay for one night, and I will give you this sable fur coat as a gift." He opened the bag to show Xu Si, revealing gold, pearls, and jewelry worth about ten thousand gold pieces. Xu Si, being young, was moved by the man's beauty and the treasures he carried.

    However, Xu Si did not know which family's daughter the man was. He feared bringing disaster by keeping him but could not bring himself to reject him. He said, "Please sit, madam. I will talk with a neighbor and return immediately."

    The man replied, "Yes." Xu Si closed the door and rushed to Shan Jue Temple to tell the abbot monk Yuan Zhi. Yuan Zhi was an elderly and wise monk whom Xu Si had always respected. Yuan Zhi was also greatly frightened when he heard the story and said, "This must be a high-ranking lady from a wealthy family who has fled for some reason. Keeping her will bring disaster, but rejecting her is unbearable. You would be better off sitting in my temple and waiting until dawn. It is not too late to return home in the morning." Xu Si agreed with the abbot's advice.

    190

    圆智之弟子某，素无赖，闻之，乃伪作徐还家状。开门灭灯入，遽上炕抱女子卧矣。是夜，其兄值宿苦寒，以取皮衣故，四更还家。持灯照炕下，有男子履，大怒，以为妻与叔奸，拔腰间刀，连断两头，奔告岳家。入门大呼，妻自内走出，其兄惊仆地，以为鬼也。正喧嚷间，而徐四与圆智亦来，方知误杀之。因相与报官，刑部以为杀奸，律本勿论，但悬女头招尸亲，竟无认者。徐四怜女子之送死，鬻其金珠，为收葬焉。

    　　○羊践前缘康熙五十九年，山东巡抚李公树德生日，司道各具羊酒为寿。连日演戏，诸幕客互相娱宴，彻夜不卧。有刑名张先生酒酣，逃席入房。将就寝，闻纱帐内嗫嗫有声，若男女交媾状。怒，以为他幕客昵优童，借其床为淫所。大呼揭帐，则两白羊跪而人淫，即群官送礼之羊也。见人惊散。张笑以为奇，遍告同人。

    Explanation

    190

    A Disciple of Yuan Zhi

    A certain disciple of Master Yuan Zhi was always a troublemaker. Upon hearing something, he pretended to return home. Opening the door and extinguishing the light, he rushed onto the bed and embraced a woman, lying down with her. That night, his brother, who was on night duty in the bitter cold, returned home at the fourth watch to fetch a fur coat. Holding a lamp, he looked under the bed and saw a man's shoes. Enraged, he thought his wife had been unfaithful with his younger brother, so he drew a knife from his waist and cut off both heads, then rushed to inform his father-in-law's house. Upon entering, he shouted, and his wife came out from inside. His brother was startled and fell to the ground, thinking it was a ghost. In the midst of the commotion, Xu Si and Yuan Zhi also arrived, and they only then realized that he had mistakenly killed someone. They all reported it to the authorities, and the Ministry of Punishment considered it a case of killing an adulterer, which according to the law should not be prosecuted. However, they hanged the woman's head to summon her relatives, but no one came forward to identify her. Xu Si, moved by the woman's tragic death, sold her jewelry to bury her properly.

    ○ The Sheep Trampling the Previous Affair

    In the fifty-ninth year of the Kangxi era, Governor-General Li Shude of Shandong celebrated his birthday. Officials and local administrators each presented sheep and wine as gifts. Plays were performed for several days, and all the officials entertained each other, staying up all night. Mr. Zhang, an expert in legal matters, got drunk and left the banquet to go into a room. About to go to sleep, he heard a whispering sound inside the gauze curtain, as if it were a man and woman engaged in intimate activities. Angered, he thought another official was close to a young actor and had used the bed for such purposes. Shouting, he pulled the curtain, and saw two white sheep kneeling while a man was having sex with them—these were the sheep presented as gifts by the officials. Seeing people, the sheep scattered in fear. Mr. Zhang laughed, thinking it was strange, and told everyone about it.

    191

    少顷，张昏迷仆地，以手自批其颊，骂曰：“老奴可恶！我与谢郎生死因缘，隔四百七十年方得一聚，谈何容易！又被汝惊散。破人婚姻，罪不可饶。”言毕，又自批颊。抚军闻之来视，笑慰之曰：“谢家娘子，何必如此。吾生日本意放生行善，今将尔等数百只尽行放生，听汝配偶，以了夙缘，何如？”张听毕叩首曰：“谢大人。”跃然起矣。此事梁瑶峰相公言。

    　　○鬼神欺人以应劫数本朝定鼎后，有顾姓者妄欲纠常熟、无锡两邑民为乱。有黠者某，知其无益，而难于相禁，乃号于众曰：“某村关帝庙甚灵，盍祷于帝，取周将军铁刀重百二十斤者投河以卜之：沉则败，不可起兵；浮则胜，可以起兵。”其意以为铁刀必沉之物，故试之以阻众也。先祷于神，聚众投刀。刀浮水面，如蕉叶一片。众惊喜，即日揭竿起者数万人。俄而王师至，剿绝无遗。

    Explanation

    191

    A short while later, Zhang fainted and fell to the ground, hitting his own cheeks with his hands and cursing, "Old servant, how despicable! My fateful connection with Xie Lang, after being separated for 470 years, was finally reunited—how difficult that was! You scattered us again. Ruining someone's marriage, the crime cannot be forgiven." After speaking, he hit his cheeks again. The military governor, hearing this, came to see him and comforted him with a smile, saying, "Xie's lady, why act like this? My original intention was to release living beings and do good deeds. Now, I will release hundreds of you, allowing you to find your partners and fulfill your karmic ties. What do you think?" Zhang, upon hearing this, bowed and said, "Thank you, Xie sir." He then sprang up energetically. This story was told by Mr. Liang Yaofeng.

    ○ Ghosts and gods deceive people to fulfill karmic numbers. After the founding of the dynasty, a person surnamed Gu foolishly wanted to incite the people of Changshu and Wuxi to rebel. A clever person, knowing it was futile but unable to stop it, called out to the crowd, "The Guandi Temple in a certain village is very powerful. Why not pray to the god? Take the iron sword of General Zhou, weighing 120 catties, and throw it into the river to predict the outcome: if it sinks, it means failure, and we cannot raise an army; if it floats, it means victory, and we can raise an army." He thought that the iron sword would surely sink, so he used this test to discourage the crowd. First, they prayed to the god, gathered the crowd, and threw the sword. The sword floated on the water, like a piece of banana leaf. The crowd was surprised and delighted, and within the day, thousands of people rose up with raised spears. Soon after, the imperial army arrived and completely eradicated them.

    192

    ○楚陶乾隆丙寅夏，江阴县民徐甲家患黑眚，火焚其突，矢盈于甑，啸嗥无宁夕，里人咸患苦之。时邑令刘君翰长，粤西名士也，祷于神，不应；延羽士赛祈，不应；乃托刘少司空星炜为文，祷于城隍。令斋沐投炉，宿神庑下听命。翌日，无所兆，但炉灰坟起，作“楚陶”二字。令谓曰：“汝岂与楚人陶姓有冤乎？”甲大惊，吐实云：“甲幼年访其宗人某，往武昌，路患恶疾，同行者委之于道，分转沟壑死矣。有一丐者，雄躯深目，分糗食之，携与同乞。月余，病良已。

    Explanation

    192

    During the summer of Qianlong's 45th year (1780), a man named Xu Jia from Jiangyin County in the state of Chu was plagued by a black omen. His home was struck by fire, arrows filled his steamer, and he and his family were in constant distress, howling and wailing without a peaceful night, causing great suffering to the villagers. At the time, the county magistrate, Liu Hanchang, a renowned scholar from western Guangdong, prayed to the gods, but there was no response; he then invited a Taoist priest to perform a ritual, but again, there was no answer. Desperate, he commissioned Liu Xiaosikong Xingwei, a high-ranking official, to write a prayer for the city god. The magistrate purified himself and threw the prayer into the fire, staying overnight under the temple of the deity to await its command. The next day, there were no signs of any omen, but the ashes in the stove rose up like a mound, forming the characters "Chu Tao." The magistrate asked, "Are you in conflict with someone named Tao from Chu?" Xu Jia was greatly shocked and revealed the truth: "When I was young, I visited a relative named Tao in Wuchang. On the way, I fell ill with a severe disease, and my companions abandoned me by the roadside, leaving me to die in the ditches. A beggar with a strong body and deep eyes shared his food with me and carried me along to beg together. After more than a month, my illness was cured."

    193

    丐者以力凌其曹偶，所得独赢，因省啬为甲作归计，竟得归。甲素有心计，为人佣租，得婚娶，且小阜矣。亡何，丐忽至，挟巨橐，颜色窘甚。叩之，曰：“曩别后窜身绿林，浮沉湖，湘间二十载。今事败捕急，请从子而庇焉。”甲唯唯，语其子。子谓：“功令：匿盗者与盗同罪，不如放之使逸。”甲方嗫嚅未决，忽伍伯数人入，絷其人以去，甲大惊。有拍手笑于房者，其子妇也，曰：“大恩不报，新妇知若父子不忍，故已通知捕快，召之人矣。获厚资，且得赏，何惧为？”

    Explanation

    193

    A beggar overpowered his fellow beggars with force, earning more than the rest. He then became frugal and planned to return home, and he finally succeeded in doing so. "Jia" was always clever, working for someone to earn rent, getting married, and becoming slightly prosperous. Not long after, the beggar suddenly arrived, carrying a large bag and looking very distressed. When asked, he said, "After we parted, I hid in the Green Woods, drifting in and out of the lakes and regions around the Xiang River for twenty years. Now, the matter has failed, and the capture is urgent. Please let me follow you and take refuge." "Jia" hesitantly agreed and told his son. The son said, "The law states that those who hide thieves are punished the same as thieves. It would be better not to hide him but to let him escape." "Jia" hesitated and did not make a decision. Suddenly, several soldiers entered, bound the beggar, and took him away, greatly surprising "Jia." Someone in the room clapped and laughed, and it was "Jia"’s son’s wife, who said, "Great favors are not repaid. I knew that you and your father could not bear to do this, so I have already informed the constable, who has summoned him. He will gain a large sum of money and receive a reward. What to fear?"

    194

    民无可奈何，顾常大恨，不意其祟至于此也。“

    　　刘令曰：“盗劫人而子杀盗，盗当其罪，何厉之能为？顾汝享其利，则汝亦盗也。神人乌能庇盗？”无何，祟益甚，毁其家殆尽。子若妇先后卒，崇乃绝。

    　　○藏魂坛云贵妖符邪术最盛。贵州臬使费元龙赴滇，家奴张姓骑马上，忽大呼坠马，左腿失矣。费知妖人所为，张示云：“能补张某腿者，赏若干。”随有老人至，曰：“是某所为。张在省时，倚主人势，威福太过，故与为恶戏。”张亦哀求。

    Explanation

    194

    The people were helpless, but they often deeply resented the situation, unaware that the calamity had reached this point. The magistrate Liu said, "The thief robs someone, and the son kills the thief. The thief should bear his own guilt. What can the resentment achieve? But you enjoy the benefit, so you are also a thief. How can a god or human protect a thief?" Not long after, the calamity became even worse, destroying his family almost entirely. His son and wife died one after another, and the calamity then ceased.

    ○ The ritual of the Soul-Hiding Altar is where the most powerful magical and sorcerous practices are found in Yunnan and Guizhou. When Governor Fei Yuanlong of Guizhou traveled to Yunnan, his servant named Zhang was riding his horse when he suddenly shouted and fell off, losing his left leg. Fei knew it was the work of a sorcerer. Zhang announced, "Anyone who can restore my leg will be rewarded with a certain amount." Soon, an old man arrived and said, "It was I who did this. When Zhang was in the province, he relied on his master's power, indulging in too much authority and pleasure, so I played a trick on him." Zhang also begged for mercy.

    195

    老人解荷包，出一腿，小若蛤蟆，呵气持咒，向张掷之，两足如初，竟领赏去。

    　　或问费公：“何不威以法？”曰：“无益也。在黔时，有恶棍某，案如山积。官府杖杀，投尸于河。三日还魂，五日作恶，如是者数次。诉之抚军。抚军怒，请王命斩之，身首异处。三日后又活，身首交合，颈边隐隐然红丝一条，作恶如初。

    　　后殴其母，母来控官，手一坛曰：“此逆子藏魂坛也。逆子自知罪大恶极，故居家先将魂提出，炼藏坛内。官府所刑杀者，其血肉之体，非其魂也。以久炼之魂，治新伤之体，三日即能平复。今恶续满盈，殴及老妇，老妇不能容。求官府先毁其坛，取风轮扇扇散其魂；再加刑于其体，庶几恶子乃真死矣。‘官如其言，杖毙之。而验其尸，不浃旬已臭腐。”

    Explanation

    195

    An Elderly Man Opens His Bag and Throws a Leg-Like Object at Zhang

    An elderly man opens his bag and takes out a leg-like object, small as a frog. He breathes and chants a spell, then throws it at Zhang. Zhang's feet return to their original state, and the elderly man finally goes to receive his reward.

    Someone asks Fei Gong, "Why not use legal authority to intimidate him?" Fei Gong replies, "It would be of no benefit. During my time in Qian, there was a certain wicked ruffian with cases piled up like mountains. The authorities beat him to death and threw his body into the river. Three days later, he returned to life, and five days later, he committed evil acts. This happened several times. He reported it to the military governor, who was angry and requested the king's command to execute him. His body and head were separated. Three days later, he came back to life, and his body and head reconnected. There was a faint red thread around his neck, and he resumed his evil deeds.

    Later, he beat his mother, and she came to report to the authorities, holding a jar and saying, 'This is the jar where the rebellious son hides his soul. The rebellious son knew he was guilty of great evil, so at home he first extracted his soul and refined it, hiding it inside the jar. The authorities punished his physical body, not his soul. With a soul that had been long refined, he treated his newly injured body, and in three days, he could recover. Now his evil deeds are complete, and he beat an elderly woman. The elderly woman could not tolerate it. She asked the authorities to first destroy his jar, to use a wind wheel fan to scatter his soul; and then punish his body. Perhaps then the wicked son will truly die.' The authorities followed her advice, beat him to death, and when they examined his body, it was already rotten within ten days."

    196

    ○老妪为妖乾隆二十年，京师人家生儿辄患惊风，不周岁便亡。儿病时，有一黑物如鸺留盘旋灯下，飞愈疾，则小儿喘声愈急，待儿气绝，黑物乃飞去。

    　　未几，某家儿又惊风，有侍卫鄂某者，素勇，闻之，怒，挟弓矢相待。见黑物至，射之。中弦而飞，有呼痛声，血涔涔洒地。追之，逾两重墙，至李大司马家之灶下乃灭。鄂挟矢来灶下，李府惊，争来问讯。鄂与李素有戚，道其故，大司马命往灶下觅之。见旁屋内一绿眼妪插箭于腰，血犹淋漓，形若猕猴，乃大司马官云南时带归苗女。最笃老，自云不记年岁。疑其为妖，拷问之，云：“有咒语，念之便能身化异鸟，专待二更后出食小儿脑，所伤者不下数百矣。”李公大怒，捆缚置薪火焚之。嗣后，长安小儿病惊风竟断。

    Explanation

    196

    A Witch in Old Age

    During the 20th year of the Qianlong reign, children born in Beijing often suffered from infantile convulsions, dying before they reached one year of age. When the children were ill, a black object resembling an owl would circle around the lamp. The faster the object flew, the more urgent the child's breathing became. When the child lost breath, the black object would fly away.

    Soon after, another child in a certain family fell ill with convulsions. A brave guard named E, known for his courage, heard about it and became angry. He prepared his bow and arrows to confront the supernatural entity. When he saw the black object approach, he shot it. The arrow pierced the object, causing it to fly, accompanied by cries of pain, and blood dripped continuously to the ground. The guard pursued it, crossing two walls, until it disappeared at the stove of the house of Grand Marshal Li. Guard E arrived at the stove with his arrow, and the Li family was startled, rushing to ask about the matter. Guard E and Grand Marshal Li had a familial connection. He explained the situation, and the Grand Marshal ordered a search at the stove. They found an old woman with green eyes, who had an arrow stuck in her waist, her blood still dripping, and her shape resembling that of a monkey. She was a Miao woman brought back from Yunnan when the Grand Marshal was an official. She was very old and claimed not to remember her age. Suspecting her of being a witch, they interrogated her, and she confessed, saying, "I have a spell. When I recite it, I can transform into a strange bird, waiting until after midnight to go out and eat the brains of children. The number of those injured by me is not less than hundreds." Grand Marshal Li became furious, tied her up, placed her on a pile of firewood, and burned her. Afterward, the disease of infantile convulsions in Chang'an finally stopped.

    197

    ○署雷公婺源董某，弱冠时，暑月昼卧，忽梦奇鬼数辈审视其面，相谓曰：“雷公患病，此人嘴尖，可替代也。”授以斧，纳其袖中。引至一处，壮丽如王者居。立良久，召入，冠冕旒者坐殿上谓曰：“乐平某村妇朱氏，不孝于姑，合遭天殛。

    　　适雷部两将军俱为行雨过劳，现在患病，一时不得其人。功曹辈荐汝充此任，汝可领符前往。“董拜命出，自视足下云生，闪电环绕，公然一雷公矣。顷刻至乐平界，即有社公导往。董立空中，见妇方诟谇其姑，观者如堵。董取抽中斧一击毙之，声轰然，万众骇跪。

    　　归复命，王者欲留供职。以老母辞，王亦不强。问董何业，曰：“应童子试。”

    　　王顾左右取郡县册阅之，曰：“汝某岁可游庠。”遂醒，急语所亲。诣乐平县验之，果然震死一妇，时日悉合。方阅籍时，董窃睨邑试一名为程隽仙，二名为王佩葵，次年皆验。

    Explanation

    197

    A Man from Wuyuan Designated as the Thunder God

    Dong, a man from Wuyuan, was designated as the Thunder God when he was in his youth. One hot summer day, while napping during the day, he suddenly dreamed of several strange ghosts examining his face and saying to each other, "The Thunder God is ill; this man is sharp-tongued and can replace him." They gave him an axe and put it in his sleeve. They led him to a magnificent place, like a king's residence. He stood there for a long time, and they summoned him inside. A man wearing a crown and dragon robe sat on the throne and said, "A woman named Zhu from a village in Le Ping is disrespectful to her grandmother-in-law and should be struck by heaven.

    Just now, the two generals of the Thunder Department were overworked from delivering rain and are now ill, so they cannot find anyone to take their place. The officials recommended you to take this position. You can take the talisman and go." Dong bowed and left. Looking down, he saw clouds forming under his feet and lightning surrounding him, and he was openly a Thunder God. In a moment, he arrived at the boundary of Le Ping, and immediately, a local deity led him there. Dong stood in the air and saw the woman scolding her grandmother-in-law, with a crowd gathered around. He took the axe from the middle and struck her down. The sound was thunderous, and the crowd was terrified and knelt.

    He returned to report, and the king wanted to keep him in service. Dong refused on the grounds of his old mother, and the king did not insist. The king asked Dong what his profession was, and he said, "I am preparing for the junior examination."

    The king looked around and took the register of the prefecture and county to read, saying, "You will pass the examination in a certain year."
