    归至家，卷其袖，犹存数豆，皆黄金也。悔曰：“得毋仙乎！”急奔至弃豆处迹之，豆不见而麻布犹存。以足蹑之，冉冉云生，便觉轻举，见人民村郭，历历从脚下经过。至一处，琼宫绛宇，小青衣侍户外曰：“郎果至矣。”入，扶老妇人出，曰：“吾与汝有宿缘，小女欲侍君子。”篙工谦让非耦。妇人曰：“耦亦何常之有？缘之所在即耦也。我呼渡时，缘从我生；汝肯渡时，缘从汝起。”

    　　言未毕，笙歌酒肴，婚礼已备。篙工居月余，虽恩好甚隆，而未免思家。谋之女，女教仍以足蹑布，可乘云归。篙工如其言，竟归丁水桥。乡亲聚观，不信其从天而下也。

    　　嗣后屡往屡还，俱以一布为车马。篙工之父母恶之，始焚其布，异香屡月不散，然往来从此绝矣。或曰：“姓白者，白虹精也。”

    237

    Translation:

    When he returned home, he rolled up his sleeves and found several beans still inside, which turned out to be gold. He regretted, saying, "Could this be a sign of immortality?" He hurried to the place where he had discarded the beans and searched for them. The beans were gone, but the piece of cloth remained. When he stepped on it, the clouds slowly rose, and he felt as if he were about to ascend. He saw villages and houses clearly passing beneath his feet.

    He arrived at a place with jade palaces and vermilion buildings. A young girl dressed in green, standing outside the door, said, "The master has finally arrived." She led him inside, and an old woman came out, saying, "I have a predestined connection with you, and my daughter wishes to serve you as a wife." The boatman hesitated, feeling that they were not a suitable match. The old woman replied, "What is a suitable match? It is all determined by fate. When I called for a ferry, our fates began; when you agreed to cross, our fates were sealed."

    Before she finished speaking, music, singing, and a feast were already prepared, and the wedding was ready. The boatman stayed for about a month, and although they were deeply affectionate, he still missed his home. He asked the girl, who taught him to step on the cloth again to return home by riding the clouds. The boatman followed her advice and returned to the water bridge in Ding village. The villagers gathered to watch, unable to believe that he had descended from the sky.

    After that, he would often visit and return, using the piece of cloth as his carriage and horse. However, his parents disapproved of him, and they burned the cloth. A strange fragrance lingered for several months but eventually faded. From then on, he stopped visiting altogether. Some say that the white-haired man was a spirit associated with white rainbows.

    238

    ○冷秋江乾隆十年，镇江程姓者，抱布为业，夜从象山归。过山脚，荒冢累累，有小儿从草中出，牵其衣。程知为鬼，呵之，不去。未几，又一小儿出，执其手。前小儿牵其西，西皆墙也，墙上簇簇然黑影成群，以泥掷之；后小儿牵往东，东亦墙也，墙上啾啾然鬼声成群，以沙撒之。程无可奈何，听其牵曳。东鬼西鬼始而嘲笑，继而喧争，程不胜其苦，仆于泥中，自分必死。忽群鬼呼曰：“冷相公至矣！此人读书，迂腐可憎，须避之。”果见一丈夫，魁肩昂背，高步阔视，持大扇击手作拍板，口唱“大江东”，于于然来，群鬼尽散。其人俯视程，笑曰：“汝为邪鬼弄耶！吾救汝。汝可随吾而行。”程起从之，其人高唱不绝。行数里，天渐明，谓程曰：“近汝家矣，吾去矣。”程叩谢问姓名，曰：“吾冷秋江也，住东门十字街。”

    Explanation

    238

    In the year 1745 during the reign of the Qianlong Emperor, a man surnamed Cheng from Zhenjiang, engaged in the business of carrying cloth, returned from Xiang Mountain at night. Passing the foot of the mountain, he came across a cluster of abandoned tombs. Suddenly, a small child emerged from the grass and grabbed his clothes. Recognizing it as a ghost, Cheng scolded it, but the ghost did not leave. Soon after, another small child appeared and took his hand. The first child led him west, where there were walls, and on the walls, a crowd of dark shadows gathered, throwing mud at him; the second child led him east, where there were also walls, and on the walls, a crowd of ghostly sounds gathered, sprinkling sand on him. Cheng had no choice but to let them pull him. The east ghosts and west ghosts started laughing and then arguing, and Cheng could not bear the suffering and fell into the mud, thinking he would surely die. Suddenly, the crowd of ghosts called out, "Cold Scholar has arrived! This person reads books, is old-fashioned and detestable, must be avoided." Indeed, he saw a man with broad shoulders and upright back, with high steps and broad gaze, holding a large fan and hitting it like a clapper, singing "Great River East," coming in a leisurely manner, and the crowd of ghosts all dispersed. The man looked down at Cheng and said with a smile, "Are you being toyed with by evil spirits? I will save you. You can follow me." Cheng got up and followed him, and the man continued to sing without stopping. After walking several miles, the sky gradually became light, and he said to Cheng, "Near your home now, I will leave." Cheng bowed to thank him and asked for his name. The man replied, "I am Cold Autumn River, living on East Gate Cross Street."

    239

    程还家，口鼻窍青泥俱满。家人为薰沐毕，即往东门谢冷姓者，杳无其人。

    　　至十字街问左右邻，曰：“冷姓有祠堂，其中供一木主，名嵋，乃顺治初年秀才。

    Explanation

    239

    Cheng returned home, and his mouth, nose, and orifices were all filled with green mud. The family members finished bathing and fumigating him, and he immediately went to the east gate to thank the person surnamed Leng, but there was no trace of the person.

    He went to the intersection and asked the neighbors on both sides, who said, "The Leng family has a clan hall, inside which there is a wooden tablet. It is named Mei, and he was a scholar from the early years of the Shunzhi era."

    240

    秋江者，其号也。“

    　　○钉鬼脱逃句容捕者殷乾，捕贼有名，每夜伺人于阴僻处。将往一村，有持绳索者贸贸然急奔，冲突其背，殷私忆此必盗也，尾之。至一家，则逾垣入矣。殷又私忆捕之不如伺之。捕之不过献官，未必获赏；伺其出而劫之，必得重利。

    　　俄闻隐隐然有妇女哭声，殷疑之，亦逾垣入。见一妇梳妆对镜，梁上有蓬头者以绳钩之，殷知此乃缢死鬼求代耳，大呼破窗入。邻右惊集，殷具道所以，果见妇悬于梁，乃救起之。妇之公姑咸来致谢，具酒为款。散后，从原路归，天犹未明。背簌簌有声，回顾，则持绳鬼也。骂曰：“我自取妇，干汝何事？而破我法！”以双手搏之。殷胆素壮，与之对搏，拳所着处冷且腥。天渐明，持绳者力渐惫，殷愈奋勇，抱持不释。路有过者见殷抱一朽木，口喃喃大骂，上前谛视，殷恍如梦醒，而朽木亦坠地矣。殷怒曰：“鬼附此木，我不赦木！”取钉钉之庭柱，每夜闻哀泣声，不胜痛楚。

    Explanation

    240

    Autumn River is his name.

    ○ The ghost-nailer who escaped from Juzhong was Yin Gan, a famous thief-catcher. Every night, he would wait for people in dark, secluded places. On his way to a village, he saw someone holding a rope, rushing blindly and colliding with his back. Yin secretly thought this must be a thief and followed him. Upon reaching a house, the person jumped over the wall and entered. Yin then thought that catching him was not as good as waiting for him. Catching him would only mean presenting him to the authorities, but he might not receive a reward; waiting for him to come out and seizing him would surely bring great profit.

    Soon, he heard a faint sound of a woman crying. Yin suspected it and also jumped over the wall to enter. He saw a woman combing her hair in front of a mirror, with a bald-headed figure on the beam holding a rope. Yin realized this was a hanged ghost seeking a replacement, and he shouted loudly, breaking through the window. The neighbors gathered in alarm, and Yin explained the reason. Indeed, he saw the woman hanging from the beam and saved her. The woman's husband and mother-in-law came to thank him, and they prepared wine to entertain him. After the gathering, Yin returned by the same route, but it was still dark. He felt a rustling sound on his back and turned around to see the ghost holding the rope. He shouted, "I am taking the woman myself, what business is this of yours? And you are breaking my method!" He grappled with the ghost with both hands. Yin was naturally courageous and fought back. The place where the fists landed was cold and smelly. As the day grew brighter, the rope holder's strength gradually waned, but Yin fought even more fiercely, holding on tightly without letting go. Someone passing by saw Yin holding a piece of rotten wood, muttering angrily. They approached to see clearly, and Yin suddenly felt as if he had woken from a dream, and the rotten wood also fell to the ground. Yin angrily said, "The ghost is attached to this wood, I will not forgive this wood!" He took a nail and nailed it to the pillar in the courtyard. Every night, he heard the sound of sobbing, unbearable pain.

    241

    过数夕，有来共语者、慰唁者、代乞恩者，啾啾然声如小儿，殷皆不理。中有一鬼曰：“幸主人以钉钉汝，若以绳缚汝，则汝愈苦矣。”群鬼噪曰：“勿言，勿言，恐泄漏机关，被殷学乖。”次日，殷以绳易钉如其法。至夕，不闻鬼泣声。

    　　明旦视朽木，竟遁去。

    　　○樱桃鬼熊太史本，僦居京师之半截胡同，与庄编修令舆居相邻，每夜置酒，互相过从。

    　　八月十二日夜，庄具酒饮熊，宾主共坐。忽桐城相公遣人来招庄去，熊知其即归，独酌待之。自斟一杯置几上，未及饮，杯已空矣。初犹疑己之忘之也，又斟一杯伺之。见有巨手蓝色从几下伸出探杯，熊起立，蓝手者亦起立，其人头、目、面、发，无一不蓝。熊大呼，两家奴悉至，烛照，无一物。庄归闻之，戏熊曰：“君敢宿此乎？”熊年少气豪，即命童奴取被枕置榻上而麾童出，独持一剑坐。

    Explanation

    241

    After several nights, there came those who came to speak, those who came to console, and those who came to beg for mercy, their voices small and continuous like children's, but Yin ignored them all. Among them, one ghost said, "Fortunately, the owner nailed you; if you were tied with a rope, you would suffer even more." The ghosts shouted in unison, "Do not speak, do not speak, for fear of revealing the mechanism, and having Yin learn from it." The next day, Yin replaced the nails with ropes as instructed. By evening, no ghost crying could be heard. The next morning, when looking at the rotten wood, it had disappeared.

    Xiong Taishi Ben, who rented a residence in the half-way lane of the capital, lived next to Zhuang Bianxiu Lingyu. They often visited each other and drank together every night. On the night of the twelfth day of the eighth month, Zhuang prepared wine for Xiong, and they sat together as host and guest. Suddenly, a messenger from the Grand Secretary of Tongcheng came to invite Zhuang away. Xiong knew that Zhuang would return, so he prepared to wait alone. He poured himself a cup of wine and placed it on the table. Before he could drink, the cup was already empty. At first, he thought he had forgotten to drink it, so he poured another cup to see what would happen. He saw a large blue hand reach out from under the table and probe the cup. Xiong cried out, and the servants from both households came, lighting candles to investigate, but found nothing. When Zhuang returned and heard about it, he teased Xiong, saying, "Do you dare to stay here tonight?" Xiong, being young and bold, immediately ordered his servant to fetch bedding and pillows and place them on the bed, then dismissed the servant and held a sword while sitting.

    242

    剑者，大将军年羹尧所赠，平青海血人无算者也。时秋风怒号，斜月冷照，榻施绿纱帐，空明澄澈。街鼓鸣三更，心怯此怪，终不能寐。忽几上铿然掷一酒杯，再铿然掷一酒杯。熊笑曰：“偷酒者来矣。”俄而一腿自东窗进，一目、一耳、一手、半鼻、半口；一腿自西窗进，一目、一耳、一手、半鼻、半口，似将人身当中分锯作两半者，皆作蓝色。俄合为一，ㄦㄦ然怒睨帐中，冷气渐逼，帐忽自开。熊起拔剑砍之，中鬼臂，如着敝絮，了无声响。奔窗逃去，熊追至樱桃树下而灭。

    Explanation

    242

    The sword, a gift from General Nian Gengyao, is one that has shed countless lives in the pacification of Qinghai. At that time, the autumn wind howled fiercely, and the slanted moon cast a cold light. The bed was draped with a green silk canopy, and the atmosphere was clear and transparent. The street drum sounded at the third watch of the night, and the heart was filled with fear of this monster, ultimately unable to sleep. Suddenly, a clinking sound came as a wine cup was thrown onto the table, followed by another clinking sound of a wine cup being thrown. Xiong laughed and said, "The wine thief has arrived." Soon, one leg entered from the east window, with one eye, one ear, one hand, half a nose, and half a mouth; another leg entered from the west window, also with one eye, one ear, one hand, half a nose, and half a mouth, as if the human body had been sawn in half, all of them blue. Soon, they combined into one, glaring angrily at the tent. The cold air gradually pressed in, and the tent suddenly opened by itself. Xiong rose and drew his sword to strike it, hitting the ghost's arm, like hitting old rags, with no sound. It ran to the window and escaped, and Xiong chased it to the cherry tree, where it vanished.

    243

    次早，主人起，见窗外有血痕，急来询问，熊告所以。乃斩樱桃树焚之，尚带酒气。窗外有司阍奴，老矣，既聋且瞽，所卧窗榻乃鬼出入经过处，杳无闻见，鼾声如雷。

    　　熊后年登八旬，长子巡抚浙江，次子监司湖南，常笑谓人曰：“余以胆气、福气胜妖，终不如司阍之聋且瞽尤胜妖也。”

    　　○鼠啮林西仲福建耿藩之变，厦门司马林西仲不降，被缚入狱。西仲平素画一小像，忽被鼠啮断其头，环颈一线如刀截者。家人号哭，以为不祥。未几，王师破耿，出西仲于狱，复其官，加迁三级。西仲还家，家人置酒庆再生。是夕，闻群鼠声啾啾甚忙，扛一物置几上去。视之，所衔去小像之头，共持来还西仲也。

    243

    Translation

    1. Translation of the source text in English, with Chinese characters above the translation.

    次早，主人起，见窗外有血痕，急来询问，熊告所以。乃斩樱桃树焚之，尚带酒气。窗外有司阍奴，老矣，既聋且瞽，所卧窗榻乃鬼出入经过处，杳无闻见，鼾声如雷。

    The next morning, the master woke up, saw bloodstains outside the window, and hurriedly came to inquire. Xiong explained the reason. Then, he cut down the cherry tree and burned it, which still smelled of alcohol. Outside the window was an old gatekeeper who was both deaf and blind. The window he slept by was where the ghost entered and left. He heard and saw nothing, and his snoring was like thunder.

    　　熊后年登八旬，长子巡抚浙江，次子监司湖南，常笑谓人曰：“余以胆气、福气胜妖，终不如司阍之聋且瞽尤胜妖也。”

    Xiong lived to be eighty years old. His eldest son became the governor of Zhejiang, and his second son became an official in Hunan. He often laughed and told people, "I overcame the demon with courage and good fortune, but I was not as fortunate as the gatekeeper who was deaf and blind."

    　　○鼠啮林西仲福建耿藩之变，厦门司马林西仲不降，被缚入狱。西仲平素画一小像，忽被鼠啮断其头，环颈一线如刀截者。家人号哭，以为不祥。未几，王师破耿，出西仲于狱，复其官，加迁三级。西仲还家，家人置酒庆再生。是夕，闻群鼠声啾啾甚忙，扛一物置几上去。视之，所衔去小像之头，共持来还西仲也。

    During the rebellion of the governor of Fujian, Geng, Lin Xizhong, the military governor of Xiamen, did not surrender and was captured and imprisoned. Xizhong had always drawn a small portrait, which was suddenly bitten by a mouse, cutting off its head. A line around the neck like a cut with a knife. The family wailed, believing it was an omen. Not long after, the imperial army defeated Geng, released Xizhong from prison, restored his position, and promoted him three ranks. Xizhong returned home, and the family held a banquet to celebrate his rebirth. That night, he heard a group of mice making a lot of noise, carrying something to the table. When he looked, it was the head of the small portrait, which the mice carried back to him.

    1. A line-by-line explanation of your translation choices, including how you handled challenging components.

    • 次早，主人起，见窗外有血痕，急来询问，熊告所以。乃斩樱桃树焚之，尚带酒气。

      • 次早 - "The next morning"
      • 主人起 - "the master woke up"
      • 见窗外有血痕 - "saw bloodstains outside the window"
      • 急来询问 - "hurriedly came to inquire"
      • 熊告所以 - "Xiong explained the reason"
      • 乃斩樱桃树焚之，尚带酒气 - "then cut down the cherry tree and burned it, which still smelled of alcohol"

    • 窗外有司阍奴，老矣，既聋且瞽，所卧窗榻乃鬼出入经过处，杳无闻见，鼾声如雷。

      • 窗外有司阍奴 - "outside the window was an old gatekeeper"
      • 老矣，既聋且瞽 - "who was both deaf and blind"
      • 所卧窗榻乃鬼出入经过处 - "the window he slept by was where the ghost entered and left"
      • 杳无闻见 - "he heard and saw nothing"
      • 鼾声如雷 - "his snoring was like thunder"

    • 熊后年登八旬，长子巡抚浙江，次子监司湖南，常笑谓人曰：“余以胆气、福气胜妖，终不如司阍之聋且瞽尤胜妖也。”

      • 熊后年登八旬 - "Xiong lived to be eighty years old"
      • 长子巡抚浙江 - "His eldest son became the governor of Zhejiang"
      • 次子监司湖南 - "His second son became an official in Hunan"
      • **常笑
    244

    ●卷七○尹文端公说二事乾隆十五年，尹文端公总督陕西。苏州顾某者，为绥德州知州，貌素丰。是年九月，顾赴西安求见，则羸已甚。尹公疑其病，问之。顾跪而请曰：“某生平读书，从不信鬼神事，况敢妄言于大人前耶！今旦暮将死，不敢不告为身后计。

    　　本年五月初七日，清晨起坐书斋，见一人青衣皂帽持帖入曰：“某官请公会讯，备骑在门。‘视其帖，同寅汤┉也。某即上马出城。北行三十里，至公廨，有古衣冠者迎揖曰：”所以屈公至者，为欲造姓名册送上帝，须与公会办。’某未答，旁一吏跪启：“册草创未就，须八月二十四日方可誉清。‘古衣冠者目皂衣人送某还，约至期勿爽。某复上马，行三十里，入署，见己身僵卧床上，妻子号泣于旁。皂衣者推某身自其口入，格格然如不可复合，四肢筋骨五脏之间，酸楚莫状。

    Explanation

    244

    In the 15th year of the Qianlong reign (1750), Grand Secretary Yin Wenden was the Governor-General of Shaanxi. Gu Mou from Suzhou was the governor of Suizhou, and he was usually plump. In the ninth month of that year, Gu went to Xi'an to seek an audience, but he was already extremely thin. Yin Wenden suspected that he was ill and asked him about it. Gu knelt and pleaded, saying, "I have always read books and never believed in matters of spirits and gods, how could I dare to speak falsely in front of you? I am about to die soon and dare not not tell you for the sake of my afterlife.

    On the 7th day of the fifth month of this year, early in the morning, I sat up in my study and saw a man in blue clothes and a black hat holding a letter saying, 'A certain official invites you to attend a meeting, and a horse is waiting at the door.' Looking at the letter, it was from my colleague Tang. I immediately mounted my horse and left the city. Traveling north for thirty miles, I arrived at the office, where a person in ancient attire greeted me and said, 'We invited you here to create a list of names to send to the Heavenly Emperor, and we need to work together on it.' I had not answered when a clerk knelt and said, 'The draft is not yet completed and will not be finalized until the 24th day of the eighth month.' The person in ancient attire looked at the man in black clothing and sent me back, saying to come on time. I returned on my horse, traveling thirty miles, and entered the office, where I saw myself stiffly lying on the bed, with my wife and children crying beside me. The man in black clothing pushed me into the ghost's mouth, and it felt like it couldn't be put back together, causing intense pain in my limbs, bones, and organs."

    245

    苏醒后始进米饮，自此部署公私。至八月二十四日，晨起即具衣冠，诀别幕友妻子，泣嘱曰：“尸勿寒，且缓殓。‘至午昏晕，类中风者。果皂衣人来，引至前处。古衣冠者坐堂上，列两几于前，如世间会审状，吏逐名点唱，无相识者。至第三名，即本州之皂隶某也；第八十五名，本州之柬房吏某也；其余人，眼中虽甚熟悉，而不知姓名。呼二人到案前问之，亦云：”不知何以到此。’古衣冠者笑曰：“公何问耶？公永当在此共事，自然具晓一切。‘问：”来当何时？’曰：‘今年十月初七日，公趁此时速归部署家事可也。’复拱手别，苏醒如故，身之狼狈，尤甚于前。未几，此县大疫，一吏一役俱染疫亡。今已九月，死期不远，故来诀别大人。“尹公慰之再三，泣拜去。

    Explanation

    245

    After waking up, he began to take rice porridge, and from then on, he arranged public and private affairs. On the twenty-fourth day of August, upon waking up in the morning, he immediately dressed in formal attire and bid farewell to his assistants and their families, weeping and instructing them, "Do not let the body grow cold, and delay the burial for now." In the afternoon, he became dizzy and fainted, like someone suffering from a stroke. Indeed, a person dressed in black came and led him to the previous place. An anciently dressed person sat on the hall, placing two tables in front, as if for a judicial hearing in the world. Officials called out names one by one, and no one was recognized. The third name was a certain bailiff of this prefecture, and the eighty-fifth name was a certain clerk of the letter office of this prefecture. The others, though they seemed very familiar to him, he did not know their names. He called the two to the front of the bench and asked them, and they also said, "We do not know why we are here." The anciently dressed person smiled and said, "Why are you asking me? You will always be here to work together, and naturally you will understand everything." He asked, "When will you come?" and replied, "On the seventh day of the tenth month of this year, you should take this time to quickly return and arrange your family affairs." Again, he bowed and parted, and woke up as before, but his body was in a worse state than before. Not long after, a great epidemic broke out in this county, and one official and one servant were all infected and died. Now it is September, and the time of death is not far away, so I have come to bid farewell to the great man. Governor Yin consoled him repeatedly, and he wept and bowed farewell.

    246

    明年正月，尹公巡边，过绥德州，内幕许孝章者，素知其事，方留心访顾，而顾仍无恙，来谒于辕，体充实如故。公戏之曰：“鬼言何以灵于吏役而不灵于汝耶？”顾叩头谢恩，亦不解其何故。

    　　公督陕时，按华阴县某禀启曰：“为触犯妖神陈情禀死事：卑职三厅前有古槐一株，遮房甚黑，意欲伐之。而邑中吏役佥曰：”是树有神，伐之不可。‘某不信，伐之，并掘其根。根尽，见鲜肉一方；肉下有画一幅，画赤身女子横卧。

    　　卑职心恶之，焚其画，以肉饲犬。是夜，觉神魂不宁，无病而憔悴日甚，恶声汹汹，目无见而耳有闻，自知不久人世，乞大人别委署篆者来。“尹公得禀，袖之与幕客传观曰：”此等禀帖，作何批发？“言未毕，华阴县报病故文书至矣。

    Explanation

    246

    Next year in January, Governor Yin was inspecting the border regions and passed through Suizhou Prefecture. A person named Xu Xiaozhang, who had always known about the matter, was paying close attention and visiting. Xu still appeared to be fine and came to pay respects at the carriage, his body as robust as before. The governor playfully said, "Why do the words of the ghost work on officials but not on you?" Xu knelt and thanked him, but did not understand why.

    When Governor Yin was overseeing Shaanxi, he reviewed a report from a certain official of Huayin County, which stated: "A plea to the authorities due to offending an evil spirit: I have an ancient locust tree in front of my office that shades the house very darkly, and I intended to cut it down. The officials and clerks of the county all said, 'This tree has a spirit, and it cannot be cut down.' I did not believe it and cut it down, and also dug out its roots. After digging up the roots, I saw a piece of fresh meat. Under the meat, there was a painting of a naked woman lying horizontally.

    I felt disgusted by it, burned the painting, and fed the meat to the dogs. That night, I felt restless in spirit, without any illness but growing increasingly frail. There were ominous sounds, and I could see nothing but heard things. I knew I would not live much longer and begged the great man to appoint another official to take over." Governor Yin received the report, put it in his sleeve, and showed it to his advisors, saying, "What should be done with this kind of report?" Before he finished speaking, a report of the death of the official from Huayin County arrived.

    247

    ○霹雳脯海州朱先生，康熙间人，貌三四十岁，或出或隐，不知寒暑。常曰：“海州气象好，惜读书者少耳！”出游数年，归语人曰：“吾家竹子殊博雅，可与谈；山阳阎百诗亦后来之秀，惜其俱未闻道耳！”居亡何，又语人曰：“我何罪于天而今日有雷击我？我不得不相抗。相恐惊诸君，诸君须避之。”至期，云雨海冥，见大蜘蛛脚自空中下，雷乍响而哑矣，旷野有血肉一团，大如车轮。朱指示人曰：“此斗败霹雳脯也。”以酒烹之，独坐而啖。又一日，雷雨复集，朱张口空中，吐白丝数百丈，盘密如网。有火龙腾空而至，奋鬣舒爪于网外，终不能入。良久，入云去。朱叹曰：“海滨多怪物，不可久居，吾将逝矣。”竟去，不知所终。人疑为蜘蛛精也。

    　　○瘟鬼乾隆丙子，湖州徐翼伸之叔岳刘民牧作长洲主簿，居前宗伯孙公岳颁赐第。

    Explanation

    247

    The Thunderbolt Spirit of Hai Zhou, Mr. Zhu, was a man of the Kangxi era. He appeared to be in his thirties or forties, often appearing and disappearing, oblivious to the changing seasons. He would often say, "The atmosphere of Hai Zhou is favorable, but it is a pity that there are few who engage in reading!" After traveling for several years, he returned and told others, "My bamboo is exceptionally refined and cultured, and can be discussed with; Yan Baishi of Shanyang is also an outstanding figure of later times, but it is a pity that neither of them has yet attained the Dao!" Soon after, he said to others, "What sin have I committed against heaven that I am struck by lightning today? I cannot help but resist. I fear that this will frighten you all, so you must avoid it." On the appointed day, the clouds gathered and the sea grew dark, and a giant spider's leg descended from the sky. The thunder suddenly roared and then fell silent, and in the open field, there was a mass of blood and flesh as large as a cartwheel. Mr. Zhu pointed to it and said, "This is the defeated Thunderbolt Spirit!" He cooked it with wine and ate it alone. On another day, thunder and rain gathered again, and Mr. Zhu opened his mouth to the sky, spitting out hundreds of feet of white silk that formed a dense net. A fire dragon soared through the air, raising its mane and stretching its claws outside the net, but it could not enter. After a long while, it ascended into the clouds and disappeared. Mr. Zhu sighed, "There are many strange creatures by the coast, and it is not suitable to stay here for long. I will depart." He left, and his final destination was unknown. People speculated that he was a spider spirit.

    In the year Qianlong's Bingzi, Liu Minmu, the brother-in-law of Xu Yishen from Huzhou, served as the magistrate of Changzhou and lived in the mansion granted by the former Grand Secretary Sun Gong.

    248

    翼伸归湖之便访焉。天暑，浴于书斋，月色微明，觉窗外有气喷入，如晓行臭雾中，几上鸡毛帚盘旋不已。徐拍床喝之，见床上所挂浴布与茶杯飞出窗棂外。窗外有黄杨树，杯触树碎，声铿然。徐大骇，唤家奴出现，见黑影一团，绕瓦有声，良久始息。

    　　徐坐床上，片时，帚又动。徐起，以手握帚，非平时故物，湿软如妇人乱发，恶臭不可近，冷气自手贯臂，直达于肩。徐强忍持之。墙角有声，如出瓮中者，初似鹦鹉学语，继似小儿啼音，称：“我姓吴，名中，从洪泽湖来，被雷惊，故匿于此，求恩人放归。”徐问：“现在吴门大瘟，汝得非瘟鬼否？”曰：“是也。”

    　　徐曰：“是瘟鬼，则我愈不放汝，以免汝去害人。”鬼曰：“避瘟有方，敢献方以乞恩。”徐令数药名而手录之，录毕，不胜其臭，且臂冷不可耐。欲放之，又惧为祟。家奴在旁，各持坛罐，请纳帚而封焉。徐从之，封投太湖。

    Explanation

    248

    Wingchen took the opportunity to visit the lake on his return. On a hot day, he bathed in his study, the moonlight faintly bright. He felt a breath or vapor entering from outside the window, like walking in a fog at dawn. A broom with chicken feathers spun continuously on the desk. Wingchen slapped the bed and shouted, but saw the bath towel and teacup fly out of the window. Outside the window was a locust tree, and the teacup broke against the tree with a loud sound. Wingchen was greatly frightened and called his servants to appear. They saw a dark shadow moving around the roof tiles, making a sound, and it lasted for a long time before it stopped.

    Wingchen sat on the bed, and after a short while, the broom moved again. He got up and took hold of the broom, which was not the usual one. It was wet and soft, like a woman's tangled hair, and emitted a foul odor that was repulsive to touch. A cold sensation traveled from his hand up his arm to his shoulder. Wingchen held it tightly despite the discomfort. A sound came from the corner of the wall, like something emerging from a jar. It initially resembled a parrot speaking and then changed to the cry of a child, saying, "I am surnamed Wu, my name is Zhong. I came from Hongze Lake and was startled by lightning, so I hid here. I ask for your mercy to let me return." Wingchen asked, "Now there is a great plague in Wu Gate. Are you not a plague spirit?" The ghost replied, "Yes."

    Wingchen said, "If you are a plague spirit, then I will not let you go, to prevent you from harming others." The ghost said, "There is a way to avoid the plague. I dare to offer this method to beg for your mercy." Wingchen wrote down the names of the medicines as the ghost recited them. After finishing, he could not bear the odor any longer and his arm was too cold to endure. He wanted to release the ghost but feared it might cause further harm. His servants, standing by, each held a jar and asked him to seal the broom and put it inside. Wingchen agreed, and they sealed the broom and threw it into Taihu Lake.

    249

    所载方：雷丸四两，飞金三十张，朱砂三钱，明矾一两，大黄四两，水法为丸，每服三钱。苏州太守赵文山求其方以济人，无不活者。

    　　○千年仙鹤湖州菱湖镇王静岩，家饶于财，房室高敞。有九思堂，广可五六亩，宴客日暮，必闻厅柱下有声，如敲竹片。静岩恶之，对柱祝曰：“汝鬼耶，则三响。”

    　　乃应四声。曰：“若仙耶，则四响。”乃应五声。曰：“若妖耶，则五响。”乃乱应无数。有道士某来设坛，用雷签插入柱下。忽家中婢头坟起，痛不可忍。道士撒签，婢痛止。间一日，婢忽狂呼，如伤寒发狂者。召医视之，按脉未毕，举足踢医，伤面血流。男子有力者四五人抱持不能禁。王之女初笄，闻婢病，来视之。初入门，大惊仆地，曰：“非婢也。其面方如墙，白色，无眼、鼻、口、耳；吐舌，赤如丹砂，长三四尺，向人翕张。”女惊不已，遂亡。女死而婢愈。

    Explanation

    249

    The recorded formula consists of: Four taels of thunderbolt fungus, thirty sheets of flying gold, three mace of cinnabar, one tael of alum, four taels of rhubarb, all made into pills with water, to be taken three mace at a time. Governor Zhao Wenshan of Suzhou sought this formula to help people, and none failed to recover.

    ○ A thousand-year-old crane, Wang Jingyan of Linhu Town, Huzhou, was rich in wealth, with spacious and lofty rooms. He had a Nine-Think Hall, which was about five or six acres in size. During banquets at dusk, he would always hear a sound under the hall pillars, like bamboo being tapped. Wang Jingyan disliked this, and he spoke to the pillar, saying, "Are you a ghost? Then make three sounds."

    It responded with four sounds. He said, "Are you a deity? Then make four sounds." It responded with five sounds. He said, "Are you an evil spirit? Then make five sounds." It responded in a chaotic manner, with no discernible pattern. A Taoist priest came to set up an altar and inserted a lightning charm into the pillar. Suddenly, a maid in the house had her head swell up, causing her unbearable pain. The Taoist priest removed the charm, and the maid's pain subsided. The next day, the maid suddenly screamed wildly, as if she had a fever and was delirious. A doctor was called to examine her, but before he could finish feeling her pulse, she kicked him with her foot, causing him to bleed. Four or five strong men tried to restrain her but could not. Wang's daughter, who had just come of age, heard about the maid's illness and came to see her. As soon as she entered, she was shocked and fell to the ground, saying, "It is not the maid. Her face is flat like a wall, white, without eyes, nose, mouth, or ears; she has a tongue that is red like cinnabar, about three to four feet long, flapping towards people." The daughter was too shocked to bear it and died. The daughter's death coincided with the maid's recovery.

    250

    王百计驱妖，有请乩仙者来，言“仙人草衣翁甚灵，可以镇邪”。王如其言，设香案置盘。乩笔砉刂然有声，穿窗而出，于窗纸上大书曰：“何苦何苦，土地受过。”主人问乩，乩言：“草衣翁因地邪未去，遽请仙驾将当方土地神发城隍笞二十矣。”自后此妖寂然。

    　　草衣翁与人酬酢甚和，所言多验。或请姓名，曰：“我千年仙鹤也，偶乘白云过鄱阳湖，见大黑鱼吞人。予怒而啄之，鱼伤脑死。所吞人以姓名假我，以状貌付我，我今姓陈，名芝田，草衣者，吾别字也。”或请见之，曰：“可。”请期，曰：“在某夜月明时。”至期，见一道士立空中，面白微须，冠角巾，披晋唐服饰，良久，如烟散也。

    Explanation

    250

    The ruler used every means to drive away the demon, and invited a medium to come, who said, "The immortal old man in grass clothes is very effective and can suppress evil." The ruler followed his advice and set up an incense table and placed a tray. The divining brush made a sharp sound, passing through the window and writing in large characters on the window paper, "Why the trouble, why the trouble, the land god has been punished." The host asked the medium, who replied, "The grass-clothed old man punished the local land god because the evil in the land had not been removed, and hastily invited the immortal to come, which will result in the local land god being beaten by the city god twenty times." Afterward, the demon was silent.

    The grass-clothed old man was very harmonious in his social interactions, and what he said was mostly verified. Someone asked for his name, and he replied, "I am a thousand-year-old immortal crane, who accidentally rode on a white cloud over Poyang Lake and saw a large black fish swallowing a person. I was angry and pecked it, causing the fish to die with a brain injury. The person swallowed by the fish gave me his name, and his appearance was given to me. I am now surnamed Chen, given name Zhiti. Grass-clothed is my pseudonym." Someone asked to see him, and he said, "Yes." They asked for a date, and he said, "On a certain night when the moon is bright." On that night, they saw a Taoist standing in the air, with a white face and a little beard, wearing a hat with a peak and clothes from the Jin and Tang dynasties. After a long time, he dispersed like smoke.

    251

    ○夏太史三事高邮夏醴谷先生督学湖南，舟过洞庭，值大风浪，诸船数千，泊岸未发。夏性急，欲赶到任日期，命舵工逆风而行，诸船随之扬帆。至湖心，风愈大，天地昏冥，白浪如山，见水面二短人，长尺许，面目微黑，掠舟指橹似巡逻者。诸船中人俱见之。风定日出，渐隐去矣。

    　　公居督学衙门，家丁子弟白日见怪，见者必病。公夫人扃闭子弟，午后不许至园；嘱公致祭，公不信。是夜，阅卷灯下，闻哭声自西来，殷殷田田，群响杂沓；飞沙打窗，如雨而下。公厉声曰：“吾已悉尔意，明日祭汝可也！”其声渐远而灭。公诘朝寻其声来之处，有破屋一间，木主数十，皆前任学臣阅卷幕友卒于署者，因为文具牲牢祭之，此后怪绝。

    Explanation

    251

    Three Events of the Summer Historian

    Mr. Xia Ligu, the supervisor of education in Hunan, was traveling by boat across Dongting Lake when he encountered a fierce storm. There were thousands of ships anchored at the shore, having not yet set sail. Xia, known for his impatience, wanted to reach his post on time, so he ordered the helmsman to sail against the wind. The other ships followed and raised their sails. As they reached the center of the lake, the wind grew even stronger, and the sky and earth became dark. White waves surged like mountains, and they saw two short figures on the water, about a foot tall, with slightly dark faces. These figures passed by the boats, pointing at the oars as if they were patrolling. All the people on the other ships saw them. When the wind calmed and the sun came out, the figures gradually disappeared.

    The official resided in the supervisory academy. His household servants and children saw strange things during the day, and those who saw them would fall ill. His wife locked the children in and forbade them from going to the garden after noon. She asked him to offer a sacrifice, but he did not believe. That night, while reading papers by the lamp, he heard crying sounds coming from the west, deep and continuous, with many mixed sounds. Sand flew and hit the windows, falling like rain. The official sternly said, "I have understood your meaning; I will offer a sacrifice to you tomorrow!" The sound gradually faded and disappeared. The next morning, he searched for the source of the sound and found a broken house with dozens of wooden tablets, all of which were the deceased assistants of former supervisory officials who had died in the office. He wrote an article and prepared offerings to sacrifice to them, and after that, the strange phenomena ceased.

    252

    公门生朱士从福建入部，至山东荏平道中，日暮夜宿，风雨交至，遣家人先行觅店，停车于三叉路口待之。夜二更，天地昏黑，见远树中火光忽上忽下，疑为家人持火至矣。少顷，火光渐近，大如车轮，错落数十，高者至苍天，低者及马足。大骇，以为必非人灯。近视之，光火中有三人掠车而过，其中行者当额闪闪有眼，朱衣博带，眉须伟然；旁侍儿锦衣玉貌，扶之而行；最前一白须老翁，伛偻先驱，背穴有孔如碗大，火光从此孔出，如灶突泄烟者然，见人了无惊异，徐步入远村而没。少顷，家人与店家至，云共见之，相与诧骇而已。

    　　○石崇老奴才康熙间，任雨林进士有诗名，宰河南巩县。昼卧书室，见簪花女郎持名纸称石大夫招饮。舆夫盈门，俱来迎接，任不觉身随之行。良久，至一府，闳巍然，主人戴晋巾，锦プ，叉手出迎，谈论风发。坐定，席设水陆奇珍，皆目所未睹，女乐二人，舞亻参々然。

    Explanation

    252

    A Disciple of a Public Official Named Zhu Shigang, from Fujian, was traveling to the capital when he reached the road leading to Renping in Shandong. As night fell, he stopped to rest, and heavy rain and wind began to blow. He sent his family ahead to find an inn and parked his carriage at a three-way intersection, waiting for them. At the second watch of the night, the sky and ground were dark, and he saw a flickering light in the distant trees, which he initially thought was his family holding a torch. Soon, the light approached, large as a carriage wheel, with dozens of flames, some reaching the sky and others touching the horse's hooves. He was greatly alarmed, thinking it must not be a human-made light. Upon closer inspection, he saw three figures passing by the carriage, each with a flickering light. The middle figure had a glowing eye on his forehead, dressed in a red robe and wide sash, with a majestic appearance; beside him were two attendants in splendid attire and beautiful faces, supporting him. The figure in front was an elderly man with a white beard, bent over, leading the way. His back had a large hole like a bowl, from which the fire emerged, like smoke escaping from a stove. He saw people without any surprise, slowly walking into a distant village and disappearing. Soon after, his family and the innkeeper arrived, saying they had all seen it, and they were all amazed and frightened.

    ○ An Old Slave of Shi Chong During the Kangxi Era, Jinshi Ren Yulin was a renowned poet and served as the magistrate of Gong County in Henan. One day, while resting in his study during the day, he saw a flower-adorned young lady holding a name card and saying that Shi Daifu had invited him to drink. Servants filled the courtyard, all coming to greet him, and Ren Yulin found himself following them. After a while, they arrived at a grand mansion, with the host wearing a Jin cap, dressed in a brocade robe, and greeting with crossed hands. They talked animatedly. When they sat down, the table was set with rare delicacies from both land and sea, all of which he had never seen before. Two female entertainers performed a graceful dance.

    253

    酒酣，主人起，握任手行至后园，极亭台花木之胜。园后有井，水绿色，主人手黄金勺呼左右：“酌水为任公解酲。”任初沾唇，觉有辛恶之味，唇为之焦，因辞谢不举其勺。主人强之，众美人伏地劝请，任不得已为尽之。俄而，腹痛欲裂，呼号求归。主人拱手曰：“客果醉矣，且暂别再会。”任仓皇登车，痛愈甚，从原路归。过城隍庙，城隍神趋出迎，曰：“石季伦老奴才又毒人乎！昨作主饮君者，晋石崇也。崇生时取精多，用物宏；诛死时受孙秀屠割，血肉狼藉；强魂不散，为罗刹尊神，誓杀名士三千，以泄生平好名之忿。吾第十九人，君第二十九人也。吾以生平正直，诉冤上帝。帝不能救，封为城隍神，赐药二丸，曰：‘有真名士被害者，以此救之。’君有文行，故在此相救。”言毕，取药塞任口中，任痛遽止。顷刻，汗出而寤。其原卧之处，家人环泣，已迷懵二日矣。

    Explanation

    253

    After the guests had drunk to the point of intoxication, the host stood up, took Ren's hand, and led him to the garden, where they admired the beauty of the pavilions, terraces, and flowers. Behind the garden was a well, and the water was green. The host held a golden spoon and called for his attendants: "Pour some water to help Ren sober up." As soon as Ren touched the water to his lips, he felt a bitter and unpleasant taste, and his lips began to burn. He politely declined to take more, but the host insisted, and the many beautiful women on the ground urged him to drink. Ren, unable to refuse, finished the water. Soon after, he felt intense abdominal pain, shouting and crying out for home. The host bowed politely and said, "You are indeed drunk, let's part ways for now and meet again later." Ren hurriedly got into his carriage, but his pain only worsened as he returned by the same route. As he passed by the city god's temple, the city god emerged to greet him, saying, "Is this old rascal Shi Chong trying to poison people again! The one who served as the host and made you drink was Jin Shi Chong. Shi Chong took much from others and used things extravagantly during his life; he was brutally killed and dismembered by Sun Xiu, with his blood and flesh scattered everywhere. His strong soul did not dissipate and became a demon god, vowing to kill three thousand famous scholars to vent his lifelong resentment over his reputation. I am the nineteenth person, and you are the twenty-ninth. Due to my lifelong integrity, I appealed to the Heavenly Emperor for justice, but the Heavenly Emperor could not save me. I was enfeoffed as a city god and given two pills, saying, 'For any true scholar who is harmed, use this to save them.' You have both literary talent and moral conduct, so I am here to save you." After speaking, the city god took the medicine and placed it in Ren's mouth. Ren's pain immediately subsided. In a moment, he broke out in sweat and woke up. His family, gathered around him, wept, and he had been in a daze for two days.

    254

    后修巩县故城，掘地得碑，镌“金谷”两大字，类索幼安笔法，始知石氏金谷不在今洛阳也。

    Explanation

    254

    After repairing the old city of Gongxian, they dug into the ground and found a stele inscribed with the two large characters "Jingu," which resembled the calligraphy of Suo You'an. It was then known that the Jingu of the Shi family was not in the present-day Luoyang.

    255

    ○鬼差贪酒杭州袁观澜，年四十，未婚。邻人女有色，袁慕之，两情属矣。女之父嫌袁贫，拒之。女思慕成瘵卒。袁愈悲悼，月夜无以自解，持酒尊独酌。见墙角有蓬首人手持绳，若有所牵，睨而微笑。袁疑为邻之差役，招曰：“公欲饮乎？”其人点头，斟一杯与之，嗅而不饮。曰：“嫌寒乎？”其人再点头。热一杯奉之，亦嗅而不饮。然屡嗅则面渐赤，口大张不能复合。袁以酒浇入其口，每酒一滴，则面一缩，尽一壶，而身面俱小，若婴儿然，痴迷不动。牵其绳所缚者，邻氏女也。袁大喜，具酒罂取蓬首人投而封之，面八卦镇压之，解女子缚，与入室为夫妇。夜有形交接，昼则闻声而已。

    　　逾年，女子喜告曰：“吾可以生矣！且为君作美妻矣。明日某村女气数已尽，吾借其尸可活，君以为功，兼可得资财作奁费。”袁翌日往访某村，果有女气绝方殓，父母号哭。袁呼曰：“许为吾妻，吾有药能使还魂！”其家大喜，许之。

    Explanation

    255

    A Ghostly Messenger's Love Affair

    In Hangzhou, there was a man named Yuan Guanlan, who was forty years old and unmarried. A neighbor's daughter was beautiful, and Yuan admired her; their feelings for each other were mutual. However, the girl's father disapproved of Yuan due to his poverty and refused the match. The girl, pining for Yuan, fell ill with consumptive disease and died. Yuan was deeply saddened and, on a moonlit night, unable to console himself, held a wine vessel and drank alone. He saw a bald-headed person standing in the corner of the wall, holding a rope as if leading someone, glancing and smiling. Yuan suspected this person was a neighbor's messenger and called out, "Do you wish to drink?" The person nodded, and Yuan poured a cup of wine for him. The person sniffed but did not drink. Yuan asked, "Are you cold?" The person nodded again. Yuan then warmed a cup of wine and offered it, but the person sniffed it and still did not drink. However, after sniffing the wine several times, the person's face turned red, and his mouth opened wide, unable to close. Yuan poured wine into the person's mouth, and with each drop, the person's face shrank. After finishing a whole jar of wine, the person's body and face became small, like an infant, dazed and immobile. The person being led by the rope was the neighbor's daughter. Yuan was greatly pleased and prepared a wine jar, captured the bald-headed person, sealed him with the Eight Trigrams, untied the girl, and brought her into his room to be his wife. At night, they had physical contact, but during the day, only sounds were heard.

    A year later, the woman happily told Yuan, "I can live again! And I will be your beautiful wife. Tomorrow, a girl in a certain village will have exhausted her life force. I can borrow her body to revive myself. You can claim credit for saving her, and we can also acquire wealth for our dowry." The next day, Yuan went to visit the village and found a girl who had just died and was being prepared for burial, with her parents wailing. Yuan called out, "Agree to be my wife, and I have a medicine that can bring her back to life!" The girl's family was overjoyed and agreed.

    256

    袁附女耳低语片时，女即跃起，合村惊以为神，遂为合卺。女所记忆，皆非本家之事。逾年，渐能晓悉，貌较美于前女。

    　　○李倬李倬者，福建人，乾隆庚午贡生，赴京乡试，路过仪征。有并舟行者，自称姓王名经，河南洛阳县人，赴试京师，资费不足，求李挈带。李许之。同舟言笑甚欢，出所作制艺，亦颇清雅，惟篇幅稍短耳。与共食，必撒饭于地，每举碗，但嗅其气，无一粒纳喉者。李疑而憎之。王似解意，谢曰：“某染膈症，致有此累，幸毋相恶。”既至京师，将赁寓所。王长跪请曰：“公毋畏，我非人也。乃河南洛阳生员，有才学，当拔贡，为督学某受赃黜落，愤激而亡，今将报仇于京师，非公不能带往。入京城时，恐城门神阻我，需公低声三呼我名，方能入。”

    Explanation

    256

    A girl named Yuan Fu whispered in the ear of another girl for a while, and the girl immediately jumped up. The entire village was surprised and regarded it as a miracle, and thus they were married. What the girl remembered were not events from her own family. After a year, she gradually began to understand, and she became more beautiful than the previous girl.

    ○ Li Zhuo, a scholar from Fujian who passed the provincial examination in the year Gengwu during the Qianlong era, traveled to the capital for the imperial examination, passing through Yizheng. There was someone traveling by boat with him who introduced himself as Wang Jing, a native of Luoyang County, Henan Province, who was also going to the capital for the examination but lacked the funds. He asked Li Zhuo to accompany him. Li Zhuo agreed. They spoke and laughed together on the boat, and Wang Jing showed him his literary compositions, which were quite elegant, though the pieces were somewhat short. When they ate together, Wang Jing would always scatter food on the ground, and when he lifted his bowl, he would only smell the air without putting any food in his mouth. Li Zhuo found this strange and disliked him. Wang Jing seemed to understand and apologized, saying, "I am afflicted with a diaphragmatic condition, which has caused this inconvenience. I hope you will not hold it against me." After arriving in the capital, they were about to rent a lodging. Wang Jing knelt and begged, "Sir, do not be afraid. I am not a human being. I was a scholar from Luoyang, Henan, with talent and learning, and should have been selected as a scholar-official, but I was dismissed due to bribery by a censor. I died in anger and frustration. Now I am coming to seek revenge in the capital, and only you can accompany me. When we enter the city, the guardian deities of the city gates may block me. You will need to call my name three times in a low voice for me to enter."

    257

    其所称督学某，即李之座师。李大骇，拒之。鬼曰：“公党师拒我，我行且祟公。”

    　　李无奈何，如其言。

    　　舍馆定，即往谒座主。其家方环泣，声达户外。座主出曰：“老夫有爱子，生十九年矣，聪明美貌，为吾宗之秀。前夜忽得疯疾，疾尤奇，持刀不杀他人，专杀老夫，医者莫名其病，奈何？”李心知其故，请曰：“待门生入视郎君。”

    Explanation

    257

    The supervisor he mentioned is actually R's mentor. Li was greatly alarmed and refused him. The ghost said, "If you ally with your teacher and reject me, I will bring misfortune upon you."

    Having no choice, Li followed his advice.

    After settling in his lodging, he immediately went to visit his mentor. His family was weeping all around, and the sound could be heard outside the door. The mentor came out and said, "I have a beloved son who is nineteen years old, intelligent and handsome, the best among my clan. Last night, he suddenly contracted a strange madness, a disease so peculiar that he holds a knife and only kills me, not others. The doctors cannot diagnose his illness, what can be done?" Li knew the cause and said, "Wait for me to examine your son."

    258

    言未毕，其子在内笑曰：“吾恩人至矣，吾当谢之，然亦不能解我事也。”李入室，握郎君手，语移时。旁人不解，更骇愕，都来问李，李告之故。于是举家跪李前，求为关说。李谓其子曰：“君过矣。君以被黜之故，气忿身死，毕竟非吾师杀君也。今若杀其郎君，绝其血食，殊非以直报怨之道。况吾与君有香火情，独不为我地乎？”其子语塞，嗔目曰：“公语诚是，然汝师当日得赃三千，岂能安享？吾败之而去足矣。”手指曰：“某室有玉瓶，价值若干，为我取来。”至则掷而碎之，又手指曰：“某箱内有貂裘数领，价值若干，为我取来。”至则举火焚之。事毕，大笑曰：“吾无恨矣。为汝赦老奴。”拱手作去状，其子霍然病已。

    Explanation

    258

    Before the words were finished, his son inside laughed and said, "My benefactor has arrived; I should thank him, but he cannot resolve my affairs." Li entered the room, grasped the young master's hand, and spoke for some time. The bystanders did not understand, even more shocked and surprised, and all came to ask Li, who told them the reason. Thus, the whole family knelt before Li, asking him to intervene on their behalf. Li said to his son, "You are wrong. Because you were dismissed, you died in anger, but it was not my master who killed you. Now, if you kill his young master, cut off his ancestral sacrifices, it is not the way of justice to repay resentment. Especially since I and you have a spiritual connection, would you not consider my position?" His son was at a loss for words and glared, saying, "Your words are indeed true, but your master received a bribe of three thousand; how could he enjoy it peacefully? I will ruin him and leave." He pointed and said, "There is a jade bottle in a certain room, worth how much? Bring it to me." When it arrived, he threw it and broke it. Then he pointed again, saying, "There are several fur coats in a certain box, worth how much? Bring them to me." When they arrived, he lit a fire and burned them. After the matter was done, he laughed and said, "I have no regrets. For you, I will pardon the old servant." He bowed his hands and made a gesture of departure, and his son suddenly recovered from his illness.

    259

    李是年登第，行至德州，见王君复至，则前驱巍峨，冠带尊严，曰：“上帝以我报仇甚直，命我为德州城隍，尚有求于吾子者。德州城隍为妖所凭，篡位血食垂二十年，我到任时，彼必抗拒，吾已选神兵三千，与妖决战。公今夜闻刀剑声，切勿谛视，恐有所伤。邪不胜正，彼自败去，但非公作一碑记晓谕居民，恐四方未必崇奉我也。公将来爵禄亦自非凡，与公诀矣。”言毕拜谢，垂泪而去。

    Explanation

    259

    In the year Li passed the imperial examinations, he traveled to Dezhou. Upon arriving, he saw Wang again, who appeared in a grand procession, dressed in dignified attire. Wang said, "Heaven has avenged me in a very just manner and appointed me as the city god of Dezhou. There is still something I need from you. The city god of Dezhou has been possessed by a demon for nearly twenty years. When I took office, the demon resisted, but I have already chosen three thousand divine soldiers to fight against the demon. Tonight, you will hear the sounds of swords and spears; do not look closely, as you might be harmed. Evil cannot overcome righteousness, and the demon will be defeated. However, unless you write a stele to inform the residents, the demon may not be fully defeated and might still be revered in other places. Your rank and salary will also be extraordinary. Farewell."

    260

    是夜，闻城内外兵马喧然，至五鼓始寂。李诘朝往城隍庙焚香作记，道士已磨墨相待，云：“昨夜大王到任，托梦贫道，教相迎也。”李为镌石立碑，今犹存德州大东门外。

    　　○王将军妾苏州慕崇士，宰河南汲县。未遇时，馆京师任姓家，寓半截胡同。晚间独宿，灯下见物黑而毛，攫其书簏。慕手剑逐之，无所得。次晚，月下如厕，有女子冉冉来。慕疑主人婢妾，蹲不敢起。女竟不去，而冷风凄然。慕始惊惧，投以瓦，了不复见。慕踉跄归至书斋，则女子在床矣；军装持刀，容貌甚丽；呼之不应，驱之不去；召他人观之，皆不能见。慕遂病，呓语曰：“我明朝王将军妾也，久不得祭，故遣儿辈取食，汝以剑伤之；我亲来谢过，汝又蹲厕辱我。我故来索命。”

    Explanation

    260

    That night, Li heard the sound of military activity within and outside the city walls, which continued until the fifth watch of the night when it became quiet. The next day, Li went to the city god temple to burn incense and write an inscription. The Taoist priest had already ground ink to welcome him and said, "Last night, the city god arrived in office and had a dream, instructing me to welcome you." Li had the inscription carved on a stone and erected as a monument, which still exists outside the eastern gate of Dezhou.

    ○ Wang General's concubine, Suzhou Mu Chongshi, served as the magistrate of Jixian County in Henan. Before his rise to power, he was a guest at the home of a family surnamed Ren in the capital, residing in Halfway Alley. One evening, while staying alone, he saw a dark, furry creature snatch his book case. Mu chased it with his sword but failed to catch it. The next evening, under the moonlight, while going to the toilet, a woman appeared slowly. Mu suspected her to be a servant or concubine of the household, so he crouched down and did not dare to stand up. The woman did not leave, and a cold wind blew. Mu became frightened and threw a tile at her, but she disappeared without a trace. Mu staggered back to his study, where he found the woman lying on the bed; she was dressed in military attire, holding a sword, and was very beautiful. He called out to her, but she did not respond, and he could not drive her away. When he called others to see her, they could not see her at all. Mu then fell ill and muttered in his sleep, saying, "I am the concubine of the Ming dynasty general Wang. For a long time, I have not been properly honored, so I sent my children to fetch food. You injured one of them with your sword; I came personally to apologize, but you humiliated me by squatting in the toilet. That is why I have come to take your life."

    261

    同寓宾客俱为哀祈，女曰：“能以衣服车马送我归故乡，姑贷汝。”众如其言，慕苏醒。食粥未半晌，女又复来曰：“吾为汝辈所给，衣服领袖并未裁缝，吾何以为衣耶？可速选缝人善治之。”众客愈骇，视所陈之衣，果未开摺也。整治再拜，慕竟病除。

    　　三年，慕登进士，选河南汲县知县，路过开封，宿客店。店之西偏，扃室甚固，慕疑之。窥窗隙，见朱棺一口，横于中堂，凝尘数寸，棺之前和题曰：“王将军亡妾张氏。”慕大惊且悔，心郁郁不乐。薄暮，女果至，装束如前，曰：“昔妾逼君，妾之罪也；今君窥妾，妾之缘也。妾在此数十年，非取人见代，不能自拔于幽冥，故今夜来伴君。”慕大惧，连夜呼驺入城，告开封同寅，将求道士驱之。开封守令留饮达旦，翌早与共至店中，一书童自缢于床。守令怒，剖其棺，尸装束鲜浓，僵而不腐。焚之，竟无他怪。

    Explanation

    261

    The guests staying in the same inn all mourned and prayed for him. The woman said, "If you can send me back to my hometown with clothes and carriages, I will forgive you." The guests did as she said, and Mu regained consciousness. After eating porridge for only a short while, the woman came back and said, "I am being given by you, but the clothes and collars have not been sewn; how can I wear them? You must quickly find a seamstress to finish them." The guests were even more startled, and upon examining the clothes, they indeed found them uncut and unwrapped. They made repairs and bowed twice, and Mu's illness was cured.

    Three years later, Mu passed the imperial examination and was appointed as the magistrate of Jixian County in Henan. He passed through Kaifeng and stayed at an inn. In the western part of the inn, there was a locked room that was very secure, and Mu suspected it. Looking through the window crack, he saw a red coffin lying in the middle of the hall, covered with several inches of dust, and the front of the coffin was inscribed with "Wang General's deceased concubine Zhang." Mu was greatly shocked and regretful, and his heart was full of sadness and unease. At dusk, the woman indeed arrived, dressed as before, and said, "In the past, I forced you, which was my fault; now you see me, which is our fate. I have been here for several decades, and unless someone takes my place, I cannot escape from the underworld. Therefore, I am coming tonight to accompany you." Mu was greatly afraid and called for a carriage to enter the city, informing his fellow officials in Kaifeng about his intention to seek a Taoist to exorcise the ghost. The governor and magistrate of Kaifeng invited him to drink until dawn, and the next morning they went to the inn together. A servant boy hanged himself on the bed. The governor and magistrate were angry and opened the coffin; the body was dressed in bright and fresh clothes, stiff but not decayed. They burned it, and there were no other strange occurrences.

    262

    ○仙鹤扛车方绮亭明府作令江西，其同僚郭姓者，四川人，言少时曾上峨嵋山，意欲弃世学道，见老翁长髯秀貌，戴羽巾，飘飘然导之前行。至一处，宫殿巍峨，似王者居，翁指示曰：“汝欲学道，非王命不可。王外出未归，汝少待。”俄而仙乐嘹嘈，异香触鼻，两仙鹤扛水精车，车中坐王者，状如世上所画香孩儿，红衣文葆，洁白如玉，口嬉嬉微笑，长不满尺许，诸神俯伏迎入宫。老翁奏曰：“有真心学道人郭求见。”王命传入，注视良久，曰：“非仙才，速送回人间。”老翁掖郭下。郭问曰：“王何以年少？”老翁笑曰：“为仙为圣为佛，及其成功，皆婴儿也。汝不闻孔子亦儒童菩萨，孟子云：”大人者，不失其赤子之心‘乎？吾王已五万岁矣！“郭无奈何，仍自山下归家，犹记其殿门外朱书二对，云：”胎生卵生湿生化生，生生不已；天道地道人道鬼道，道道无穷。“

    Explanation

    262

    The Official Fang Qiting, while serving as the magistrate of Jiangxi, recounted that his colleague, a man surnamed Guo from Sichuan, once mentioned during his youth that he had ascended Mount Emei with the intention of renouncing the world and seeking the Dao. He encountered an elderly man with a long, handsome beard, wearing a feathered headband, who led him along in a light and ethereal manner. They arrived at a place where a magnificent palace stood, resembling the residence of a king. The old man pointed and said, "If you wish to learn the Dao, it must be with the king's permission. The king is currently out and has not returned; please wait a moment." Soon after, celestial music filled the air, and a fragrant aroma wafted through the nose. Two cranes carried a crystal chariot, upon which sat a ruler, resembling the divine child often depicted in the world, dressed in a red robe and adorned with elaborate embroidery, his skin as white as jade, smiling gently, no more than a foot tall. Various gods bowed and greeted him as he was led into the palace. The old man then reported, "There is a sincere seeker of the Dao named Guo who wishes to see the king." The king ordered him to be brought in and examined him closely before saying, "You do not possess the talent to become a sage or immortal; quickly send him back to the human world." The old man escorted Guo out. Guo asked, "Why is the king so young?" The old man laughed and replied, "Whether one becomes a sage, saint, or Buddha, upon achieving enlightenment, they all return to the state of an infant. Have you not heard that Confucius was also known as the 'Buddha of the Scholarly Child,' and Mencius said, 'The great person does not lose the heart of an infant'? Our king is already five thousand years old!" Guo had no choice but to descend the mountain and return home, still remembering the two pairs of red inscriptions on the palace gate, which read: "Born from a womb, born from an egg, born from moisture, born from transformation, life continuously arises; the heavenly way, the earthly way, the human way, the ghostly way, each path is endless."

    263

    ○红花洞溧水知县曹江初官蜀时，夏日昼寝，见二隶卒牵马来邀，与俱行，约二十里馀，复有一人乘骏马，约束如军官，持令箭呼曰：“奉上帝命，烦君点放洞犯，幸勿辞劳。”曹愕然，莫知其故。再行二三里，至深山，有穴，榜曰“红花洞”。

    Explanation

    263

    Red Flower Cave: When Cao Jiang, the magistrate of Lishui, first served in Shu, during a summer day nap, he saw two low-ranking officials come to invite him with horses. They accompanied him for about twenty miles, and then another person, riding a fine horse and dressed like a military officer, held a command arrow and called out, "In accordance with the command of the Heavenly Emperor, I request your assistance in inspecting and releasing the prisoners from the cave. Please do not hesitate." Cao Jiang was startled and did not understand the reason. After traveling another two or three miles, they arrived at a deep mountain where there was a cave. Above the cave, a sign read "Red Flower Cave."

    264

    石门一双，封钥甚固。洞口胥吏七八人，具公案文册，跪迎道左。军官以令箭付曹，嘱云：“照册点放。”言毕，乘马去。

    　　曹登座，一吏禀请启洞，向洞大呼“开门”者三，有阴气随呼而出，冷逼毛发。须臾，女鬼数千，蓬首垢面，纷然杂至，哀号困苦之声，不可言状。吏按册唱名，开锁具，驱向南行。诸鬼逡巡，若不得已而往者。最后三女鬼向曹哀求免放，曹辞以“奉帝命，不能为力”，三鬼愤惋骂曰：“二十年后，会当相报！”

    　　放既毕，军官复来嘱隶曰：“曹公劳矣，须好送还家。”隶卒仍以马送。至中途，经大河，马渡水，忽失前足而堕，惊寤，见家人环哭，方知已死一日，心秘其事，不敢言于人。

    Explanation

    264

    A pair of stone gates stood sealed and locked very securely. At the entrance of the cave, several officials knelt to welcome on the left side of the path, with official desks and documents before them. The officer handed over an imperial command to Cao and instructed him, saying, "Release according to the register." After giving his instructions, he rode away on his horse.

    Cao took his seat, and one official requested permission to open the cave, calling out "Open the door" three times toward the cave. A cold, ghostly vapor came out with the calls, making the hair stand on end. Soon, thousands of female ghosts, with disheveled hair and dirty faces, arrived in a disordered and mixed manner, their sounds of wailing and suffering beyond description. The official called out names according to the register, unlocked the restraints, and drove them southward. The ghosts hesitated, as if going unwillingly but with no choice. Finally, three female ghosts begged Cao to spare them, but Cao replied, "In obedience to the emperor's command, I cannot exert force or influence." The three ghosts angrily cursed, saying, "In twenty years, we will surely seek revenge!"

    After the release was completed, the officer returned and instructed the officials, saying, "Cao has worked hard; you must ensure he returns home safely." The officials still sent him back on his horse. Reaching the middle of the journey, they crossed a great river, and the horse suddenly lost its front legs and fell. Cao awoke in a state of shock, seeing family members crying around him, and realized he had been dead for a day. He held the matter secret in his heart and dared not speak to others about it.

    265

    后二十年，长男妇病产卒，未期年，次媳当产亦病，忽作呓语呼姑至前曰：“红花洞事发矣。我房舍已定，当与李氏为邻矣。”指其小叔曰：“继我者当在此君。可恨翁当时箭在手，乐得作人情，何故不肯乎？”言毕，张目大呼，血流破面，腹溃肠出，死。姑与小叔奔告于曹，曹大骇，自忆此梦实未尝语人，不知乃媳何从知也。殓后，寄其柩于古寺，寺中旧有朱棺一口，询之，果为某家妻李氏棺也。曹后第三子妻妇，亦以产卒。三妇年岁虽各有大小，计其始生，皆与梦时相上下。后侧室生儿，皆无恙。

    　　○大毛人攫女西北妇女小便，多不用溺器。陕西咸宁县乡间有赵氏妇，年二十余，洁白有姿，盛夏月夜，裸而野溺，久不返。其夫闻墙瓦飒拉声，疑而出视，见妇赤身爬据墙上，两脚在墙外，两手悬墙内，急而持之。妇不能声，启其口，出泥数块，始能言，曰：“我出户溺，方解裤，见墙外有一大毛人，目光闪闪，以手招我。

    Explanation

    265

    After twenty years, the eldest son's wife died during childbirth, and before a year had passed, the second son's wife also fell ill during childbirth. She suddenly began to mutter incoherently and called her mother-in-law to the front, saying, "The red flower cave affair has happened. My dwelling place has been determined, and I will be neighbors with the Li family." She pointed to her younger brother-in-law and said, "The one who will succeed me is this man. It's regrettable that the old man had an arrow in his hand; he could have done a favor, but why didn't he do it?" After finishing her words, she opened her eyes and shouted, blood flowed and broke her face, her abdomen burst and intestines spilled out, and she died. Her mother-in-law and younger brother-in-law rushed to report to Cao, who was greatly shocked, recalling that he had never told this dream to anyone, not knowing how his daughter-in-law could know about it. After the funeral, they stored her coffin in an ancient temple, where there was an old red coffin. When they asked about it, they found it was indeed the coffin of Li, the wife of a certain family. Cao's third son's wife also died during childbirth. The three women, though of different ages, all were around the time of the dream. Later, the concubine gave birth to a child, and all were unharmed.

    In the northwest, women often urinate in the open without using a vessel. In the countryside of Xianning County, Shaanxi Province, there was a woman from the Zhao family, about twenty years old, fair-skinned and beautiful. On a summer moonlit night, she went out to urinate naked and did not return for a long time. Her husband heard the sound of tiles rustling and suspected something, so he went out to look. He saw her naked, crawling on the wall, with her feet outside the wall and her hands hanging inside. He hurriedly held her. The woman could not speak, and when he opened her mouth, he found several pieces of mud inside. She finally spoke, saying, "I went out to urinate, just taking off my pants, and saw a large hairy person outside the wall with flashing eyes, who waved his hand at me."

    266

    我急走，毛人自墙外伸巨手提我髻至墙头，以泥塞我口，将拖出墙。我两手据墙挣住，今力竭矣，幸速相救。“赵探头外视，果有大毛人，似猴非猴，蹲墙下，双手持妇脚不放。赵抱妇身与之夺，力不胜，及大呼村邻。邻远，无应者。急入室取刀，拟断毛人手救妇。刀至，而妇已被毛人拉出墙矣。赵开户追之，众邻齐至。毛人挟妇去，走如风，妇呼救声尤惨。追二十余里，卒不能及。

    　　明早，随巨迹而往，见妇死大树间：四肢皆巨藤穿缚，唇吻有巨齿啮痕，阴处溃裂，骨皆见。血裹白精，渍地斗余。合村大痛，鸣于官。官亦泪下，厚为殡殓，召猎户擒毛人，卒不得。

    　　○狐仙冒充观音三年杭州周生，从张天师过保定旅店，见美妇人跪阶下，若有所祈。生问天师，天师曰：“此狐也，向我求人间香火耳。”生曰：“盍许之？”天师曰：“彼修炼有年，颇得灵气，若与香火，恐恣威福，为人间祟。”生爱其美，代为祈请。

    Explanation

    266

    I was quickly running, when a hairy being reached out from outside the wall with a huge hand, grabbing my hair and pulling me up to the top of the wall. It then plugged my mouth with mud and was about to drag me out. I held onto the wall with both hands, struggling desperately, but my strength was now exhausted. I pleaded, "Please save me quickly."

    Zhao peeked out to look, and indeed, there was a huge hairy being, resembling a monkey but not quite, squatting at the base of the wall, holding the woman's feet with both hands without letting go. Zhao embraced the woman's body and tried to wrestle it away, but his strength was not enough. He then shouted loudly to the villagers. The neighbors were far away and did not respond. Zhao rushed into the room to fetch a knife, intending to cut off the hairy being's hand to save the woman. When the knife arrived, the woman had already been dragged out of the wall by the hairy being. Zhao opened the door and chased after it, and the other neighbors all arrived. The hairy being carried the woman away, running like the wind, and the woman's cries for help were especially pitiful. They chased after them for more than twenty miles, but finally could not catch up.

    The next morning, they followed the large tracks and saw the woman dead between two large trees: all four limbs were bound with large vines, there were large teeth marks on her lips, her private parts were torn apart, and her bones were visible. Blood mixed with white semen stained the ground for more than a dou. The entire village was greatly saddened and reported the incident to the authorities. The official also shed tears and gave a generous burial. He summoned hunters to capture the hairy being, but finally could not.

    ○ A Fox Spirit Masquerading as the Bodhisattva Guanyin for Three Years

    Zhou, a man from Hangzhou, followed Zhang the Taoist Master to a lodging in Baoding, where he saw a beautiful woman kneeling at the steps, as if praying for something. Zhou asked the Taoist Master, who said, "This is a fox, asking me for offerings from the human world." Zhou said, "Why not grant it?" The Taoist Master replied, "It has been cultivating itself for many years and has gained a lot of spiritual energy. If we give it offerings, it might abuse its power and become a pestilence in the human world." Zhou loved its beauty and prayed on its behalf.

    267

    天师曰：“难却君情，但令受香火三年，毋得过期可也。”命法官批黄纸付之去。

    　　三年后，生下第出都，过苏州，闻上方山某庵观音极著灵异，将生祷焉。至山下，同祷者教以步行，曰：“此山观音甚灵，凡肩舆上山者，中道必仆。”生不信，肩舆上山。未数十武，杠果折，生坠地，幸无所伤，遂下舆步行。入庙，见香烛极盛，所谓观音者坐锦幔中，勿许人见。生问僧，僧曰：“塑像太美，恐见者辄生邪念故也。”生必欲启视。果极妖冶，不类他处观音。谛视之，颇似曾相识者。良久恍然，是旅店中妇人。生大怒，指而数之曰：“汝昔求我说情，故得此香火。汝乃不感我恩而坏我舆，何太没良心也？且天师只许汝受香火三年，今已过期，恋此不去，岂竟忘前约乎？”语未毕，像忽扑地碎，僧大骇，亦无可奈何。俟生去，纠金为之重塑，而灵响从此寂然。

    Explanation

    267

    The Celestial Master said, "It is hard to refuse your kindness, but let her receive incense and fire offerings for three years, and do not exceed the term." He ordered the judge to write on yellow paper and give it to her.

    Three years later, the man left the capital for the first time and passed through Suzhou. He heard that the Guanyin at a certain temple on Shangfang Mountain was extremely powerful and miraculous, so he intended to pray there. Upon arriving at the foot of the mountain, those who were praying together told him to walk instead of being carried up the mountain, saying, "The Guanyin on this mountain is very powerful, and anyone carried up the mountain in a sedan chair will certainly fall in the middle." The man did not believe them and insisted on being carried up the mountain. After not taking many steps, the pole indeed broke, and the man fell to the ground, fortunately without injury, and then got off the sedan chair and walked. Upon entering the temple, he saw the incense and candles burning brightly, and the so-called Guanyin sat in a brocade curtain, not allowed to be seen. The man asked the monk, who replied, "The statue is too beautiful, and we fear that seeing it will lead to improper thoughts." The man insisted on seeing it. It was indeed extremely beautiful and unlike any other Guanyin he had seen. Upon closer inspection, he realized it was somewhat familiar. After a long time, he suddenly recognized it as the woman from the inn. The man became very angry and pointed at the statue, scolding it, "You once asked me to plead for you, so you received this incense and fire offerings. You did not feel my kindness and damaged my sedan chair, how ungrateful are you? Moreover, the Celestial Master only allowed you to receive incense and fire offerings for three years, now you have exceeded the term, staying here unwillingly, have you completely forgotten the previous agreement?" Before he finished speaking, the statue suddenly fell and broke. The monk was greatly startled and could do nothing about it. After the man left, they collected money to rebuild the statue, but the spiritual response became silent from then on.

    268

    ○吴生手软乾隆二十四年五月，丰县宰卢世昌修邑志，聘苏州吴生为誊录，与同事者同住一楼。忽具衣冠揖同事友曰：“吾死矣，以后事累公。”友问故，吴愀然云：“我初赴丰时，至沛县，道上遇一妇人，求与共载，我以车小不许。妇随车行二十里，心窃讶之。何舆夫，皆不见，始知为鬼。晚投旅店，人静后，妇来坐榻上语我曰：”君与我年俱廿九，合为夫妇。‘我大骇，以枕投之，随响而没。自此不复见形，时闻耳边嚅嚅作语，求作夫妇，呼我为’写字人‘，噪聒不已。问：’如何酬汝汝方去？‘曰：“与我钱二百，置楼板上，我即去。’如其言。既而我钱在，妇来缠扰如初，奈何奈何？”友人咸相解慰，令二僮守之。

    Explanation

    268

    In the fifth month of the twenty-fourth year of the Qianlong era (1759 CE), the magistrate of Feng County, Lu Shichang, was compiling the local gazetteer and hired a scholar from Suzhou, referred to as Wu, to assist with the transcription. Wu lived in the same building as his colleagues. One day, Wu suddenly dressed in proper attire and bowed to his colleagues, saying, "I am going to die. Please take care of my affairs after I am gone." His colleagues asked why, and Wu, with a sad expression, explained:

    "When I first came to Feng County, I passed through Pei County. On the road, I encountered a woman who asked to ride in my cart. I refused because the cart was too small. The woman followed the cart for twenty miles, and I secretly wondered why the cart drivers were all gone. It was then that I realized she was a ghost. In the evening, after arriving at an inn, when everyone had gone to sleep, the woman came and sat on the bed, speaking to me. She said, 'You and I are both twenty-nine years old. We should be husband and wife.' I was greatly frightened and threw a pillow at her. She vanished with a sound. Since then, I have not seen her form again, but I often hear her murmuring in my ear, asking to be my wife and calling me 'the writer.' She is continuously noisy and disturbing. I asked her, 'How can I make you go away?' She replied, 'Give me two hundred taels of silver and place it on the floor of the building, and I will leave.' I followed her instructions. However, after placing the money, the woman continued to bother me as before. What can I do?"

    His colleagues tried to comfort him, and they had two servants watch over him.

    269

    越数日，楼上大呼，众奔上，见吴倒地，腹右刀戳一洞，肠半溃出，喉下食嗓已断。扶起之，绝无痛楚。卢公往视，吴手招之近前，作一“冤”字。卢曰：“是何冤？”曰：“欢喜冤家也。今早妇人来逼我死，以便作夫妻。我问：”作何死法？‘妇指案上刀曰：“此物佳。’余取刺右腹，痛不可忍，妇人亟以手按摩之，曰：”此无济也。‘所摩处遂不觉痛。我问：“然则如何？’妇人自摩其颈作刎势曰：”如此方可。‘我复以刀断左喉，妇人跌足叹曰：“此亦无济，徒多痛苦耳。’又以手按摩之，亦不觉痛。指右喉下曰：”此处佳。‘余曰：“我手软矣无能为也，卿来刺之。’妇遂披发摇首，持刀直前，而楼下诸公已走上矣。

    Explanation

    269

    A few days later, a loud cry came from upstairs, and the crowd rushed up to see Wu lying on the ground. His right side had been stabbed with a knife, and his intestines were partially protruding. The esophagus below his throat had been severed. They lifted him up, but he showed no signs of pain. Mr. Lu went to examine him, and Wu gestured for him to come closer, forming the character "冤" (injustice). Mr. Lu asked, "What injustice?" Wu replied, "It's a 'lovers who quarrel' situation. This morning, the woman came to force me to die so we could be together. I asked, 'How should I die?' She pointed to the knife on the table and said, 'This is good.' I took it and stabbed my right abdomen, which was unbearably painful. The woman quickly massaged the area with her hand and said, 'This won't help.' The area she massaged no longer felt pain. I asked, 'Then what?' The woman gestured to her own neck as if to slit it and said, 'This way would be better.' I then cut my left throat, and the woman stepped back and sighed, 'This won't help either, it will only cause more pain.' She massaged the area again, and it no longer felt pain. She pointed to the right side of my throat and said, 'This is good.' I said, 'I'm too weak to do it, you do it.' The woman then loosened her hair, shook her head, held the knife, and moved forward, while the people from downstairs had already come upstairs.

    270

    彼闻人来，掷刀奔去。“卢公诧异，为延医纳其肠。吴始不能饮食，用药敷治，亦遂平复。妇人不复再至。吴生至今尚存。

    　　○狐祖师盐城村戴家有女为妖所凭，厌以符咒，终莫能止；诉于村北圣帝祠，怪遂绝。

    　　已而有金甲神托梦于其家曰：“吾圣帝某部下邹将军也。前日汝家妖是狐精，吾已斩之，其党约明日来报仇，尔等于庙中击金鼓助我。”翌日，戴家集邻众往。

    　　闻空中甲马声，乃奋击金钲铙鼓，果有黑气坠于庭，村前后落狐狸头甚夥。越数日，其家又梦邹将军来曰：“我以灭狐太多，获罪于狐祖师。狐祖师诉于大帝。

    Explanation

    270

    When the creature heard someone coming, it threw away its knife and fled. Lu Gong was astonished and, in a surprising act, extended his intestines to summon a doctor. Wu initially could not eat or drink, but after being treated with medicine, he eventually recovered. The woman (likely the creature) did not return. Wu still exists to this day.

    In another incident, a woman in the village of Dài was possessed by a fox spirit. Despite using talismans and spells, the haunting could not be stopped. She sought help at the temple dedicated to the saintly emperor in the north of the village, and the strange occurrences ceased.

    A few days later, a golden-armored deity appeared in a dream to the family, saying, "I am General Zōu, a general under the saintly emperor. The day before, the fox spirit haunting your family was slain by me. Its allies have agreed to seek revenge tomorrow. You should strike gongs and drums at the temple to assist me."

    The next day, the Dài family gathered neighbors and went to the temple. They heard the sound of armored horses in the air and struck gongs and drums. As a result, black mist fell in the courtyard, and many fox heads fell in the village.

    Several days later, the family dreamed again of General Zōu, who said, "I have been punished by the fox ancestor for killing too many foxes. The fox ancestor has complained to the supreme deity."

    271

    某日，大帝来庙按其事，诸父老盍为我祈之。“众如期往，伏于廊下。

    　　至夜半，仙乐嘹嘈，有冕服乘辇者冉冉来，侍卫甚众；后随一道人，庞眉皓齿，两金字牌署曰“狐祖师”。圣帝迎谒甚恭。狐祖师曰：“小狐扰世，罪当死，但部将歼我族类太酷，罪不可逭。”圣帝唯唯。村人自廊下出，跪而请命。有周秀才者骂曰：“老狐狸！须白如此，纵子孙淫人妇女，反来向圣帝说情，何物‘狐祖师’，罪当万斩！”祖师笑不怒，从容问：“人间和奸何罪？”周曰：“杖也。”祖师曰：“可知奸非死罪矣。我子孙以非类奸人，罪当加等，要不过充军流配耳，何致被斩？况邹将军斩我一子，并斩我子孙数十何耶？”周未及答，闻庙内传呼云：“大帝有命：邹将军嫉恶太严，杀戮太重，念其事属因公，为民除害，可罚俸一年，调管海州地方。”村人欢呼合掌，向空念佛而散。

    Explanation

    271

    One day, the Great Emperor came to the temple to oversee the matter, and the elders asked, "Why don't you pray for me?" The villagers went as scheduled and hid under the eaves.

    At midnight, heavenly music resounded, and a figure dressed in ceremonial robes and riding in a carriage approached slowly, accompanied by a large entourage. Following him was a Taoist priest with thick eyebrows and white teeth, holding two golden tablets inscribed with "Fox Ancestor." The Holy Emperor greeted him with great respect. The Fox Ancestor said, "The little foxes have disturbed the world, and they deserve to die, but the generals have killed too many of my kind, and the crime cannot be forgiven." The Holy Emperor nodded in agreement. The villagers came out from under the eaves, kneeled, and asked for mercy. A scholar named Zhou shouted, "Old fox! With such white hair, allowing your descendants to seduce women, and now you come to plead with the Holy Emperor, what kind of 'Fox Ancestor' are you? You deserve to be executed thousands of times!" The Fox Ancestor smiled without anger and calmly asked, "What crime is committed in the human world by adultery?" Zhou replied, "It is punished by flogging." The Fox Ancestor said, "It can be seen that adultery is not a capital offense. My descendants committing adultery with people of different species, the crime should be punished more severely, but at most, they should be exiled or sent to serve in the military. Why would they be executed? Why did General Zou kill one of my children and dozens of my descendants?" Zhou had not finished speaking when he heard a voice inside the temple announce, "The Great Emperor has issued a command: General Zou was too strict in punishing evil, killed too many, considering that it was for the public good, to eliminate a public menace, can be fined one year's salary, and be transferred to manage the area of Haizhou." The villagers cheered, clasped their hands together, prayed to the sky, and dispersed.

    272

    ○纣之值殿将军天台僧智果好游，山行迷路，至大石洞。坐一道者，萝衣薜裳。僧跪而请曰：“某幸遇仙人，愿受教。”道者曰：“予人也，非仙也，子来胡为？”僧曰：“某入山已数日，腹枵甚，敢有云浆之请。”道者曰：“子姑待，吾往后山觅之。”

    　　去有顷，携一物来，状轮而色鲜白。道者破之，自吸其浆，以其余授僧，曰：“此千年茯苓也。”因令僧坐，问：“岳飞将军安否？秦桧死否？”僧曰：“此宋朝事也，今易代数百年为大清矣。”因告以《宋史》所载岳事颠末。道者惨然曰：“岳将军终不免乎！”遂大哭，曰：“吾姓周，名通，岳将军麾下小将也。

    Explanation

    272

    A Monk Named Zhi Guo from Tian Tai Mountain, Who Was a Palace Guard Under King Zhou, Enjoyed Traveling. While Wandering in the Mountains, He Got Lost and Found Himself at a Large Stone Cave. Sitting Down with a Daoist, the Monk Saw the Daoist Wearing Clothes Made of Wild Vines and Moss. The Monk Kneeling Down and Said, "I am fortunate to meet a celestial being; I wish to receive instruction." The Daoist replied, "I am a human, not a celestial being; why did you come?" The monk said, "I have been in the mountains for several days, and my stomach is very empty; I dare to ask for a drink of cloud water." The Daoist said, "Please wait a moment; I will go to the back of the mountain to find it."

    After a short while, the Daoist brought an object, which was round and white. He broke it open, drank the liquid himself, and gave the rest to the monk, saying, "This is a thousand-year-old poria." He then made the monk sit down and asked, "Is General Yue Fei well? Is Qin Hui dead?" The monk replied, "This is a matter from the Song dynasty; now, after several hundred years, it is the Qing dynasty." He then told the Daoist the details of Yue Fei's story as recorded in the Song Shi (History of the Song). The Daoist said sadly, "Is General Yue Fei ultimately doomed?" He then burst into tears and said, "My name is Zhou Tong, and I was a minor general under General Yue Fei."

    273

    当秦桧以金牌召兵时，我知有难，遂逃于此，食灵草得不死。我师教勿出洞，出洞即死。汝宜速出，迟恐无及。“僧惧，拜辞而行。

    　　路甚纡曲，备历险阻。忽望崖上坐一巨人，长丈余，遍体绿毛如翠锦，骇而奔还告道者。道者曰：“此予师商高，纣王之值殿将军也，为飞廉、恶来所谮，避居此山。性好食野兽，故其状与人异。子往拜祈，兼可问商代事。”僧故蠢野，无所记忆。见巨人礼拜毕，便问纣宠妲己事。巨人曰：“汝误矣，妲者，南宫女官之称；己戊者，女官之行次。女官非止一人也，汝所问何妃？”僧不能答，又问文王受命事。曰：“吾不知文王为何人，或是西方诸侯姬昌耶？其人事纣甚恭，并无称王之事。”因问：“汝所问者，何人告汝？”曰：“书上云云。”巨人问：“何物为书？”僧手作书状示之。巨人笑曰：“我当时尚无此物。”言毕，以一臂搂僧行如飞，置之平地，拱手而别，已在天台郊外矣。

    Explanation

    273

    When Qin Hui issued a military decree with a gold tablet, I knew there was trouble and fled here, where I consumed a spiritual herb and thus avoided death. My master taught me not to leave the cave, for leaving would mean certain death. You should leave quickly, for if you delay, it might be too late. The monk, fearing this, bowed and took his leave.

    The path was winding and fraught with danger. Suddenly, he saw a giant seated on a cliff, over ten feet tall, covered in green fur like a jade brocade. Frightened, he ran back to tell the hermit. The hermit said, "This is my master, Shang Gao, the palace guardian general of King Zhou. He was slandered by Feilian and E'lae and took refuge in this mountain. He loves eating wild beasts, so his appearance differs from that of ordinary people. You should go and pay your respects, and you may also inquire about matters of the Shang Dynasty." The monk, being simple and rustic, had no memory. After bowing to the giant, he asked about King Zhou's favorite consort, Lady Da Ji. The giant said, "You are mistaken. Da is the title for a female official in the southern palace; Ji is her rank. There are many female officials, so which one are you asking about?" The monk could not answer and then asked about King Wen receiving his mandate. The giant replied, "I do not know who King Wen is, perhaps he is Western Lord Ji Chang? He served King Zhou with great reverence and never claimed to be a king." The monk then asked, "Who told you about these things?" The monk replied, "It is written in books." The giant asked, "What is a book?" The monk made a gesture representing a book. The giant laughed and said, "I did not have such things at that time." After saying this, he carried the monk with one arm, walking like flying, and placed him on the ground. He bowed with clasped hands and bid farewell, and the monk found himself already outside the outskirts of TianTai.

    274

    ○疟鬼上元令陈齐东，少时与张某寓太平府关帝庙中。张病疟，陈与同房，因午倦，对卧床上。见户外一童子，面白，衣帽鞋袜皆深青色，探头视张。陈初意为庙中人，不之问。俄而张疟作。童子去，张疟亦止。又一日寝，忽闻张狂叫，痰如涌泉。陈惊寤，见童子立张榻前，舞手蹈足，欢笑顾盼，若甚得意者。陈知为疟鬼，直前扑之，着手冷不可耐。童走出，飒飒有声，追至中庭而没。张疾愈，而陈手有黑气，如烟熏色，数日始除。

    Explanation

    274

    A Ghost of Malaria

    When young, Chen Qidong, the magistrate of Shangyuan, lived temporarily in the Guandi Temple in Taiping Prefecture with someone named Zhang. Zhang was suffering from malaria, and Chen shared a room with him. One day, feeling tired at noon, they lay face to face on the bed. Chen saw a young boy outside the door, with a pale face and deep blue clothing, hat, shoes, and socks, peering at Zhang. Initially, Chen thought the boy was someone from the temple and did not inquire further. Soon after, Zhang's malaria attack began. The boy left, and Zhang's malaria also stopped. Another day, while sleeping, Chen suddenly heard Zhang screaming wildly, with phlegm gushing like a spring. Chen woke up in a start and saw the boy standing in front of Zhang's bed, dancing his hands and feet, laughing and looking around, as if very pleased. Chen realized it was the malaria ghost and charged at it, but found his hands were cold and unbearable. The boy ran out, making a rustling sound, and Chen chased him to the middle courtyard where the boy disappeared. Zhang's illness was cured, but Chen's hands had black vapor, like the color of smoke熏, which took several days to disappear.

    275

    ○误学武松杭州马观澜家，每四时必祭其门。予问：“古礼：门为五祀之一，今此礼久不行，君家独行之，何也？”马曰：“余家奴陈公祚好酒，每晚必醉敲门归。一日，闻户外喧呶声，往视之，奴扑地曰：”奴归，见门外一男一妇，俱无头，头持在手。妇呼曰：“吾汝嫂也。吾淫属实，吾夫杀我可也。汝为小叔，不当杀我。

    　　夫杀我时，心软手噤<齿介>不下，汝夺刀代杀，此事岂汝所宜与耶？吾每来相寻，为汝主人家门神呵禁，今故伺汝于门外。“因大骂唾奴面。其男鬼掷头撞奴，奴倒地。闻人声，二鬼才散。‘马氏众家人扶至床，自言少年曾有此事，当时看小说，慕武松之为人，不意遭此冤孽。或告之曰：”小说都无实事，何得妄学？且武松杀嫂，为嫂杀兄故也。若寻常犯奸，王法只杖决耳，汝何得代兄杀嫂？’言未终，奴张目作女声曰：“公道自在人心，何如何如。‘向言者三叩头而死。”

    Explanation

    275

    A Woman Mistakenly Imitates Wu Song

    In the family of Ma Guanlan in Hangzhou, sacrifices were offered to the door at the four seasons. I asked, "According to ancient rituals, the door is one of the five ancestral spirits, but this ritual has long been abandoned. Your family alone still practices it. Why is that?" Ma replied, "My servant Chen Gongzuo loves alcohol and always returns home drunk at night. One day, he heard a noisy commotion outside and went to investigate. The servant fell to the ground and said, 'When I returned, I saw a man and a woman outside the door, both without heads, holding their heads in their hands. The woman called out, "I am your sister-in-law. I did indeed commit adultery, and it was right for my husband to kill me. You, as my younger brother, should not have killed me.

    When my husband was killing me, his heart softened and his hand froze, unable to proceed. You took the knife and killed me in his place. How could you be involved in such a thing? I often come to seek you out, but your master's door gods prevent me. That is why I am waiting for you outside.' She then cursed and spat on the servant's face. The male ghost threw his head at the servant, who fell to the ground. When he heard people approaching, the two ghosts dispersed." The other members of the Ma household helped him back to bed, where he confessed that he had experienced this as a youth. At the time, he had read a novel and admired Wu Song's character, but did not expect to suffer such karmic retribution. Someone told him, "Novels are not based on real events. How could you foolishly imitate them? Moreover, Wu Song killed his sister-in-law because she had killed her brother. If it were just a case of adultery, the law would only punish with a beating. Why did you take your brother's place in killing your sister-in-law?" Before the speaker finished, the servant opened his eyes and spoke in a woman's voice, "Justice lies in people's hearts. What can I do about it?" He then bowed three times to the speaker and died.

    276

    马氏以鬼言故祭门神甚敬，世其家。
