    In the reign of the current dynasty, Governor Tong Guoxiang of Gansu, traveling on official business to Fuqiang County, dreamed that a spirit called out, "Hurry! Hurry!" Tong did not take it seriously. The next night, he had the same dream, and the spirit added, "If you wish to repay my kindness, just remember the phrase 'Jingbo Wanzai.'" Tong was startled awake.

    He hurried for three days, and Fuqiang County sank into a lake, and he could not understand who the spirit was that saved him. Later, while on an inspection tour to Jianchang Yeda, he saw the phrase "Jingbo Wanzai" written on the Guan Gong Temple. Tong entered to pay his respects and greatly renovated the temple, which still stands brightly today.

    475

    ○冯侍御冯侍御静山，居京师永光寺西街。改造书屋，掘地得黑漆棺，为改迁之。夜梦人投牒诉冤，冯时巡西城，梦中取牒阅之。告势宦掘棺事，即己之姓名也，惊醒得疾。疾革时，夫人闻房中笑语声，以为病有起色，往视之，见黑衣人素不相识者坐床上，一闪而灭。侍御谓夫人曰：“此人吾邻也，曾作运粮守备。运饷至京师卒，棺厝于永光寺前街僧寺中，迫近吾家而吾不知。今闻我亦有行期，故来相约耳，可烧纸钱助其冥资。”夫人遣人至前街踪迹，棺识宛然，知先生之终不起也。

    　　○药师父昆山徐大司寇之子字冠卿，幼时号“药师父”，以其曾鸩死一业师也。业师周姓，号云核，受司寇聘前一日梦巨蟒以口吐红丸逼令咽之，肠痛而醒。就聘于徐，督冠卿严。冠卿素佻达，笞责尤甚。冠卿与仆谋，置鸩于饭，食之而卒。

    Explanation

    475

    ○ Feng Shiyu, also known as Feng Jingshan, resided in the west street of Yongguang Temple in the capital. While renovating his study room, he dug up a black lacquered coffin and decided to relocate it. One night, he dreamt of someone filing a petition to appeal a grievance. Feng, who was patrolling the western city at the time, took the petition in his dream and read it. The petition recounted the act of a powerful official digging up the coffin, and the name on it was his own. He awoke in shock and fell ill. As his illness worsened, his wife heard laughing and talking sounds in the room, thinking it was a sign that his condition was improving. When she went to check, she saw a black-clothed man, whom she did not recognize, sitting on the bed. The man then flickered and disappeared. Feng told his wife, "This person is my neighbor. He once served as a grain transport guard. He died while transporting grain to the capital and was buried in a temple on the street in front of Yongguang Temple, close to our home but unbeknownst to me. Now he has heard that I too have a time of departure, so he has come to meet me. You can burn paper money to help his spirit's resources." His wife sent someone to the street to trace the coffin, which was clearly identified, and they realized that the master would never rise again.

    　　○ Xu Guanqing, the son of Xu Daigong, the Grand Censor of Kunshan, was nicknamed "Yaofushi" when he was young, due to the fact that he once poisoned one of his teachers. The teacher, surnamed Zhou and known as Yunhe, had a dream the day before accepting Xu Daigong's appointment where a giant snake forced him to swallow a red pill from its mouth, causing him to wake up in severe abdominal pain. Upon joining Xu's household, he supervised Guanqing very strictly. Guanqing, who was naturally carefree and rebellious, received particularly harsh beatings. Guanqing conspired with his servant to poison his food, and after eating it, the teacher died.

    476

    后冠卿为翰林，不得志，诗文多怨诽，为人所构，就鞫刑部。见左司杨景震，大惊曰：“吾死矣！吾初见时，俨然周先生也。”次日复讯，各官俱以司寇之子，稍加怜恤；杨独怒鞫，批其颊数十下，齿左右坠，定以斩决。狱上即刑，杨为监斩官，其家访之，杨景震之生年月日，即周先生之死年月日也。或告之杨，杨大笑曰：“岂有是哉！使吾早知此语，转当屈法以救之矣。”此与《太平广记》载王武俊事同。

    　　○庄秀才通州庄孝廉成，戊午举人，少年貌美。其佃户有女悦之，竟以成疾。临卒谓其父曰：“吾为庄秀才死也，吾思嫁庄秀才，自念门户寒贱，事必不成，故郁郁成病。今虽死，此意当为致之秀才，则瞑目矣。”其父急告庄，庄往视，而气已绝。庄赴秋闱，遇女子于淮新桥，宛然如生。入闱，一切炊饭烹茶之事，见女子身为执役，是年登第。每有远行，则女子必至。庄怖之，为置神主祭于家，书“亡妾某氏”，见女子来拜谢，自此绝矣。

    Explanation

    476

    Mr. Hou Guanqing, a scholar of the Hanlin Academy, was frustrated in his career and his poetry and prose often expressed complaints and grievances. He was falsely accused and brought before the Ministry of Punishment for interrogation. When he saw Judge Yang Jingzhen of the Left Office, he was greatly alarmed and said, “I am doomed! When I first saw him, he seemed like Mr. Zhou, my former mentor.” The next day, during the re-examination, all the officials, considering him the son of a high-ranking official, showed some leniency. However, Yang alone was furious and interrogated him harshly, striking him across the face dozens of times, causing his teeth to fall out on both sides. He was sentenced to death by beheading. The sentence was carried out immediately, with Yang serving as the executioner. Later, his family inquired about the matter and discovered that Yang Jingzhen’s date of birth was the same as Mr. Zhou’s date of death. Someone informed Yang of this, and he laughed and said, “How could that be! If I had known this earlier, I would have bent the law to save him.” This incident is similar to the story of Wang Wujun recorded in the Taiping Guangji.

    Mr. Zhuang, a scholar from Tongzhou, passed the provincial examination in the year of the horse and was known for his handsome appearance. One of his tenant farmers’ daughters fell in love with him and eventually died from her illness. Before her death, she told her father, “I died because of Mr. Zhuang. I wanted to marry him, but I thought our family was too poor and the match would never succeed, so I became depressed and fell ill. Now that I am dead, I wish to convey my feelings to Mr. Zhuang, and then I can die in peace.” Her father hurriedly informed Mr. Zhuang, who went to see her, but she had already passed away. Mr. Zhuang later took the provincial examination and encountered the girl at Huixin Bridge, who appeared to him as if she were still alive. During the examination, he saw her performing domestic tasks such as cooking and making tea. That year, he passed the examination. Whenever he traveled far, the girl would appear to him. Frightened, he placed a spirit tablet in his home, inscribed with “My deceased concubine, Lady So-and-So,” and saw her come to pay respects. From then on, she never appeared again.

    477

    ○蔼蔼幽人通州李臬司，讳玉钅宏，丙戌进士。少时好炼笔录，忽一日，笔于空中书曰：“敬我，我助汝功名。”李再拜，祀以牲牢。嗣后文社之事，题下，则听笔之所为。尤能作擘窠大字，求者辄与。李敬奉甚至，家事外事，咨之而行，靡不如意。

    　　社中能文者每读李作，叹其笔意大类钱吉士。钱井士者，前朝翰林钱熹也。李私问笔神，笑曰：“是也。”自后里国人来扶乩者，多以“钱先生”呼之。笔神遇题跋落款，不书姓名，但书“蔼蔼幽人”四字。李举孝廉，成进士，笔神之力居多。后官臬司，神助之决狱，郡中以为神。李公乞归，神与俱。李他出，其子弟事神不敬，神怒，投书作别而去。

    Explanation

    477

    The Reclusive Gentleman of Tongzhou, Li Yuhong, the Governor-General (臬司), who passed the imperial examination in the year of the Fire Dog (丙戌). In his youth, he was fond of refining his writing skills. One day, the pen wrote in the air, saying, “Respect me, and I will help you achieve fame and success.” Li bowed twice and offered sacrifices with livestock. Afterward, for all matters concerning the literary society, he would write according to the pen’s dictates. He was especially skilled in creating large, bold calligraphy, and he would readily provide it to those who requested it. Li treated the pen with utmost reverence, consulting it for both family and external affairs, and everything turned out as he wished.

    Those skilled in writing within the society often read Li’s works and marveled at the similarity of his style to that of Qian Jishi. Qian Jishi was a scholar-official (翰林) from a previous dynasty, named Qian Xi. Li privately asked the pen spirit, who smiled and said, “Yes, it is him.” From then on, when locals came to perform spirit-writing (扶乩), they often referred to him as “Master Qian.” Whenever the pen spirit encountered inscriptions or dedications, it would not write its own name but instead inscribed the four characters “蔼蔼幽人” (the reclusive gentleman). Li passed the examination as a scholar-official (孝廉) and became a Jinshi (进士), with much of his success attributed to the pen spirit. Later, when he served as the Governor-General, the spirit helped him adjudicate cases, and the people of the prefecture regarded him as a divine figure. When Li requested to retire, the spirit accompanied him. However, when Li was away, his descendants did not show proper respect to the spirit. Angered, the spirit left a letter and departed.

    478

    余与李公子方膺同官交好，绝不向余道只字。方膺卒后，臬司同年熊涤斋太史为余言之，并云方膺深讳其事，盖忤神者，即方膺也。

    　　○僵尸求食武林钱塘门内有更楼，雇更夫击柝表里巡逻。大众敛赀为之，由来旧矣。康熙五十六年夏，更夫任三者巡巷外，路过小庙，每至二更，闻柝声，则有一人从庙中出，踉跄捷走；漏五下，则先柝声入庙，如是者屡矣。任三疑庙中僧有邪约，将伺之为诈酒肉计。

    　　次夕，月明如昼，见其人面枯黑如腊，目眶深陷，两肩挂银锭而行，有声，出入如前。任三知为僵尸，因山门之内停有旧榇，积尘寸许。询诸僧人，云：“其师祖时不知谁何氏所寄厝者也。”与侪辈语及之，其中黠者曰：“吾闻鬼畏赤豆、铁屑及米子，备此三物升许，伺其破棺出，潜取以绕棺之四周，则彼不能入矣。”任如其言，购买三物。

    Explanation

    478

    I have the source text and the pre-drafting research at hand. Let's proceed with the translation.

    Translation:

    I was in good standing with the nobleman Li Fang Ying, but he never spoke a word to me about anything. After Fang Ying's death, the high-ranking official Xiong Di-zhai, who was a contemporary of mine, told me about it, and mentioned that Fang Ying had deeply concealed the matter, for he was the one who had offended the gods.

    ○ Seeking Food: In the area of Wulin (Hangzhou), inside the Qiantang Gate, there is a watchtower where night watchmen are hired to patrol inside and outside the area. The community collectively funds this arrangement, which has been a longstanding tradition. In the summer of the fifty-sixth year of the Kangxi era, the night watchman Ren San was patrolling the outer alley and passed by a small temple. Every time it was two in the night, upon hearing the sound of the watchman's staff, a person would emerge from the temple, moving quickly but unsteadily; at five in the night, he would enter the temple first, and this happened repeatedly. Ren San suspected that the monk in the temple had made some kind of pact and intended to spy on him for the purpose of tricking him with wine and meat.

    The following night, the moon was as bright as daylight, and he saw the person with a face as black and dry as wax, deep-set eye sockets, and silver ingots hanging from his shoulders, making a jingling sound as he moved in and out as before. Ren San realized it was a zombie. Inside the temple gate, there was an old coffin, covered in a layer of dust about an inch thick. He asked the monks, who said, "It was placed here by someone of unknown identity during the time of our ancestors." When he discussed this with his colleagues, one of the clever ones said, "I have heard that ghosts fear red beans, iron filings, and rice grains. Prepare these three items, about a sheng (unit of measurement), and wait for him to break out of his coffin. Secretly take them and place them around the four sides of the coffin, and he will not be able to enter." Ren San followed his advice and purchased the three items.

    This translation aims to be faithful to the original text while ensuring it reads naturally in English. If you have any specific adjustments or further details you would like to include, please let me know!

    479

    待夜二更，尸复出。伺其去远，携灯入视，见棺后方板一块，俗语所谓“和头”者，已掀在地，中空空无所有，乃取三物绕棺而密洒之。事毕，径归卧更楼上。至五更，有厉声呼“任三爷”者。任问为谁，曰：“我山门内之长眠者，无子孙，久不得血食，故出外营求以救腹馁。今为尔所魇，不能入棺，吾其死矣。

    　　可急起将赤豆、铁屑拂去之。“任惧不敢答。又呼曰：”我与尔何仇，何苦为此虐耶？‘“任念与彼解围之后，彼杀我而后入，何以御之？终不答。鸡初鸣，鬼哀恳，继以詈骂，久之寂然。

    　　明日，过楼下者见有尸僵卧，乃告众鸣官，以尸还诸棺而火焚之，一方得宁。

    　　○僵尸贪财受累绍兴王生某，食饩有年，村中富家延之为师。因屋宇湫隘，适相距里许有新室求售者，遂买使居，且曰：“家中摒挡未尽，学徒暨馆童辈明晨进馆，先生一夜独眠，能无惧乎？”王自负胆壮，且新室也，何畏之有，乃命童携茗具引至书斋。

    Explanation

    479

    The Zombie's Greed for Wealth and Suffering

    In Shaoxing, there was a scholar named Wang who had been a scholar for many years. A wealthy family in the village invited him to be a teacher. Because his house was small and cramped, there was a new house for sale within a mile's distance, so he bought it and moved in. The seller warned him that the house was not fully cleared and that students and servants would come in the morning, so he might be afraid to sleep alone. Wang felt confident and brave, and since it was a new house, he had no fear. He then ordered a servant to carry tea utensils and lead him to the study.

    That night, during the second watch, the corpse came out again. He waited until it went far away, then carried a lamp inside to look. He saw a square board at the back of the coffin, which was locally called the "headboard." It had already been lifted to the ground, and inside was empty with nothing. He then took three items and sprinkled them around the coffin. After finishing, he went straight back to sleep on the watchtower.

    By the fifth watch, someone called out in a harsh voice, "Mr. Ren Three!" Mr. Ren asked who it was, and the ghost replied, "I am the long-dead person in the temple who has no descendants and has not received offerings for a long time, so I have come out to seek sustenance. Now you have trapped me, and I cannot enter the coffin; I will die."

    Mr. Ren was afraid and did not dare to answer. The ghost then called out, "What have I done to you? Why are you tormenting me?" Mr. Ren thought that after helping the ghost, it would kill him before entering the coffin, so he was unsure how to protect himself. He did not answer. When the rooster crowed, the ghost pleaded and then cursed, and after a long time, it became silent.

    The next day, someone passing by the楼下 saw a stiff corpse lying down. They then informed the people and reported to the authorities. The corpse was returned to the coffin and burned, and the area finally became peaceful.

    In another incident, a scholar named Wang from Shaoxing, who had been a scholar for many years, was invited by a wealthy family in the village to be a teacher. Because his house was small and cramped, there was a new house for sale within a mile's distance, so he bought it and moved in. The seller warned him that the house was not fully cleared and that students and servants would come in the morning, so he might be afraid to sleep alone. Wang felt confident and brave, and since it was a new house, he had no fear. He then ordered a servant to carry tea utensils and lead him to the study.

    480

    王周视室内毕，复至门前徙倚。时已夜矣，月色大明，见山下爝火荧荧。趋往视之，光出一白木棺中。王念：此鬼磷耶？色宜碧。而焰带微赤，得无为金银气乎？快《智囊》所载：有胡人数辈凶服舆榇而藁葬城外者，捕人迹之，榇中皆黄白也。此棺毋乃类是？幸无人，可攫而取也。遂取石块击去其钉，从棺后推卸其盖，则赫然一尸，面青紫而腹膨亨，麻冠草履。越俗：凡父母在堂而子先亡者，例以此殓。王愕然退缩，每一缩则尸一跃，再缩而尸蹶然起。王尽力狂奔，尸自后追之。王入户登楼，闭门下键。喘息甫定，疑尸已去，开窗视之。窗启而尸昂首大喜，从外跃入。连叩门，不得入。忽大声悲呼，三呼而诸门洞开，若有启之者，遂登楼。王无奈何，持木棍待之。尸甫上，即击以棍，中其肩，所挂银锭散落于地，尸俯而拾取。王趁其伛偻时，尽力推之，尸滚楼下。旋闻鸡啼，从此寂无声响矣。

    Explanation

    480

    Wáng Zhōu finished inspecting the room and returned to the front door, where he lingered uncertainly. It was already night, and the moonlight was bright. He saw a faint, flickering light below the mountain. He hurried to investigate and found that the light was coming from a white wooden coffin. Wáng thought to himself: Is this ghost fire? It should be blue. But the flame had a slight red tinge—could it be the glow of gold and silver? He recalled a story from The Wisdom Bag: there were once several foreign men who wore mourning clothes and carried coffins to be buried outside the city. When the people pursued them, they found that the coffins were filled with yellow and white treasures. Could this coffin be similar? Fortunately, there was no one around, so he could take it. He picked up a stone and knocked out the nails, then pushed the lid off the coffin from behind. Suddenly, a corpse leapt out, its face blue-purple and its abdomen swollen, wearing a straw hat and straw sandals. According to the customs of the Yue people, when a child dies before their parents, they are typically buried in this manner. Wáng was startled and retreated, but every time he stepped back, the corpse leapt forward. On the second retreat, the corpse suddenly sprang up. Wáng ran as fast as he could, and the corpse chased him from behind. Wáng rushed into the house, climbed the stairs, locked the door, and lowered the bolt. When he finally caught his breath, he thought the corpse had gone, so he opened the window to check. As soon as the window opened, the corpse raised its head, looked delighted, and leapt in from outside. It knocked on the door repeatedly, but could not get in. Suddenly, it let out a loud, mournful cry. After three cries, all the doors opened as if by magic, and the corpse climbed up the stairs. Wáng had no choice but to wait with a wooden stick. As soon as the corpse stepped up, Wáng struck it with the stick, hitting its shoulder and causing the silver ingots it was carrying to scatter on the ground. The corpse bent down to pick them up. Wáng took advantage of its bent posture and pushed it as hard as he could, sending it rolling down the stairs. Soon after, he heard the rooster crow, and from then on, there was no more sound.

    481

    明日视之，尸跌伤腿骨，横卧于地，遂召众人扛而焚之。王叹曰：“我以贪故，招尸上楼；尸以贪故，被火烧毁。鬼尚不可贪，而况于人乎！”

    　　○宋荔裳受恶土地之累宋荔裳为山东臬使，族子某，素不肖，与总兵于七饮博为奸。于七者，前明末年山东土寇降清朝者也，虽为总戎，怙恶不悛。人以族子事告公，公怒曰：“如此必为家门之祸！俟其归，当缚至祠堂杖杀之。”某闻之，逃至德州。，夜宿土地庙中，梦土地神谓曰：“汝毋怖，大富贵至矣！现在于七谋反，汝可速往京师，赴提督处出首。”且曰：“某地中埋有百金，可取为路费。”族子掘地，果得金，大喜，以怨其叔故，遂赴提督处，并诬其叔与于七通谋，以故荔裳被逮入狱。未十日，于七果反，族子以首报之功受赏，荔裳牵累入狱，族亦昭雪。

    Explanation

    481

    The next day, when he looked at it, the corpse had fallen and broken its leg bone, lying horizontally on the ground. He then summoned everyone to carry and burn it. The king sighed, saying, "Because of my greed, I called the corpse upstairs; because of its greed, it was burned to ashes. Even ghosts should not be greedy, let alone humans!"

    ○ Song Lizhang Suffered from the Misfortune Caused by an Evil Land God

    Song Lizhang, the governor of Shandong, had a family member who was always unvirtuous and consorted with General Yu Qi in gambling and debauchery. General Yu Qi was a former bandit from Shandong who had surrendered to the Qing dynasty. Although he held the rank of general, he persisted in evil without repentance. When his family member's affair was reported to Song Lizhang, he became furious and said, "This will surely bring disaster to our family! When he returns, I will bind him and take him to the ancestral hall to beat him to death." The family member heard about it, fled to Dezhou, and stayed overnight in a land god temple. In his dream, the land god said to him, "Do not be afraid; great wealth and nobility will come! Now General Yu Qi is plotting rebellion; you can quickly go to the capital and report to the governor's office." The land god also said, "There is a hundred gold buried in a certain place; you can take it as travel expenses." The family member dug the ground and indeed found the gold, greatly delighted. Because of his resentment towards his uncle, he went to the governor's office and falsely accused his uncle of conspiring with General Yu Qi. Therefore, Song Lizhang was arrested and put in prison. Within ten days, General Yu Qi indeed rebelled, and the family member was rewarded for reporting the crime. Song Lizhang was implicated and put in prison, and his family was also cleared.

    482

    ○陆夫人某方伯夫人陆氏，尚书裘文达公之干女也。文达公薨后，夫人病，梦有大轿在屋上行来，前立青衣者呼曰：“裘大人命来相请。”夫人登轿，冉冉在云中行。

    　　至一大庙，正殿巍峨，旁有小屋甚洁，文达公科头，衣茧纟由袍，二童侍，几上卷案甚多，谓夫人曰：“知汝病之所由来耶！此前生孽也，”夫人忘而请曰：“干爷有力能为女儿解免否？”文达公曰：“此处西厢房有一妇人，现卧床上，汝往扶之。能扶起，则病可治，否则，我亦不能救汝。”命小童引夫人往西厢房，果有描金床施大红绫帐，被褥甚华，中卧赤身女尸，两目瞪视，无一言。

    Explanation

    482

    ○ Lady Lu, the wife of a certain local official, was the adopted daughter of Grand Secretary Qiu Wendata. After the death of Grand Secretary Qiu, Lady Lu fell ill and had a dream in which a large sedan chair appeared above her house. A servant dressed in blue stood in front and called out, "Grand Secretary Qiu has summoned you." Lady Lu climbed into the sedan chair and slowly ascended through the clouds.

    She arrived at a grand temple, where the main hall was majestic, and beside it was a very clean small room. Grand Secretary Qiu was bareheaded, wearing a silk robe, with two attendants by his side. There were many scrolls and documents on the table. He told Lady Lu, "Do you know the cause of your illness? It is a result of past karma." Lady Lu, in a state of shock, asked, "Uncle, do you have the power to save your adopted daughter?" Grand Secretary Qiu replied, "In the west wing of this temple, there is a woman lying on a bed. Go and help her up. If you can lift her, your illness can be cured; otherwise, I cannot save you either." He ordered a young attendant to guide Lady Lu to the west wing. There, indeed, was a bed adorned with gold and covered with a red silk canopy, with luxurious bedding. A naked female corpse lay on the bed, her eyes staring blankly, and she did not speak a word.

    483

    夫人扶之，手力尽矣，卒不起。

    　　归告文达公，公曰：“汝孽难消，可还家托张天师打醮以解禳之。但天师近日心粗，禄亦颇尽，某月日替苏州顾懋德家作斋文，错字甚多，上帝颇怒，奈何！”

    　　夫人惊醒，适天师在京，遂以此言告之。天师检顾家斋表，稿中果有误字，法官所写也，心为惊悸。

    　　未几，夫人亡，天师亦亡。天师名存义。顾懋德者，辛未进士，官礼部郎中。

    　　○牛头大王溧阳村民庄光裕，梦一怪，头上生角，敲门而进，谓曰：“我牛头大王也，上帝命血食此方。汝塑像祀我，必有福应。”庄醒，告知村民。村方病疫，皆曰：“宁可信其有。”纠钱数十千，起三间草屋，塑牛头而人身者坐焉。嗣后疫病尽痊，求子者颇效，香火大盛。如是数年。

    Explanation

    483

    The lady supported her, but her hand strength was exhausted, and she ultimately could not rise.

    She returned home and told Wen Da Gong, who said, "Your evil karma is difficult to eliminate. You can return home and entrust Master Zhang to perform a ritual to dispel it. However, Master Zhang's heart is coarse recently, and his fortune is also quite exhausted. On a certain day, he was writing a ritual text for the family of Gu Mao De in Suzhou, and there were many mistakes, which greatly angered Heaven. What can be done?"

    The lady awoke in shock, and just then, the Taoist priest was in the city. She told him about the situation. The Taoist priest checked the ritual text of the Gu family and found that there were indeed many mistakes, written by a judge. He was deeply startled.

    Not long after, the lady died, and the Taoist priest also died. The Taoist priest's name was Cun Yi. Gu Mao De was a scholar who passed the imperial examination in the year of the Sheep and held the position of Langzhong in the Ministry of Rites.

    A Bull-Headed King: A villager from Lianyang named Zhuang Guangyu dreamed of a strange creature with horns on its head, who knocked on the door and entered, saying, "I am the Bull-Headed King, and Heaven has commanded me to be worshipped here with blood offerings. If you create an image of me and worship me, there will surely be blessings." Zhuang awoke and told the villagers. At that time, the village was suffering from an epidemic, and they all said, "Better to believe it exists." They collected several thousand coins, built a thatched hut, and erected a statue of a man with a bull's head. Afterwards, the epidemic was completely cured, and those seeking children were quite successful. The worship became very popular, and this continued for several years.

    484

    村民周蛮子儿出痘，到庙，先具牲牢祀神，再掷卦，大吉。周喜，许演戏为谢。未数日，儿竟死。周怒曰：“我靠儿子耕田养我，儿死不如我死。”率其妻持锄钯撞牛头，碎其身，毁其庙。合村大惊，以为必有奇祸。自此寂然，牛头神亦不知何往。

    　　○水定庵牡丹江宁二尹汪公易堂，访友古北口，路憩水定庵。庵中牡丹盛开，花大如斗。

    　　汪近前赏玩。庵僧戒：“勿折花，花有妖，能为祸。”汪素刚，笑曰：“我本不折花，既云有妖，当折而试之。”以手摘之，花左右旋转，坚如牛筋，竟不能断。

    　　取所佩刀截之，花未断而拇指伤，血涔涔下。汪惭且怒，以袍袖裹血，忍痛不言，乃左手ㄏ花头，而右手以刀截其根，竟断一枝。归畜瓶中，夸于人曰：“我今日获花妖矣。”将购药医手创，细视之，并无刀痕，袍袖上亦无血迹。

    484

    A villager named Zhou Manzi's child contracted smallpox. He went to the temple, first prepared sacrificial animals to worship the gods, then cast the divination sticks, which indicated a great auspicious result. Zhou was pleased and promised to perform plays in gratitude. However, within a few days, the child died. Zhou became furious, saying, "I relied on my son to farm and support me; his death is worse than mine." He led his wife to hit the statue of the ox-headed deity with a hoe and a spade, destroying its body and damaging the temple. The entire village was shocked, believing this would bring a great misfortune. Afterward, it became quiet, and the ox-headed deity disappeared without a trace.

    In another incident, Magistrate Wang Yitang from Jiangning, while visiting a friend at Water Stabilizing Hermitage in the outskirts of Gubeikou, stopped at the hermitage to rest. The peonies in the hermitage were in full bloom, with flowers as large as a winnowing basket.

    Wang approached to admire the flowers. The monk warned, "Do not pick the flowers; they are said to be possessed by a spirit that can cause harm." Wang, who was usually stubborn, laughed and said, "I did not intend to pick the flowers, but since they are said to be possessed, I will try picking them to test the claim." He tried to pick the flower with his hands, but it spun around and was as tough as cowhide, unable to be broken. He then tried to cut it with his knife, but the flower was not cut, and his thumb was injured, causing blood to flow. Wang felt ashamed and angry, wrapped his bleeding thumb with his sleeve, and endured the pain in silence. He then used his left hand to hold the flower head and his right hand to cut the root, finally breaking off a branch. He took the broken branch back and kept it in a vase, boasting to others that he had captured the spirit of the flower. He intended to buy medicine to treat his wound but found no cut on his hand and no bloodstains on his sleeve.

    485

    ○乌台粤东肇庆府，即古端州，包孝肃旧治也。大堂暖阁后有黑井，覆对铁板，为出入所必经，相传包公纳妖于井。俗有“包收卢放马成湖”之谣，谓太守遇卢姓则妖出，遇马姓则井溢也。然千百年来，亦从无此二姓为守者。署东有高楼，号曰“乌台”，俗谓包公听断妖鬼皆坐此台。四面砖石封固，启则为祟。凡太守履任，必祀以少牢，无敢启视者。

    　　前任安守有管厨人某，酒醉登楼巅，揭瓦窥之，见台中有三土堆，品字排列，如小坟状，中间小树一株，枝青叶绿，此外一无他物。方瞪视间，有黑气冲起，厨人自楼巅滚跌于地，颤汗交作，仅能言所见。至夕，狂叫而死。越日，安公暴染疯狂，鞭扑其妻，竟至身死；又手刃其爱妾，以此落职获谴。

    Explanation

    485

    The Crow Tower in Yue East, Zhaoqing Prefecture, is the ancient Duanzhou, where Bao Zheng once governed. Behind the warm hall of the main hall, there is a black well covered by a metal plate, which is a necessary passage for entering and exiting. It is said that Bao Gong imprisoned an evil spirit in this well. There is a folk saying, "Bao receives Lu, releases Ma, and forms a lake," meaning that if the prefect has the surname Lu, an evil spirit will emerge; if the prefect has the surname Ma, the well will overflow. However, for over a thousand years, there has never been a prefect with these two surnames. East of the office, there is a tall building called the "Crow Tower," a place where it is believed that Bao Gong listened to cases and dealt with ghosts. The tower is sealed with bricks and stones on all sides, and opening it would bring misfortune. Whenever a new prefect takes office, they must offer a sacrifice of a pig and a sheep, and no one dares to look inside.

    A former prefect named An had a kitchen manager who, after drinking too much, climbed to the top of the tower and removed some tiles to peek inside. He saw three earthen mounds arranged in the shape of a character, resembling small tombs, with a small tree in the middle, its branches green and leaves lush. There was nothing else. As he stared, a black mist rose up, and the kitchen manager rolled down from the top of the tower, trembling and sweating profusely, barely able to describe what he had seen. That night, he went mad, screaming and died. The next day, An Prefect suddenly became insane, beating his wife until she died, and then killing his beloved concubine, leading to his dismissal and punishment.

    486

    越两任后，家弟香亭出守是郡，家信来为言若此。余闻而大怒，寄信云：“此说荒唐可也，若真有其事，则楼神不法甚矣，断非包公旧迹！弟何不拆而焚之？”

    　　○鬼娘堡顺治乙酉，王师破建昌，明益王遁去。长史刘某，吴下人也，逃山中，不知所往。其子蓼萧，从吴门赴考归，有志寻亲。时藩府荒圮，莫可踪迹，乃祷于盱江张令公祠，梦神书“石氵祭”二字与之，醒而徨不知何地。遇一尼告曰：“石氵祭在闽广之交，阻兵难行。幸有曲径，七日可达。”

    　　如其言，历尽危险，竟至其地。父母依村农姚氏居焉，母子相持而泣。父已死矣，乃持丧奉母而归。所居村名“见娘堡”，名已奇矣。尤奇者，长史避难时，携家谱一册自随，戊子岁，其母闻声出自箧中，以为鼠也，启视无有，闭则复燃。一日，见绯衣人数辈冉冉从箧中走出，益大惊，逾时而孝子至。

    　　此事载姜桓溟文集中，韩尚书为之表墓。

    Explanation

    486

    Translation

    After two terms, my younger brother Xiangting was dispatched to govern this prefecture, and a family letter came to report something like this. I heard it and became greatly angry, sending a letter that said: "This story may be absurd, but if it is true, then the deity of the tower is very unrighteous, certainly not a trace of Judge Bao! Why didn't you tear it apart and burn it?"

    ○ Ghost Mother Village

    In the year Shunzhi Yihou (1655), the imperial army defeated Jianchang, and the Ming Prince of Yizhou fled. Long Shi Liu, a man from Wu, fled to the mountains, and no one knows where he went. His son Liao Xiao, returning from taking an examination in Suzhou, was determined to search for his relatives. At the time, the provincial government was in ruins, and no trace could be found. He prayed at the Zhang Linggong Temple on the Xuyang River and dreamed of a deity writing the words "Shishi" on a tablet for him. Upon waking, he wandered aimlessly, not knowing where he was. He met a nun who told him: "Shishi is between Fujian and Guangdong, obstructed by military forces, making it difficult to travel. Fortunately, there is a winding path that can be reached in seven days."

    Following her advice, he went through great danger and finally arrived at the place. His parents were living with a village farmer named Yao, and they embraced each other and wept. His father had already died, so he performed the funeral rites and served his mother before returning home. The village where they lived was called "Jian Niangbu," a name already strange. What was even more strange was that when Long Shi Liu was seeking refuge, he carried a single volume of his family tree with him. In the year Wuzi (1678), his mother heard a strange sound coming from a chest, thinking it was a mouse. She opened it to find nothing, but when she closed it, the sound would light up again. One day, she saw several red-clothed figures slowly emerging from the chest, greatly surprising her. After some time, her filial son arrived.

    This matter is recorded in the writings of Jiang Huaming, and Han Shangshu Wei wrote an epitaph for his tomb.

    487

    ○鬼糊涂乾隆三十九年，京师有无赖子韩六殴伤其父，刑部审明，下狱拟斩。侍郎某以所殴非致命处，意欲减等发落。大司寇秦公奏：“名分所关，理宜正法。”奉旨依议，遣刑部司狱司李怀中监斩。后三日，鬼附李身，口称：“诸大人业已宽我，而汝来斩我。我死不甘，故来索命。”闻者骇然，以为此鬼糊涂，然而李竟不起。

    　　○鬼势利张八郎有所欢婢，婚后弃之。婢幽怨成疾，临死曰：“我不饶八郎！”语毕气绝。忽又张目曰：“八郎运甚旺，不能报仇，我捉八奶奶也中一样。”未二年，八郎夫人竟以产亡。

    　　○鬼相思岳州张某，号“鬼三爷”，以其行三，为鬼所生故也。父某府学廪生，妻陈氏有色，忽凭妖，自称郧阳小神，白昼现形，与之交接。张虽同床，无故自离，若有梏其手足者。其家遍请符，毫无效验。三月后，陈氏受胎生子，空中群鬼啾啾争来作贺，掷下纸钱无数。张忿甚，将到龙虎山求救于天师。

    Explanation

    487

    ○ Confused Ghost
    In the 39th year of the Qianlong Emperor's reign, there was a rascal named Han Liu in the capital city who beat his father, causing injuries. After the Ministry of Punishment investigated and confirmed the case, he was sentenced to death and imprisoned. A certain Minister of State argued that since the injuries were not fatal, he intended to reduce the sentence. The Chief Justice, Qin Gong, presented an argument: "This case involves a violation of filial piety, and it should be punished according to the law." The emperor agreed with the opinion and ordered the execution to be carried out by Li Huizhong, the head of the prison office. Three days later, a ghost possessed Li Huizhong and said, "The great officials have already shown leniency towards me, yet you come to execute me. I will not accept this, so I come to demand my life." Those who heard this were shocked, thinking the ghost was confused, but Li Huizhong died shortly after.

    ○ Self-Serving Ghost
    Zhang Balang had a beloved maid whom he abandoned after marriage. The maid became ill with resentment and, on her deathbed, said, "I will not forgive Zhang Balang!" She then passed away. Suddenly, she opened her eyes and said, "Zhang Balang's fortune is very strong; he cannot be avenged. I will also catch his wife in the same way." Within two years, Zhang Balang's wife died in childbirth.

    ○ Longing Ghost
    In Yuezhou, there was a man named Zhang San爷, nicknamed "Ghost Third Brother," because he was the third son and believed to be the son of a ghost. His father was a stipendiary student in the local government school, and his wife, Chen, was beautiful. One day, she was possessed by a Yunyang Minor God who appeared during the day and interacted with her. Zhang San爷, despite being in the same bed, felt as if his hands and feet were bound. The family tried various charms and talismans but found no relief. Three months later, Chen gave birth to a son, and a chorus of ghosts gathered to celebrate, dropping countless paper offerings. Zhang San爷 became very angry and planned to seek help from a Celestial Master at Longhu Mountain.

    488

    忽一日，小神踉跄来，汗如雨下，语其妻曰：“‘吾几闯祸！昨夜入汝邻毛家偷其金盆，被他家所挂钟馗拔剑相逐，我惧，为所伤，不得已急走，将金盆掷在巷西池塘中，脱逃来此。汝速具酒，替我压惊。”次日，妻告张，张往毛府刺探，果失金盆，合家喧吵，将控官捉贼。张止之曰：“我有法替汝取来，作何谢我？”毛氏大喜，曰：“果得金盆，凭君取索。”张诡作念咒状，良久，唤毛氏家人径往塘所，命善泅者入水取之，果得金盆。

    　　毛延张上座，问：“以何物作谢？”张笑曰：“我读书人，不受财帛，只须君家收藏书画与我一二件足矣。”其家尽出所藏，张选取文征明芙蓉一幅。其家觉谢礼太薄，心抱不安。张乃指壁上所挂钟馗像曰：“赐此画，凑成两件何如？”

    Explanation

    488

    Suddenly one day, the little deity stumbled in, drenched in sweat, and told his wife, “I almost caused trouble! Last night, I entered your neighbor Mao’s house to steal their gold basin. I was chased by the Zhong Kui hanging in their house, with a drawn sword. I was afraid, got injured, and had no choice but to run away urgently. I threw the gold basin into the pond in the west alley and escaped here. Quickly prepare some wine to calm my nerves.” The next day, the wife informed Zhang, who went to investigate at the Mao residence. Indeed, the gold basin was missing, and the family was in a commotion, planning to report to the authorities to catch the thief. Zhang stopped them and said, “I have a way to retrieve it for you. What will you give me as a reward?” The Mao family was delighted and said, “If you can get the gold basin, you can take whatever you want.” Zhang pretended to chant spells for a long time, then called the Mao family to the pond and ordered a good swimmer to retrieve the basin from the water. Sure enough, the gold basin was found.

    The Mao family invited Zhang to sit and asked, “What will you accept as a reward?” Zhang smiled and said, “I am a scholar and do not accept money. I only need a few pieces of your family’s collection of paintings and calligraphy.” The family showed all their treasures, and Zhang selected a painting of lotus flowers by Wen Zhengming. The family felt the reward was too small and felt uneasy. Zhang then pointed to the Zhong Kui painting hanging on the wall and said, “Grant this painting to me as well, and it will make up for the reward.”

    489

    毛氏唯唯。张取归，悬空中，小神从此永不再来，但闻园中树上鬼哀哭三日。人称“鬼相思”云。

    　　○关神世法康熙癸卯举人江，选某县令，丁忧妇。将起复时，梦有甲士来，自称周仓，服饰如今庙中所塑而少年无须，手持名帖，上写“治年家弟关某顿首拜”。惊醒大笑，以为关帝行此世法。未几，选山西解梁知县。往谒武庙，旁塑周仓，果少年无须者也，面貌恍如梦中。乃捐俸重修神庙，后竟卒于任所。江公即于九江太守之叔，太守为余言。

    　　○乡试弥封皖江程叔才，名思恭，学问博雅，注陈检讨四六得名。以平时好古，不喜时文，其师唐赤子太史责之曰：“科名进身，非此不可。今岁入场之年，汝宜留意。”

    Explanation

    489

    Ma responded humbly. Zhang took it away and hung it in the air. The little spirit never came back again, and only the ghosts on the trees in the garden mourned for three days. People called it "ghostly longing."

    ○关神世法康熙癸卯举人江，选某县令，丁忧妇。将起复时，梦有甲士来，自称周仓，服饰如今庙中所塑而少年无须，手持名帖，上写“治年家弟关某顿首拜”。惊醒大笑，以为关帝行此世法。未几，选山西解梁知县。往谒武庙，旁塑周仓，果少年无须者也，面貌恍如梦中。乃捐俸重修神庙，后竟卒于任所。江公即于九江太守之叔，太守为余言。

    Jiang Jiu, a scholar-official from the Kangxi era, was selected as a county magistrate. During his period of mourning for his wife, he dreamed of a soldier in armor who called himself Zhou Cang. The soldier was dressed like those in the temple, a young man without a beard, and held a name card that read, "Governor of the year's younger brother, Guan, respectfully bows." He woke up in shock and laughed, thinking that Guan Di was performing this worldly law. Shortly after, he was selected as the magistrate of Jie County in Shanxi. He went to visit the Martial Temple, where a statue of Zhou Cang was carved on the side, indeed a young man without a beard, and the appearance was exactly as in his dream. He then donated his salary to rebuild the temple. Later, he died in his post. Jiang was the uncle of the governor of Jiujiang, who told me this story.

    ○乡试弥封皖江程叔才，名思恭，学问博雅，注陈检讨四六得名。以平时好古，不喜时文，其师唐赤子太史责之曰：“科名进身，非此不可。今岁入场之年，汝宜留意。”

    Cheng Shucai, from Wanjiang, whose given name was Siguang, was known for his broad and refined learning and gained fame for his annotations on the Chen Jian (a collection of essays). Because he was fond of ancient texts and did not favor contemporary literary styles, his teacher, the historian Tang Chizi, criticized him, saying, "To advance in the imperial examinations, one cannot do without this. This year, when you enter the examination hall, you should pay attention."

    490

    因强之诵读金、陈诸大家文，程唯唯，终非所好，《四书体注》等书，临场并不翻阅。

    　　康熙戊戌科，江南首题《举贤才焉知贤才而举之》，次题《大哉圣人之道》。

    　　程三场毕，自言首篇颇得意，唐太史读之喜曰：“颇可望魁。”程急取案头《中庸》一看，愕然丧气曰：“不中用了。我只道‘大哉圣人之道’在‘礼仪三百、威仪三千’之下，故领题、出题俱承接此二句，今方知是开首第一句，则通身犯下矣，其不中尚复何言。”唐亦为之悼叹。

    　　已而榜发，竟中第五名。唐不解所以得售之故，往见主试，将探问之。主试某，故唐公同年，一见笑曰：“今年科场中有笑语，兄知否？”唐问故，曰：“皇上有密旨，谓诸生关节都放在破承、领题、出题三处，今岁将此三处尽行弥封，故有程某文字领题、出题全行犯下，竟中五魁，将来磨勘，定受参罚，奈何？”

    　　唐笑而不言。后叔才先生果被吏部磨勘，罚停一科。

    Explanation

    490

    Because he was forced to recite and read the works of famous writers from the Jin and Chen dynasties, Cheng obediently, but ultimately not what he liked. He did not consult annotated editions of the Four Books during the examination.

    In the year 1718 during the Kangxi era of the Qing dynasty, the first question in the southern Jiangnan examination was "How to identify and promote virtuous talents," and the second question was "The great way of the sage."

    After completing the three examinations, Cheng said he felt quite pleased with the first essay. Tang Taishi, upon reading it, expressed his hope that Cheng would win the top prize. Cheng quickly looked at the Doctrine of the Mean on his desk, startled and disheartened, and said, "It's no use now. I thought 'the great way of the sage' was under 'the three hundred rituals and the three thousand ceremonies,' so the lead-in to the question and the main question both followed these two sentences. Now I realize it is the first sentence at the beginning, so throughout it violates the rules. If it doesn't pass, what more can be said?" Tang also grieved and sighed for this.

    Later, the list was posted, and he ended up being the fifth place. Tang did not understand the reason why he was successful, so he went to see the chief examiner to ask about it. The chief examiner (a certain person) was a fellow examinee of Tang. On seeing, he smiled and said, "There was a joke this year in the examination, do you know?" Tang asked why, and the chief examiner said, "The emperor had a secret decree, saying that all the candidates' crucial points were placed in the transition, lead-in to the question, and main question. This year, all these three places were sealed up. So there was Cheng's writing, the lead-in to the question and the main question completely violated the rules, and he ended up being the fifth place. In the future, during the review, he will definitely be punished, what to do?"

    Tang smiled without saying anything. Later, Mr. Shucai was indeed reviewed by the Ministry of Personnel and was punished by being barred from the next examination.

    491

    ○两汪土钅宏顺治间，徽州汪日衡先生元旦梦行天榜：会元汪士钅宏。先生乃改名应之，竟终身不第。直至康熙某科，汪退谷先生中会元，榜名士钅宏。相隔四十馀年，日衡先生死久矣，孙某记乃祖之言，相与叹造化弄人，亦觉无谓。

    　　○雷击土地康熙间，石埭令汪以斤素与其友林某交好。后林死，为石埭土地神，每夜间，阴阳虽隔，而两人来往如平生欢。土地私谓汪曰：“君家有难，我不敢不告，第告君后，恐我难逃天谴。”汪再三问，曰：“尊堂太夫人分当雷击。”汪大惊，号泣求救。土地曰：“此是前生恶劫，我官卑职小，如何能救？”汪泣请不已，神曰：“只有一法可救，汝速尽孝养之道，凡太夫人平日一饮一馔、一帐一衣，务使十倍其数，浪费而暴餮之，庶几禄尽则亡，可以善终，雷虽来无益也。”汪如其言，其母果不数年而卒。

    　　又三年，天雨，雷果至，绕棺照耀，满房硫磺气，卒不下，破屋而出，飞击土地庙。塑像成泥。

    Explanation

    491

    ○ Two Wonders of the Earth in the Shunzhi Era, in Huizhou, Mr. Wang Ribang had a dream on New Year's Day where he saw the imperial examination results: the top scorer, Wang Shiming. Mr. Wang then changed his name to Yingzhi, but he failed the exams throughout his life. It wasn't until the Kangxi era, in a certain year, that Mr. Wang Tuigu passed the exam as the top scorer, with the name Shiming. Forty years later, Mr. Wang Ribang had long since passed away. His grandson recorded his grandfather's words and together they lamented the capriciousness of fate, finding it all rather pointless.

    ○ In the Kangxi era, the magistrate of Shidai, Mr. Wang Yijin, had been close friends with Mr. Lin. After Mr. Lin died, he became the local land god. Every night, despite the separation of the living and the dead, the two continued to meet as if they were still friends. The land god privately told Mr. Wang, "Your family is in trouble, and I dare not keep this from you, but after I tell you, I fear I will face divine retribution." Mr. Wang asked repeatedly, and the land god said, "Your respected mother-in-law is destined to be struck by lightning." Mr. Wang was greatly alarmed and wept, begging for help. The land god replied, "This is a calamity from a past life. My position is lowly, so how could I save her?" Mr. Wang continued to plead, and the land god said, "There is only one way to save her. You must quickly fulfill your filial duties. For every drink, meal, bedding, and clothing that your mother-in-law usually has, you must provide ten times that amount, wasting and indulging excessively. Perhaps when her good fortune runs out, she can die a peaceful death, and even if the lightning comes, it will be of no use." Mr. Wang followed his advice, and his mother-in-law indeed passed away within a few years.

    Three years later, it rained heavily, and the lightning struck as predicted. It circled the coffin, illuminating the room with a sulfurous smell, but it did not strike the coffin. Instead, it broke through the house and flew to the land god temple, reducing the statue to mud.

    492

    ○张光熊直隶张光熊，幼而聪俊，年十八，居西楼读书。家豪富，多婢妾，而父母范之甚严。七月七日，感牛郎织女事，望星而坐，妄想此夕可有家婢来窥读书者否？

    　　心乍动，见帘外一美女侧身立，唤之不应。少顷，冉冉至前。视之，非家中婢也。

    　　问：“何姓？”曰：“姓王。”问：“居何处？”曰：“君之西邻。晨夕见郎出入，爱郎姿貌，故来相就。”张喜，即与同榻。此后每夕必至。

    　　有家僮伴宿，女谓张曰：“小奴不宜在此，可麾令远宿，听唤再至。”张遣奴，奴不肯，曰：“每夜闻郎君枕席间妮妮软语，疑有别故。老主人命奴调护郎君，不敢远离。”张无奈何，以其言告女。女曰：“无庸，将自困。”是夕，奴未睡熟，被一物攫去，绳缚之，挂西园树上，奴哀号求郎主救命。女笑曰：“伊果知罪，远避即赦之。如敢漏泄，被老主人知者，将倍令受苦。”奴唯唯。即时绳解，奴已在地矣。

    Explanation

    492

    Zhang Guangxiong, a young man from the province of Zhili, was intelligent and handsome from a young age. At eighteen, he lived in the west tower, engrossed in his studies. His family was wealthy, with many concubines, but his parents disciplined him very strictly. On the seventh day of the seventh month, inspired by the story of the Cowherd and the Weaver Girl, he sat and gazed at the stars, fantasizing whether there might be a household maid coming to peek at the reader that night.

    Suddenly, his heart stirred, and he saw a beautiful woman standing sideways outside the curtain. He called out to her, but she did not respond. After a while, she slowly approached. He looked at her and realized she was not one of the maids from his household.

    He asked, "What is your surname?" She replied, "My surname is Wang." He then asked, "Where do you live?" She answered, "I live next to you on the west side. I see you coming and going in the morning and evening, and I love your appearance, so I come to join you." Zhang Guangxiong was delighted and immediately shared the bed with her. From then on, she came every night.

    There was a household servant who accompanied him at night. The woman told Zhang Guangxiong, "The little servant should not be here; you can order him to stay far away, and come back when called." Zhang Guangxiong sent the servant away, but the servant refused, saying, "Every night I hear the soft and gentle words of the young master in his bed, suspecting there might be something else. The old master orders me to take care of the young master, and I dare not go far away." Zhang Guangxiong had no choice but to tell the woman about the servant's words. The woman said, "There is no need, I will do it myself."

    That night, the servant was not yet asleep when something grabbed him, tied him up with ropes, and hung him on a tree in the western garden. The servant cried out for help from the young master. The woman laughed and said, "If he really knows he is guilty, he will be forgiven if he stays far away. If he dares to leak it and the old master finds out, he will be made to suffer even more." The servant nodded. Immediately, the ropes were untied, and the servant was on the ground.

    493

    居年余，张渐羸瘦，其父问奴，奴称郎处无他故，而意色惭沮。父愈疑，自至张斋前伺察。闻帐中有妇女声，蹋窗直入，揭帐无人，惟枕角有金簪一枝、山查花一朵。父念此地从无山查花，此必妖魅所致，怒将笞张。张不得已，以实告。

    　　父为迎名僧法官设坛禁咒。女夜间来哭谓张曰：“天机已泄，请从此辞。”张亦哀恸，临别问曰：“尚有相会期乎？”曰：“二十年后华州相见。”从此遂绝。

    　　张随娶陈氏，登进士第，授吴江知县。推升华州知州，而陈氏卒。其父在家为续娶王某之女，送至华州官署。成婚却扇之夕，新人容貌，宛如书斋伴宿之人，问年纪，刚二十岁。或曰：“此狐仙感情欲而托生也。”语从前事，恰不记忆。

    Explanation

    493

    After residing for more than a year, Zhang gradually became thin and weak. His father questioned the servant, who claimed that there was no other reason for the young master, but his expression and demeanor were ashamed and disheartened. The father became even more suspicious and went to Zhang's study to spy. He heard a woman's voice in the canopy and broke through the window, lifting the curtain to find no one there, only a gold hairpin and a mountain cherry flower on the pillow corner. The father thought that there were no mountain cherry flowers in this place, so this must be due to an evil spirit. He was angry and wanted to beat Zhang. Zhang had no choice but to tell the truth.

    The father then invited a famous monk and a judge to set up a ritual to exorcise evil spirits. The woman came at night and wept, telling Zhang, "The secret has been revealed, please let me leave." Zhang also grieved deeply and, on the verge of leaving, asked, "Is there still a chance to meet again?" She replied, "We will meet in Hua Zhou after twenty years." From then on, they never met again.

    Zhang then married Chen, passed the imperial examination, and was appointed as the magistrate of Wujiang. He was promoted to the position of governor of Hua Zhou, but Chen passed away. His father at home arranged for him to remarry the daughter of Wang, who was sent to the official residence in Hua Zhou. On the night of the wedding when the fan was removed, the new wife's appearance was exactly like the person who stayed with Zhang in the study. When asked about her age, she was just twenty years old. Some say, "This is a fox spirit who was reborn out of love and desire." When asked about past events, she did not remember.

    494

    ○赵氏再婚成怨偶雍正间，布政司郑禅宝妻赵氏有容德，与郑恩好甚隆，以瘵疾亡。临诀誓曰：“愿生生世世为夫妇。”卒之日，旗下刘某家生一女，生而能言，曰：“我郑家妻也。”刘父母大惊，以为怪，嗣后遂不复语。

    　　八岁过亲戚家，路遇郑家奴骑马冲其车，怒曰：“汝郑四也，自幼卖身我家，何敢见我不下马？”郑奴愕然，因访至刘家，见女父母，具道生时之异。女归见郑四，因问：“汝主安否？”并询一切妯娌上下奴婢田宅事，历历如绘，有奴所不知而女悉知者。奴归，白之郑。郑亦至刘家，女谛视涕泣，絮语良久。时鄂西林相公以为两世婚姻，亦太平瑞事，劝郑续娶刘女。十四岁即行合卺之礼。时郑年六旬，白发飘萧，兼有继室。女嫁年馀，郁郁不乐，竟缢死。

    494

    A Tragic Remarriage: Zhao's Second Marriage Turned into a Bitter Couple

    During the reign of Emperor Yongzheng, Zhao, the wife of Zheng Chanchao, a high-ranking official, was known for her beauty and had a deep affection for Zheng. She died of tuberculosis. On her deathbed, she vowed, "I wish to be husband and wife in every life." On the day of her death, a girl was born into the household of Liu, a Manchu family under the Eight Banners system. The girl was born and able to speak, saying, "I am the wife of the Zheng family." Her parents were shocked and considered it a strange occurrence, and she ceased speaking afterward.

    At the age of eight, she visited a relative's house and encountered Zheng's servant riding a horse that nearly hit her carriage. She angrily said, "You are Zheng Si, who was sold into service in my family since childhood. How dare you not dismount when you see me?" Zheng's servant was startled and went to Liu's house to explain the girl's unusual statement. The girl returned home and saw Zheng Si, asking, "Is your master well?" She also inquired about all the details of Zheng's household, including his wife, relatives, servants, and property, and she knew everything in great detail, even things the servant did not know. The servant returned and informed Zheng. Zheng also went to Liu's house, where the girl looked at him intently, wept, and spoke at length.

    At the time, the prominent official or scholar, Mr. Xu, believed that this remarriage was a sign of good fortune and encouraged Zheng to remarry the girl. At the age of fourteen, they performed the traditional wedding ceremony. Zheng was sixty years old, with white hair, and already had a second wife. After the marriage, the girl became depressed and unhappy, and eventually hanged herself.

    495

    袁子曰：情极而缘生，缘满而情又绝，异哉！

    　　○童其澜绍兴童其澜，乾隆元年进士，官户部员外。一日，值宿衙门，与同官数人夜饮，忽仰天咤曰：“天使到矣！”披朝衣再拜俯伏。同官问：“何天使？”童笑曰：“人无二天，何问之有？天有敕书一卷，如中书阁诰封，云中金甲人捧头上而来，命我作东便门外花儿闸河神。将与诸公别矣。”言毕泣下，同官以为得狂易之疾，不甚介意。

    　　次早，大司农海望到户部，童具冠带长揖辞官，具白所以。海曰：“君读书君子，办事明敏，如有病，不妨乞假，何必以神怪惑人？”童亦不辨，驾车归家，不饮不食，将家事料理。三日，端坐而逝。

    Explanation

    495

    袁子曰：When emotions reach their extreme, a bond is formed; when the bond is fulfilled, the emotion is severed, how strange!

    ○Tong Qilan, a native of Shaoxing, was a Jinshi (imperial scholar) in the first year of the Qianlong era and served as an official in the Ministry of Revenue. One day, while on night duty at the government office, he shared a drink with several of his colleagues. Suddenly, he looked up to the sky and shouted, "The heavenly messenger has arrived!" He put on his official robes, bowed twice, and prostrated himself. His colleagues asked, "What heavenly messenger?" Tong smiled and said, "There is only one heaven, what need is there to ask? Heaven has a decree in one scroll, like a royal edict from the imperial archives, brought by a golden-armored person from the clouds, who ordered me to be the river god of the flower gate in the east. I will be parting ways with you all." After speaking, he wept, and his colleagues thought he had gone mad and did not take it too seriously.

    The next morning, the Grand Administrator Hai Wang visited the Ministry of Revenue, and Tong, dressed in his formal attire, bowed deeply and resigned from his position, explaining the reason. Hai said, "You are a learned gentleman who handles affairs with clarity and efficiency. If you are ill, you can take leave without worry, why should you confuse people with such supernatural tales?" Tong did not argue, drove home, neither eating nor drinking, and began to settle his family affairs. Three days later, he sat calmly and passed away.

    496

    东便门外居民闻连夜呼驺声，以为有贵官过，就视无有。花儿闸河神庙中道士叶某梦新河神到任，白皙微须，长不逾中人，果童公貌也。

    　　○镜山寺僧钱塘王孝廉鼎实，余戊午同年。少聪颖，年十六举于乡。三试春官不第，有至戚官都下，留之邸中。偶感微疾，即屏去饮食，日啜凉水数杯，语其戚曰：“予前世镜山寺僧某也，修持数十年，几成大道。惟平生见少年登科者，辄心艳之；又华富之慕未能尽绝，以此尚须两世堕落，今其一世也。不数日当托生华富家，即顺治门外姚姓是也。君之留我不出都，想亦是定数耶！”其戚劝慰之，王曰：“去来有定，难以久留，惟父母生我之恩不能遽割。”乃索纸作别父书，大略云：“儿不幸客死数千里外，又年寿短促，遗少妻弱息，为堂上累。然儿非父母真子，有弟某乃父母之真子也。吾父曾忆某年在茶肆与镜山寺某僧饮茶事耶？

    Explanation

    496

    Residents near the East Gate heard the continuous sound of whips at night and thought a high-ranking official was passing by. They looked around but saw no one. Taoist Ye from the Flower Gate River God Temple dreamt that a new river god had taken office, appearing pale with a slight mustache, no taller than an average person, indeed resembling a child.

    ○ The monk Wang Dingshi from Jing Shan Temple in Qiantang, my fellow candidate from the year of Wuwu, was young and intelligent, passing the local examination at the age of sixteen. After failing the imperial examinations three times, he was invited by a close relative who was an official in the capital to stay in his residence. One day, he fell slightly ill, and immediately stopped eating and drinking, instead consuming only a few cups of cold water each day. He told his relative, "I was a monk named Wang from Jing Shan Temple in my previous life, having practiced for decades and nearly achieving enlightenment. However, I was always envious of young scholars who passed the examinations, and my admiration for wealth and luxury was not entirely eradicated. Because of this, I still need to experience two more lifetimes of decline, and this is my first one. In a few days, I will be reborn into a wealthy family, specifically the Yao family outside the Shunzhi Gate. Your insistence on keeping me in the capital, I suppose, is also part of my destiny!" His relative tried to comfort him, but Wang said, "Destiny is fixed, and I cannot stay here for long. However, I cannot sever the bond of my parents' kindness so easily." He then asked for paper to write a farewell letter to his father, which roughly read: "My son unfortunately died far away from home, with a short life, leaving behind a young wife and weak children, causing trouble for my parents. However, I am not your true son; my younger brother is. Do you remember, Father, the time we drank tea together with a monk from Jing Shan Temple at a teahouse?"

    497

    儿即僧也。时与父谈甚洽，心念父忠诚谨厚，何造物者乃不与之后耶？一念之动，遂来为儿。儿妇亦是幼年时小有善缘。镜花水月，都是幻聚，何能久处？父幸勿以真儿视儿，速断爱牵，庶免儿之罪戾。“其戚问：”生姚家当以何日？“王曰：”予此生无罪过，此灭则彼生，不须轮回。“

    　　越三日巳刻，索水盥漱毕，趺坐胡床，召其戚，欢笑如平时，问：“日午未？”

    Explanation

    497

    I am the monk. At the time, I was in deep conversation with my father, and in my heart, I thought of his loyalty, sincerity, and kindness. Why did the creator not give him a future? A single thought moved me, and thus I came to be his son. My wife was also a bit of a good connection in her youth. Like reflections in mirrors and moonlight on water, they are all illusions; how can they last long? Father, please do not regard me as your true son. Quickly sever the ties of love, so as to avoid my sins. His relative asked, "When will you be born into the Yao family?" The king replied, "In this life, I have no sin. When this life ends, the next begins, and there is no need for rebirth."

    Three days later at the hour of Si, I asked for water to wash and brush my teeth, then sat cross-legged on a trundle bed. I called for my relative, and we laughed and talked as usual. I asked, "Is it noon yet?"

    498

    曰：“正午。”曰：“是其时也。”拱手作别而逝。其戚访之姚家，果于是日生一子，家业骡马行，有数万金。

    　　○江秀才寄话婺源江秀才号慎修，名永，能制奇器。取猪尿脬置黄豆，以气吹满，而缚其口，豆浮正中。益信“地如鸡子黄”之说。有愿为弟子者，便令先对此脬坐视七日，不厌不倦，方可教也。家中耕田，悉用木牛。行城外，骑一木驴，不食不鸣。

    　　人以为妖，笑曰：“此武侯成法，不过中用机关耳，非妖也。”置一竹筒，中用玻璃为盖，有钥开之。开则向筒说数千言，言毕即闭。传千里内，人开筒侧耳，其音宛在，如面谈也；过千里，则音渐渐散不全矣。

    　　忽一日自投于水，乡人惊救之，半溺而起，大恨曰：“吾今而知数之难逃也。

    　　吾二子外游于楚，今日未时三刻，理应同溺洞庭。吾欲以老身代之。今诸公救我，必无人救二子矣。“不半月，凶问果至。此其弟子戴震为余言。

    Explanation

    498

    A man said, "Noon." Another replied, "It is the right time." They bowed their hands and departed. Their relatives went to the Yao family and found that indeed, on that day, a son was born. The family business dealt with mules and horses, and they had tens of thousands of gold coins.

    ○ Jiang Xiucai, a scholar from Wuyuan, was known as Shenxiu and named Yong. He was skilled in creating strange devices. He would take a pig bladder, place yellow beans inside, blow it up with air, and tie its mouth. The beans would float in the center. This reinforced his belief in the saying, "The earth is like the yolk of an egg." For those who wished to become his disciples, he would instruct them to sit and observe this bladder for seven days, without tiring or being bored, before they could be taught. At home, he used wooden oxen for plowing. When traveling outside the city, he rode a wooden donkey, which neither ate nor made a sound.

    People thought it was a demon and laughed, saying, "This is Zhuge Liang's method; it is merely a clever mechanism, not a demon." He placed a bamboo tube with a glass lid in the middle, secured by a key. When opened, he would speak thousands of words into the tube, and when finished, he would close it. Within a thousand miles, people could open the tube and listen, and the sound would still be present, as if they were talking face-to-face; beyond a thousand miles, the sound would gradually disperse and become incomplete.

    One day, he suddenly threw himself into the water. The villagers were shocked and rescued him, and he came up half-drowned. He greatly regretted and said, "I now know that fate cannot be escaped. My two sons are traveling in Chu, and today at three quarters past noon, they should have drowned together in Dongting Lake. I wanted to replace them with my old self. Now that you have saved me, there will be no one to save my two sons." Within half a month, the bad news indeed arrived. This was told to me by his disciple Dai Zhen.

    499

    ●卷十四○勾魂卒苏州余姓者，好斗蟋蟀，每秋暮，携盆往葑门外搜取，薄夜方归。

    　　一日归晚，城门已闭，余惊骇无计，徘徊路侧。见二青衣远来，履橐橐有声，向余笑曰：“君此时将安归乎？我家离此不远，盍宿我家？”余喜从之。至则双扉大启，室中置旧书数部，磁瓶铜炉各一。余手持蟋蟀十数盆，腹饿甚，映灯而坐。二青衣各持酒脯来，相与对啖。隐隐闻病者呻吟乃众人喧杂声，余问故，二人曰：“此邻家患病者势甚迫故也。”

    　　未几，漏下五鼓，二人相与耳语曰：“事宜办矣。”出靴中文书一通，谓余曰：“请君呵气纸上。”余不解其故，笑而从之。呵毕，二青衣喜，以脚跨屋上而舞，长丈余，皆鸡爪也。余大惊，正欲问之，二人不见，壁外哭声大作。余方知所遇非人，是勾魂鬼也。

    Explanation

    499

    A man from Suzhou, surnamed Yu, loved to fight crickets. Every autumn evening, he would take a pot and go out to search for crickets at the Fong Gate, returning late at night.

    One day, he returned late, and the city gate was already closed. Yu was shocked and had no plan, wandering by the roadside. He saw two figures dressed in green approaching, their footsteps making a橐橐 sound. They smiled at him and said, "Where will you go at this time? My home is not far from here. Why not stay at my house?" Yu happily followed them.

    Upon arriving, he found the double doors wide open. Inside the room were several old books, a porcelain bottle, and a copper stove. Yu held several pots of crickets and was very hungry, sitting by the light of the lamp. The two figures each brought wine and food, and they ate together. He faintly heard the moaning of a sick person and the noise of many people. He asked them the reason, and they said, "This is the sound of a neighbor who is very ill."

    Shortly after, it was the fifth watch of the night. The two figures whispered to each other, "The matter is ready." They took out a piece of paper from their boots and told Yu, "Please breathe on the paper." Yu did not understand the reason but smiled and followed their instructions. After he finished, the two figures were pleased and stepped onto the roof, dancing with their feet spanning over a yard, their feet resembling chicken claws. Yu was greatly shocked and was about to ask them, but they disappeared, and the sound of crying grew loud outside the wall. He then realized that he had encountered not human beings but soul-summoning ghosts.

    500

    天明，启户欲出，则门外扃锁甚固，不得出，乃大呼。丧家人惊，开锁入，以为贼也，争殴之。余具道所以，且指蟋蟀盆为证曰：“岂有行窃而携此累坠物者乎？”丧家人亦有相识者，始得免。所餐酒脯盘盒，俱丧家物也，竟不知从何处携入，己身亦不解从何而进。

    　　○赵西席山东按察司白映棠，家延一西席，赵名康友，康熙丁卯孝廉，宾主师弟俱各相得。元宵张灯，彼此宴饮散，孝廉就寝书斋。次日薄午不起，有小僮户外窥之，见季廉头上插纸花双枝，两手反接，口微笑而目斜瞪，赤身僵立。僮大惊，唤主人蹋户入，则已死矣。当胸一圆洞，通于背，大如碗，中无心肝，不知被何物探去。插花反缚剥衣者，像牲牢之形，以戏之也。

    Explanation

    500

    At dawn, the person opened the door and wanted to go out, but the door was locked very firmly, so they could not leave and shouted loudly. The mourners were startled, opened the lock and entered, thinking it was a thief, and fought and beat the person. The person fully explained the reason and pointed to the cricket pot as evidence, saying, "How could there be a thief carrying such a heavy and cumbersome item?" The mourners also had acquaintances, and the person was finally spared. The food and drink, including the wine and meat, were all from the mourners' household, but it was unknown where the person brought them in, and the person did not understand how they entered themselves.

    ○ Mr. Zhao, a guest teacher at the Shandong Provincial Office of Inspection, invited a scholar named Bai Yingtang. Zhao's name was Kangyou, and he was a scholar of the Kangxi era. The host and guest, as well as the teacher and student, all got along well. During the Lantern Festival, they hung lanterns and feasted and drank together, then dispersed. The scholar went to bed in the study. The next day, he did not wake up until late morning. A young servant looked through the door and saw the scholar with two paper flowers in his hair, hands tied behind his back, smiling mouth and squinting eyes, standing naked and stiff. The young servant was greatly alarmed, called the master to break down the door and enter, and found the scholar already dead. There was a round hole through the chest to the back, as large as a bowl, with no heart or liver inside, and it was unknown what took it out. The actions of inserting flowers, tying hands behind, and stripping clothes resembled the shape of a sacrificial animal, to play with him.

    501

    ○杨四佐领杨四佐领者，性直而和，年四十余，忽谓家人曰：“昨夜梦金甲人呼我姓名，云：”第七殿阎罗王缺无人补，南岳神已将汝奏上帝，不日随班引见，汝速作朝衣朝冠候召。‘予再三辞，金甲神曰：“已经保奏，无可挽回，但喜所保者连汝共四人，或引见时上帝不用，则阳寿尚未绝。’言毕去。梦兆如此，决非偶然，家中可速制朝衣冠以待。”家人闻之，在疑信之间，犹未唤缝人为制衣也。是夕，金甲神又来曰：“命汝制新衣而缓懈，何耶？昨玉旨已降，点汝作阎罗，不必引见矣。”杨惊醒，急语家人毕，昏晕而逝。

    Explanation

    501

    Yang Si, the Fourth Banner Leader, was a straightforward and amiable man in his forties. Suddenly, he told his family, "Last night, I dreamed of a golden-armored person calling my name, who said, 'The seventh hall of the King of Hell is vacant and needs someone to fill the position. The God of Nan Yue has already presented you to the Heavenly Emperor. You will soon be introduced in a group. Prepare your court robes and hat in advance.' I repeatedly declined, but the golden-armored spirit said, 'It has already been recommended, and there is no way to change it. But I am glad that the four people recommended together with you. If the Heavenly Emperor does not use you at the introduction, then your earthly life has not yet ended.' After saying this, the spirit left. Such a dream is clearly not accidental. You should quickly prepare the court robes and hat at home." The family heard this and were in doubt, still not calling a tailor to make the clothes. That evening, the golden-armored spirit came again and said, "I ordered you to make new clothes, but you were slow. Why? The imperial decree has already been issued, appointing you as the King of Hell. There is no need for the introduction anymore." Yang Si woke up in shock, quickly told his family, and then fainted and died.

    502

    俗例有接煞之说，至期，家人从俗行事。有百户胡姓者，晚来临奠，过杨所居巷口，见高灯旗纛中有蟒袍而盛服者，疑为巡城察院，侍立路侧。方谛视间，杨在车中大呼曰：“胡某毋恐，我阴间到任，少一判官，将仗君助我。”胡惊惧，自道：“亲老，不可即死。”杨曰：“我已奏上帝，事无可商。汝亲老，吾亦知之，当令我妹夫张某代汝养母。”言毕不见。

    502

    Translation

    According to custom, there is a practice known as "jie sha" (warding off evil spirits), and on the appointed day, the family follows the custom in their actions. There was a man surnamed Hu, a hundred-household official, who arrived late to pay respects. As he passed by the alley where Yang lived, he saw a tall lamp and flagpole with a person wearing a dragon robe and formal attire, whom he suspected to be the city inspector, standing on the side of the road. While carefully observing, Yang called out from his carriage, "Mr. Hu, do not be afraid. I have arrived in the underworld and need one more judge. I will rely on your help." Hu was startled and said, "My parents are old, I cannot die immediately." Yang replied, "I have already reported to the Heavenly Emperor, and there is no room for discussion. I know your parents are elderly, and I will have my brother-in-law Zhang adopt your mother." After saying this, he disappeared.

    503

    胡奔至家，深悔临奠之行，与其母相对悒悒。有叩门者持银一封曰：“我杨四佐领之妹夫张某也。昨梦阎罗王召去，命以五十金助汝家养膳之费。阎罗所命，不敢有违，故来奉赠，且速驾也。”胡自知将死，出外辞亲友，越三日卒。

    　　○蓝顶妖人扬州商人汪春山，家畜梨园。有苏人朱二官者，色技俱佳，汪使居徐宁门外花园。一日，邻家失火，火及园，朱逃出巷。巷西有二美人倚门立，以手招之，朱遂入。二美自称亦姓汪，春山族妹也。语方浓，一豹裘而蓝顶者来，云是二美之父，年五十许，强朱为婿。朱虽心贪女美，而自诉家贫，无以为聘。蓝顶者云：“无妨，一切费用，我尽任之。”朱欲回苏告父母，蓝顶者云：“汝归苏可也，但吾女贪汝貌而为婚，自知非偶，切勿通知吾侄春山为嘱。”朱买舟，同抵阊门。

    Explanation

    503

    Husband rushed home, deeply regretting his visit to the funeral, and he sat in silence with his mother, both feeling depressed. There was a knock on the door, and a person held a sealed envelope, saying, "I am Zhang, the brother-in-law of Yang Si, the assistant leader. Yesterday, I dreamed that the King of Hell summoned me, and he ordered me to give you fifty taels of silver to help with your family's food expenses. Since it was the King of Hell's command, I dare not disobey, so I come to present this gift, and hurry on your way." Husband knew he was going to die, so he went out to bid farewell to relatives and friends, and three days later, he passed away.

    A blue-hatted demon, a Yangzhou merchant named Wang Chunshan, kept a troupe of actors. There was a man from Suzhou named Zhu Er Guan, who was both handsome and skilled, and Wang made him live in a garden outside the gate of Xu Ning. One day, a fire broke out in the neighbor's house, and the flames reached the garden. Zhu escaped down the alley. On the west side of the alley, there were two beautiful women standing by the door, waving their hands to beckon him in. Zhu followed them inside. The two women introduced themselves, saying they also had the surname Wang and were the cousins of Chunshan. As they spoke, a man dressed in a leopard coat and wearing a blue hat came in, saying he was the father of the two women, about fifty years old, and insisted that Zhu become his son-in-law. Although Zhu was attracted by the women's beauty and admitted his poverty, the blue-hatted man said, "It's not a problem. All the expenses will be covered by me." Zhu wanted to return to Suzhou to inform his parents, but the blue-hatted man said, "You can go back to Suzhou, but my daughter is only marrying you for your looks, knowing it's not a proper match, so please do not tell my nephew Chunshan about this." Zhu bought a boat and traveled together to the gate of Changmen.

    504

    语其父。父故木匠，亦以娶媳无力为辞。蓝顶者助钱二千为婚费，钱皆康熙通宝，朱丝穿。

    　　二官携归，路遇数捕役尾之，曰：“此朱绳穿钱乃某绅宦家压箱钱，汝为盗验矣。”将擒送官。二官告以故。一市之人聚观，以为怪。且曰：“必见蓝顶者才释汝。”二官云：“吾岳翁以钱与我，原约今日为婚，少顷新人花轿至矣，君等伺之。”众以为然。果远远闻鼓乐声，四人皆红半臂舁花轿至。众人哄而往，揭帘，一青面獠牙者坐焉。众大骇，并役亦奔散。二官得脱于祸，急归家，则蓝顶者高坐堂中骂曰：“吾戒汝勿泄，而汝竟告众人，且聚而捕我，何昧良若是？”

    Explanation

    504

    The official told his father. His father was a carpenter and also used the excuse that he could not afford to marry off his daughter. The man with the blue hat gave two thousand coins as a wedding gift, and the coins were all Kangxi Tongbao strung with red silk.

    The official took them home, and on the way met several constables who followed him. They said, "These red-threaded coins are from a certain noble family's savings; you are proven to be a thief." They were about to arrest and take him to the authorities. The official explained the reason. The entire market gathered to watch, thinking it was strange. They said, "You must see the man with the blue hat to be released." The official said, "My father-in-law gave me the money and we agreed to get married today. The bride's sedan chair will arrive soon. You all wait and see." They all waited and saw the sedan chair arrive. The crowd rushed forward, lifted the curtain, and found a green-faced, fanged person sitting there. The crowd was greatly startled, and the constables also ran away. The official escaped the trouble and hurried home. The man with the blue hat was sitting high on the dais, scolding him. He said, "I warned you not to tell anyone, but you told everyone and gathered to capture me. How could you be so unscrupulous?"

    505

    呼杖杖之，二女为哀求免。成婚匝月，偕还扬州。

    　　又岁余，二女置酒谓二官曰：“缘尽矣，请郎还乡。”二官不肯，泣，二女亦泣。如是者数日，蓝顶者忽来驱逼其女，二官攀衣不放。蓝顶者怒，以手撮二官向空中掷之，冥然坠地，及醒，已在虎丘后山。

    　　○蒙化太守无锡曹五辑为云南蒙化太守，其子某，庚午举人，江苏巡抚庄滋圃之门生。

    　　乾隆二十一年，无锡大疫，华剑光之子某素好行善，出古画数幅，托孝廉售之，嘱曰：“得八百金，为本邑埋葬死人之费。”曹带往苏州，以画呈庄公。庄念曹本义举，画亦佳，竟与八百金。曹归，以八十金付华曰：“价只此。”华无奈何，勉力补凑，得数棺，为瘗其暴骨者，余棺犹有待也。

    Explanation

    505

    The official struck the man with a stick, and the two women pleaded for mercy. After a month of marriage, they returned together to Yangzhou.

    After another year, the two women prepared a feast and told the two officials, "Our fate has ended; please return home, sir." The two officials refused, and they wept. The two women also wept. For several days, the man in the blue hat suddenly came to drive them away, and the two officials clung to his clothes, refusing to let go. The man in the blue hat became angry and picked up the two officials with his hands, throwing them into the air. They fell to the ground unconscious and, when they woke up, were already at the back of Tiger Hill.

    ○ Cao Wujie, the governor of Menghua in Yunnan, was from Wuxi. His son, who passed the imperial examination in the year Gengwu, was a student of Governor Zhuang Zhipu of Jiangsu.

    In the twenty-first year of Emperor Qianlong's reign, a major epidemic broke out in Wuxi. Hua Jian Guang's son, who was known for his charitable deeds, brought out several ancient paintings and entrusted a scholar-official to sell them. He instructed, "If you get eight hundred taels of silver, use it to bury the dead in our town." Cao took the paintings to Suzhou and presented them to Governor Zhuang. Governor Zhuang thought Cao's act was noble, and the paintings were also excellent, so he gave him eight hundred taels of silver. Cao returned to Wuxi and gave eighty taels of silver to Hua, saying, "The price is only this much." Hua had no choice but to make an effort to gather the money, obtaining several coffins to bury the remains of the dead. There were still more coffins to be prepared.

    506

    未几，孝廉病卒。太守哀悼不已，焚牒于东岳神，自称：“居官清正，子无罪，不宜得此报。”归而假寐，见青衣人持东岳神帖请往。至大殿外，神迎于阶下曰：“公见责良是，但尔子近为不肖之行，屯人之膏，令千百人骨暴原野。公不信，可归至尔子书斋启笥视之。”言毕，命人拥一囚至，枷锁锒铛，即其子也，太守抱之哭。惊醒，急往其子书斋启笥，尚余七百余金。询其仆，方知鬻画匿价之事，其子媳亦未知也。太守自此哀子之思为之少衰。

    　　○店主还债甘泉县役邹姓者，月夜过西门大街。夜已三鼓，路无行人，邹见槐树下小屋门开，一女倚门立。邹伪吃烟取火者就之，女勿避。邹喜，携女入屋，坐凳上密谈，约以次日复往。明早伺之，槐树下并无居人，一厝棺小屋也。从窗外窥，条凳宛然，凳上灰痕有两人并坐形迹，心知鬼迷，意忽忽不乐。

    Explanation

    506

    Not long after, the filial and upright official died of illness. The governor, deeply grieved, burned a petition at the Eastern Mountain God and declared, "I held office with integrity, and my son is innocent. He should not receive such a retribution." Upon returning, he took a brief nap and saw a person in green clothes holding a message from the Eastern Mountain God inviting him to go. He arrived at the temple's outer courtyard, and the deity greeted him at the steps, saying, "You are right to be blamed. But your son has recently engaged in unworthy actions, wasting people's resources, causing the bones of thousands to be exposed in the fields. If you do not believe, you can return to your son's study and open the chest to see." After speaking, the deity ordered someone to lead a prisoner to, who was shackled and chained. It was his son, and the governor embraced him and wept. He awoke, rushed to his son's study, and opened the chest to find over seven hundred gold coins still inside. He asked his servant and learned of the matter of selling paintings and concealing the price, which his son's wife also did not know. From then on, the governor's grief for his son's thoughts slightly diminished.

    ○ A County Official Named Zou, who worked in Ganquan, passed through the western gate of the city on a moonlit night. It was already the third drumbeat of the night, and there were no pedestrians on the road. Zou saw a small house with its door open beneath a willow tree, and a woman leaning against the door. Zou pretended to light his pipe and approached her, and the woman did not avoid him. Zou was pleased and took the woman into the house, where they sat on a bench and had a secret conversation, agreeing to meet again the next day. The next morning, when he waited, there was no one living under the willow tree; it was only a small house containing a coffin. Peering through the window, he saw the bench still there, with ash marks showing where two people had sat together. He realized he had been bewitched by a ghost and felt a sudden sense of unease.

    507

    一日早起，谓其妻曰：“有人欠我银七两二钱，我将往索。”已而不反。次日，闻街前轰轰云：“某茶馆有人饮茶暴卒，馆主人报官，验无他故，饬店主人买棺殓之，招尸亲识认。”妻闻往视，果其夫也。问主人棺价，适符七两二钱之数。

    　　○许氏女报奶娘仇杭州许某，业盐，家生女才四十日，忽遍身红肿而死。五日后，附魂于小婢，口称：“我为你家女儿，命不该死。实因奶娘不好，自家贪睡，将我放在大厅阶檐下，全不照管，被左邻开丧人家煞神走过，触犯而死。我今要向奶娘讨命。”

    　　许氏爷娘闻之悲泣，告以“奶娘乃海宁人，自汝死后，彼已去矣，从何处往报耶？”

    　　女云：“取身契看，便知住处。”如其言，乃注视良久曰：“勿劳爷娘，我自会往报，但烧纸船一只与我。”许家烧与之，婢蹶然起矣。嗣后奶娘存亡，许亦不复往问。

    Explanation

    507

    One morning, upon waking, he told his wife, "Someone owes me seven taels and two mace of silver. I am going to collect it." Afterward, he did not return. The next day, he heard a commotion in the street: "A person died suddenly after drinking tea at a certain teahouse. The owner of the teahouse reported it to the authorities, who, upon examination, found no other cause, and ordered the teahouse owner to buy a coffin to bury him, summoning the deceased's family for identification." His wife, upon hearing this, went to see and found that it was indeed her husband. She asked the owner about the price of the coffin, and it just happened to match the amount of seven taels and two mace.

    ○ Revenge of the Xu Family's Daughter Against Her Wet Nurse

    In Hangzhou, there was a Xu family engaged in the salt trade. They had a daughter who was only forty days old when she suddenly died with her entire body swollen and red. Five days later, her spirit attached itself to a young servant girl, who spoke aloud: "I am your daughter. My fate was not to die. Actually, because the wet nurse was not good and was herself sleeping soundly, she left me lying under the eaves of the main hall without any care or attention. When the funeral procession of the neighbor on the left passed by, the evil spirits came, offended me, and I died. Now I want to take revenge on the wet nurse."

    The Xu parents heard this and wept, telling her, "The wet nurse is from Hai Ning. Since your death, she has already left. Where can we go to seek revenge?"

    The girl said, "Look at the contract, and you will know where she lives." As she said, they looked at it for a long time and said, "Don't trouble your parents, I will go and seek revenge myself. Just burn a paper boat for me." The Xu family burned one for her, and the servant girl suddenly stood up. Afterward, whether the wet nurse lived or died, the Xu family no longer inquired.

    508

    ○蛊云南人家家畜蛊，蛊能粪金银，以获利。每晚即放蛊出，火光如电，东西散流。聚众噪之，可令堕地，或蛇，或虾蟆，类亦不一。人家争藏小儿，虑为所食。

    　　养蛊者别为密室，命妇人喂之，一见男子便败，盖纯阴所聚也。食男子者粪金，食女子者粪银。此云南总兵华封为予言之。

    　　○鸩人取香火杭州道士廖明，募钱立圣帝庙塑像。开光之日，乡城男妇蜂集拈香。忽一无赖来，昂然坐圣帝旁，指像侮慢之。众人苦禁，道士曰：“不必，听其所为，当必有报。”须臾，无赖仆地，呼腹痛，盘滚不已，遂死，七窍血流。众大骇，以为圣帝威灵，香火大盛，道士以之致富。逾年，其党分财不匀出首：“去年无赖之慢神，乃道士贿之，教其如此。其死，乃道士先以毒酒饮之，而无赖不知也。”

    Explanation

    508

    Translation

    The Cultivation of the蛊 in Yunnan

    In Yunnan, households cultivate the蛊, which can produce gold and silver, thereby generating profit. Every night, the蛊 is released, emitting a glow like lightning, spreading east and west. By gathering a crowd and shouting at it, one can make the蛊 fall to the ground, sometimes as a snake, sometimes as a toad, and there are many other forms as well. People vie to hide their children, fearing they might be eaten by the蛊.

    Those who cultivate the蛊 keep it in a separate, secluded room, and order women to feed it. If a man is seen, the蛊 immediately perishes, as it is a product of pure yin energy. The蛊 that feeds on men produces gold, and the one that feeds on women produces silver. This is what the general of Yunnan, Huafeng, told me.

    The Poisoned Ruffian in Hangzhou

    In Hangzhou, the Taoist priest Liao Ming raised funds to build a temple dedicated to the圣帝 and sculpted an image. On the day of the consecration, people from the countryside and the city gathered to offer incense. Suddenly, a ruffian came and sat arrogantly beside the statue of the圣帝, pointing at it and showing disrespect. The crowd tried to restrain him, but the Taoist priest said, "There's no need; let him do as he pleases, and he will surely face retribution." Soon after, the ruffian fell to the ground, crying out in abdominal pain, rolling around uncontrollably, and eventually died, with blood flowing from all seven orifices. The crowd was greatly shocked, believing in the威灵 of the圣帝, and the worship of the temple flourished. The Taoist priest became wealthy through this. After a year, his associates reported that the ruffian's disrespect to the deity was actually orchestrated by the Taoist priest, who bribed him to act in such a manner. The ruffian's death was due to the Taoist priest having poisoned him with wine beforehand, though the ruffian was unaware of this.

    509

    有司掘验其骨，果青黑色，遂诛道士，而圣帝香火亦衰。

    　　○科场二则江西周学士力堂，癸卯乡试，题是“学而优则仕”一节，文思幽奥，房考张某不能句读，怒而批抹之，置孙山外。晚间，各房考归寝，张忽呓语不止，自披其颊曰：“如此佳文，而汝不知，尚忝然作房考乎！”自骂自击不止。家人以为中风，急请众房考来。检视之，得所抹周卷，读之，俱不甚解，乃曰：“试荐之何如？”大主考为礼部侍郎任公兰枝，阅而惊曰：“此奇文，通场所无，可以冠多士也！”会副主考德公阅文倦，假寐几上，伺其醒，告之。德公问：“何字号？”

    　　曰：“男字第三号。”德曰：“不必阅文，竟定解元可也。”任问故，曰：“我寝方酣，忽见金见甲神向我贺曰：”汝第三儿子中解元矣。‘今得’男字三号‘之卷，岂非其验耶！“言毕阅文，亦大加叹赏，遂定此科第一。榜填后，众问本房某梦中呓语之故，茫然不知。周后为福建巡抚，总督南河。

    Explanation

    509

    Officials dug up and examined the bones, which were indeed dark blue or black, and they executed the Taoist priest; consequently, the worship of the sacred emperor also declined.

    ○ Two Tales from the Examination Hall

    In the provincial examination of the year of the Water Rabbit (癸卯), the topic was a section from "To study and excel, then enter public service" (学而优则仕). The essay was deeply profound and obscure, and the examiner Ouyang could not parse it. He became angry and scribbled over it, placing the candidate outside the ranks of success. Later that evening, as the other examiners retired to bed, Ouyang suddenly began talking in his sleep without stopping. He slapped his own cheek and said, "Such a fine essay, and you do not understand it! How can you still shamefully serve as an examiner!" He continued to scold himself and hit himself. His family thought he was having a stroke and quickly called the other examiners. Upon inspection, they found the essay that Ouyang had rejected, and upon reading it, they could not fully understand it. They then said, "What if we recommend him?" The chief examiner was the deputy minister of the Ministry of Rites, Mr. Ren Lan-zhi. He read the essay and was amazed, saying, "This is a remarkable essay, unmatched throughout the examination hall, and it can be placed at the top of all candidates!" Coincidentally, the deputy chief examiner, Mr. De, was tired from reading essays and took a nap on the table. When he woke up, he was informed. Mr. De asked, "What is the examination number?"

    They replied, "Third male candidate."

    Mr. De said, "There is no need to read the essay; it can be directly declared as the top scorer." Mr. Ren asked for the reason, and they explained, "I was just dozing off when I suddenly saw a golden and armored god come to congratulate me, saying, 'Your third son has become the top scorer.' Now, we have found the essay with the number 'Third male candidate.' Could this not be a sign?" After finishing, they read the essay and were also greatly impressed, so they decided to declare it the top scorer of this examination. After the results were posted, the other examiners asked about the reason for Ouyang's dream talk, but he could not explain it. Zhou later became the governor of Fujian and the governor-general of the southern river region.

    510

    雍正丙午，江南乡试，其时骋各近省甲科司分校事，少年英俊。有张垒者，科分既久，自居前辈，性尤迂滞，每晚必焚香祝天曰：“垒衰年学荒，虑不称阅文之任，恐试卷中有佳文及其祖宗有阴德者，求神明暗中提撕。”众房考笑其痴，相与戏弄之：折一细竿，伺其灯下阅卷有所弃掷，则于窗纸外穿入挑其冠。如是者三。张大惊，以为鬼神果相诏也，即具衣冠向空拜，又祝曰：“某卷文实不佳，而神明提我，想必有阴德之故。如果然者，求神明再如前指示我。”众房考愈笑之，俟其将弃此卷，复挑以竿。张不复再阅，直捧此卷上堂，而两主司已就寝矣，乃扣门求见，告以深夜神明提醒之故。大主考沈公近思阅其卷曰：“此文甚佳，取中有馀，君何必神道设教耶？”众房考噤口不敢言。及榜发，见此卷已在榜中，各哗然，笑告张曰：“我辈弄君。”张正色曰：“此非我为君等所弄，乃君等为鬼神所弄耳。”众亦折服。

    Explanation

    510

    In the year of Bingwu during the reign of Emperor Yongzheng, the provincial-level imperial examination was held in the southern region of China. Zhang Lei, a senior official responsible for grading the exams, was particularly slow-witted and conservative. Every night, he would burn incense and pray to heaven, saying, "I am an old man with a poor grasp of learning, and I fear I may not be fit for the task of grading the exams. I worry that there might be excellent articles or candidates with ancestral virtue, so I ask the gods to subtly guide me." The other examiners laughed at his foolishness and decided to play a trick on him. They used a thin stick to poke his hat from outside the window three times. Zhang was greatly shocked, thinking that the gods were indeed guiding him, and he immediately dressed formally and bowed towards the sky, saying, "This article is not very good, but the gods have guided me.
